Yuanjiang Lin** - **Huán Lóng** (Housing Dragons) - **Yǎnzhōu Yù Miào** (Temple of the Mountain God in Yǎnzhōu) - **Wáng Quán** - **Guǐ Bīng** (Army of Ghosts) - **Shǒu Yín Quǎn** (Guardian Silver Dog) - **Cūn Mín Yùu Tǔdì** (Villager Meets the Local Land God) - **Zhú Pǔ Yìwù** (Strange Things in a Bamboo Garden) - **Kōngtóng Shānrén** (Hermit of the Kunlun Mountains) - **Zhāng Qiū Bó Jìn** (Burning of Silk in Zhāng Qiū) - **Wáng Gēng** - **Língzhī** (Ginseng) - **Mǎ Guǐ** (Horse Ghost) - **Sān Zú Biē** (Three-Footed Turtle) **○ Divine Auspice** The ancestor of Renzong (Zhu Yuanzhang) originally lived in Sui, later moving to Huaibei. His residence was close to a monastery, and he had a very close relationship with the abbot, who admired Renzong's sincerity. One night, he saw a fire in his house lighting up the sky and lamented, saying, "Mr. Zhu is a good man, how could heaven bring disaster upon him!" After a month, Renzong returned, and the abbot condoled him. Renzong replied, "Tonight, my wife gave birth to a boy, and I feared there might be some impurity, so I have not come to the Buddhist ground for a long time; it is not a disaster from the fire god." The boy is the High Emperor. **○ Imperial Academy** It is said that during the reign of the High Emperor, the Imperial Academy was first established. The emperor visited it and was very pleased with the grandeur and beauty of the academy. As he walked to the Guangye Hall, he happened to say, "A fortunate young man should be able to live here." Since then, for over 140 years, students who have lived in this hall have often achieved top ranks, rising to high positions and great wealth, which others could not match. Additionally, there are no spiders in any of the halls. It is said that when the academy was built, the emperor saw a spider weaving a web in a corner of the house and said, "I just built this house, and you dare to occupy it?" He scolded the spider and it fled. From then on, spiders were completely gone.

**The Examination Hall** The Nanjing Examination Hall was originally the residence of Jin Yi Commandant Ji Gang. Ji Gang was favored by the Hongwu Emperor during the Ming Dynasty, but later he was punished for illegal acts and executed, with his entire family being killed there. Some say they were buried alive there. To this day, every time the provincial examinations are held, when the candidates enter the hall, there is always a sound rising from the ground, passing over all the examination rooms, like the galloping of ten thousand horses, so it is said. **Ping Bao'er** General Ping An, also known as Ping Bao'er, served as a general at the end of the Jianwen era. He defeated the northern army at Xiao River. Ping An pursued the Prince of Yan on horseback alone, was about to catch up with him with his spear, when suddenly there was a black dragon in the sky, extended its claws and grabbed his arm, causing his horse to kneel on the ground. Ping An knew that fate was in play, sighed, gathered his troops, and stopped. Later, his army was defeated and he was captured. When he met the King, the King asked him, "What if I had caught up with you?" Ping An replied, "I wanted to capture you alive to serve as your attendant. At that time, the country followed the old customs of the Yuan dynasty, and all the princes called their attendants 'shizhang'. Otherwise, it would be hard to predict." The King rewarded and pardoned him, ordering him to take charge of the Beiping Commandery. Later, due to suspicion of his old connections, he was executed.

Yuan Taichang Góng's skill in physiognomy was renowned throughout the previous dynasty. At the beginning of the Hongwu era, Yáo Shàoshī Guǎngxiào, a highly respected monk, resided at Songshan Temple. Upon first meeting Góng, he immediately recognized him as a person capable of assisting and supporting, saying, "You are comparable to Liu Bingzhong." Later, Yáo was selected as a high-ranking monk and brought into the Prince of Yan's residence, where he participated in secret planning. He spoke of Góng to the prince, who had also heard of him before. The prince then claimed that Góng was listed in the military register and sent a flag to summon him. After Góng arrived, he did not immediately meet the prince. Instead, the prince secretly ordered the selection of nine men from the guard who were tall, had beards, and resembled the prince in appearance. The prince mixed among them, all dressed in plain clothes and went to the market. They pulled Góng into a tavern for a drink, with the prince seated third. Góng suddenly approached and pulled at the prince's sleeve, bowing and calling him "Your Highness." The others laughed at his absurdity, but Góng spoke more insistently. The prince then stood up, returned to the palace, and summoned Góng to meet him. Góng said, "Your Highness summoned me from afar, yet we met in a tavern?" When asked how he recognized him, Góng replied, "When Your Highness entered the tavern, I saw him holding a bow and arrow. I recognized him by sight; his skin was wrinkled and thin, and his hand was like a dragon's, without flesh."

The world-famous story of "distinguishing the prime minister at a Buddhist temple on Mount Song and recognizing the true king at a tavern in Chang'an" refers to this event. Gong was then asked to examine the king closely, and he greatly praised the grandeur of his appearance, saying, "At the age of forty, he will have a beard that extends beyond his navel, and he will ascend to the throne, becoming a peaceful emperor for twenty years." The king was pleased, and thus he decided on a major plan. Gong stayed in Yan City for a short time, where he met officers and soldiers, promising many of them the titles of marquis, viscount, or high-ranking official. These promises often spread far and wide. Fearing other potential problems, the king sent Gong back to his hometown. Later, when the king crossed the river and ascended to the throne, Gong was summoned by imperial edict to the capital and appointed as the Grand Master of Rites, receiving generous gifts. When the matter of selecting a crown prince came up, the emperor had a particular preference, hesitating for many years. He sent Gong to assess Emperor Renzong, saying, "He will be the future emperor." Gong also assessed Emperor Xuanzong, saying, "He will be the emperor for a thousand years." Thus, the foundation of the state was finally established. One day, Gong met Emperor Renzong in the East Palace, where the emperor asked, "How long will my life be?" Gong replied, "Beyond the age of forty-nine, your blessings will be boundless." When the year Bihai (乙巳) came, it turned out exactly as predicted. Gong's son, Zhongche, also inherited his skill and reached the rank of Grand Master of the Imperial Seal.

Omens of Passing the Imperial Examination** My uncle, who served in the Ministry of Works, had failed repeatedly in the local exams but was admitted to the Imperial Academy in Nanjing through the imperial recommendation system. During the provincial exam of the Wuxu year of the Hongzhi era, a junior student from Huizhou named Wang had a dream in which he was fiercely arguing with a Suzhou scholar named Lu. They complained to the chief examiner, who dismissed them, saying, "Lu is not from here." Upon waking, Wang could not understand the meaning of the dream, as he and my uncle had never met before. Later, Wang told a Suzhou scholar about it, and the scholar came to inform my uncle. After the results were announced, my uncle was selected, while Wang did not make the list. When my uncle went to the capital for the metropolitan exam on New Year's Day, he visited the late Wu Wending, who was then serving as an assistant in the Ministry of Personnel. Coincidentally, Mao Qingshu from Kunshan and Zhu Maizhong and Zhou Xizhong, both scholars who held the rank of Xiu Zuan, were also present. The three officials were all top scholars from my hometown. Soon after, Zhou Wen, the top scholar from Yunnan, also paid a visit. They introduced themselves and exchanged names. After a while, they left, and my uncle walked with Zhou. Zhou looked gloomy and asked, "Have your ancestors done any good deeds?" My uncle nodded but did not answer.

Zhou said, "These words of mine carry meaning. In the year of Bingchen, I once dreamed of arriving at the palace courtyard, where the names of the Jinshi (imperial scholars) were being announced. I stood at the foot of the courtyard, thinking I would be among them. Suddenly, an object fell from the sky. Upon seeing it, it was a golden palace flower. I wanted to take it and wear it. Someone nearby stopped me and said, 'You cannot take it; this is the property of the descendants of Lu Xuan Gong from Suzhou. Their family has accumulated good deeds, and they should receive this.' Turning around, I saw that it had already been placed on the head of someone else. Since then, I have been looking for this person. Now I have met you, who are a Lu from Suzhou, and you are the first to meet the top three in the palace examinations this year, and you are all from Suzhou. This is likely a sign of your success. Shall I never succeed?' My uncle nodded in agreement. At night, returning to the inn, I told my attendant about it, and I was surprised by the words of Xuan Gong. The old servant Xu Cong suddenly replied, 'Have you forgotten? Our old master's name was not "Xuan"?' It was my great-grandfather's name, which had the same pronunciation as 'Xuan' but added a 'wang' on the side. My uncle then understood and was surprised. Soon after, he was indeed selected as the top candidate. Zhou, however, never passed and obtained an official position through selection and appointment. [My uncle, when he took the Jinshi examination, dreamed of riding in a sedan chair to a Buddhist temple, and he also dreamed of drafting a legal document. Later, he obtained the position of Observing Affairs at the Imperial Court of Justice.] Recorded in the year Xinwei.

**The Bone-Reading Monk** During the reign of Emperor Yingzong, there was a monk at the Half-Tank Temple in Tiger Hill who was blind in both eyes but skilled in bone-reading. He often predicted the fortunes and misfortunes of people with remarkable accuracy. When Càn’s great-grandfather, Hu Gong, was a child, his family took him to the monk for a bone reading. The monk said, “This child will one day hold a high official position, so take good care of him.” Later, Hu Gong passed the imperial examinations and rose through the ranks to become a Censorial Councillor of Shanxi Province, ultimately retiring as a Fourth-Rank official. In the same village, there was a man named Shi Yi, who was poor and worked as a hired weaver. He brought his two sons to the monk for a reading. The monk was pleased and asked for a generous reward, saying, “These two boys have the bones of wealthy men.” He advised Shi Yi, “Do not underestimate your sons.” At first, those who heard the prediction did not believe it, but as the boys grew older, they indeed became wealthy and influential in the village. Another man, Gong Dà, came from a relatively prosperous family. He was stout and had a black mole with a long hair several inches long on his navel. He once remarked that all his good fortune was concentrated in that mole. Gong was known for his hearty laughter and often laughed loudly when speaking to others. One day, while at the monk’s temple, he was still laughing when the monk looked at him and said, “Do not laugh! Next year, the hair on your navel will fall out, and you will die!” Gong was displeased with the monk’s words and did not respond. Later, when he was bathing and rubbing his navel, the hair suddenly fell out. A few days later, he fell ill and died.

○ The Qilin of Yuan River In the autumn of the seventh year of the Chenghua era, a female cow belonging to Feng Gui, a villager from Dongtian Village in Yuan County, Changde, gave birth to a qilin on its own. At the time of birth, clouds and mist gathered thickly, and a red light filled the room. Its form was roughly like that of a deer, its hooves and tail resembling those of a cow. All over its body were scales or patterns like those of a qilin, and on its forehead was a sharp, firm bone that slightly rose from the flesh—this was its horn. As soon as it emerged from the womb, it leaped about, and no one could restrain it. The common people, being ignorant, considered it a monster, and struck it, wounding it on the right side, after which it died. Later, someone recognized it as a qilin, and the prefect Yang Xuan ordered its dried remains to be taken and stored in the treasury. When my maternal grandfather, the Canshi, was serving as the prefect of the county, he often had the remains brought for viewing. After many years, the bones and flesh had decomposed, leaving only the empty skin, and occasionally the scales (麟) would fall off. My aunts and the servants and concubines all saw it.

A Man Who Raised a Dragon Ji Zong Dao Weizhong was from Luo Chi in Liuzhou. He claimed that his great-great-grandfather, during the reign of Emperor Hongwu, served as a local inspector and lived in a mountain area known as Ji Family Village. Once, while out, he found a huge egg the size of a winnowing basket. He brought it home and had a hen incubate it. After over a hundred days, the egg hatched, producing a creature that resembled a snake but had four legs, was blue-green in color, and had scales and armor covering its body. The creature was no longer than a foot long and moved by lifting its head and raising its body, almost like a beast running. The family fed it with rice water. After several months, the creature grew larger and developed a preference for raw blood. Whenever they slaughtered sheep and pigs, they would give it blood to drink. Over time, the creature became more tame and affectionate. Whenever Ji Zong Dao moved around, the creature followed him, and when he called it out, it would coil up beside his bed. When touched, it felt cold as ice and iron. The family named it "Little Dragon," and news of it spread outside, attracting many visitors who wanted to see it. Ji Zong Dao thought, "This will lead to gossip and bring trouble to our family." So, he released the creature into a stream in the courtyard behind the office, instructing it, "Do not worry about hunger; I will send someone every day to feed you. From now on, you must not come out again."

Upon returning home, the snake had already followed him, and Ji shouted at it, saying, "You did not obey my commands, I will kill you!" The snake seemed to understand human intentions, bowing its head and lying flat, then immediately returned to the pool. From then on, it would often place blood at the pool's mouth, springing up to eat whenever it was placed there. After many years, it grew to over ten feet long. One day, a colleague's wife went to the pool to draw water. The woman was dressed in red, and the snake, seeing her, mistook the color for blood, coiled around her and bit her to death. The colleague was furious and declared, "You are a monster that harms people, I will report this to the higher authorities, and I will destroy your family." Ji was afraid, begged for mercy, and offered a generous gift, and thus the conflict was resolved. Thereafter, he hated the snake and wanted to kill it. He took a knife to the pool, still placed blood to lure it out, and then threw the knife and struck it. The snake was startled and hid, only managing to cut off part of its tail. From then on, it no longer appeared, and no one knew whether it was alive or dead. Several years later, one night, a violent wind and thunderstorm arose, and a blue dragon emerged from the pool amidst the clouds and mist, hundreds of feet long, rose into the sky and flew away. The family all pointed and said it was a transformation of the small dragon. Only Zhong, my uncle's contemporary and fellow jinshi (imperial scholar), remains.

The Temple of Mount Tai in Yanzhou Prefecture The Temple of Mount Tai in Yanzhou Prefecture has long been renowned for its miraculous phenomena. During the Hongzhi period, Gong Yuanzhi, the governor of Suzhou, once heard the sound of whips and beatings in the middle of the night. He asked those around him, and those who knew about it spoke of the temple's divine wonders, but Gong Yuanzhi did not believe them. At dawn, he went to visit the temple, but saw nothing, and summoned the person who had spoken to reprimand him. The person said, "Only if you are sincere can you gain access." The next day, after bathing and changing clothes, he went at night, prayed for a long time, and the temple gates opened for him to enter. He saw five figures dressed in crowns and robes like kings, who came out to greet him and invited him to sit in the guest seat. Gong Yuanzhi declined, and the king said, "You are an official of the sun, and I am an official of the moon; in matters of duty, we have no authority over each other, so please sit." Soon after, tea was served, and Gong Yuanzhi hesitated to drink it. The deity said, "This is tea from a ceremonial banquet; it is harmless to drink." Gong Yuanzhi then asked, "I have heard of the Ten Kings; where are the other five?" The deity replied, "They have already gone to the ceremonial banquet." He then asked to see the prison, and the deity said, "The prison is strictly guarded, and I cannot let you in, but there is one thing I must show you." The deity ordered a monk to be brought, and heated coals were used to roast his back, saying, "He is a monk from a certain temple in this area. His daily alms were used for food and drink, not for repairing the temple buildings, so he is punished in this way." Gong Yuanzhi asked, "Is there still a way to be spared?" The deity replied, "If he repents, he can be spared." He then bid farewell and left. After returning, he sent someone to secretly investigate, and found that the monk was suffering from a back abscess and was about to die. When the monk was told what he had seen, he repented in fear, spent all his money to repair the temple, and his illness was immediately cured.

### Wáng Quán Jiading Lou Tang Town resident Wáng Quán was wealthy. He had a large, robust body and a voracious appetite, eating and drinking as much as several people. He walked with a slow, unsteady gait, unable to walk quickly. Every time he bathed, he would have someone cover him with a cloth, and his wife, children, maidservants, and servants were not allowed to be near, and he forbade them from opening the cloth without permission. One day, while bathing, there was no sound of water for a long time, which surprised the family. They lifted the cloth to look, and they saw a toad as big as a basket, lying inside. Surprised, they covered him again. Later, Wáng Quán emerged, feeling dazed and as if something was lost, and he died that night. [The two stories above were told by my aunt Xu.] ### Ghost Soldiers Lu Róng lived outside the Lou Gate in Wu. In the spring of the year 1506 (丙寅年) during the Zhengde era of the Ming dynasty, one evening near dusk, Lu Róng stood alone by the door and heard a loud noise coming from the other side of the river, as if it were the sound of weapons and armor. Soon after, thousands of people appeared, but only from the waist down could they be seen; above the waist, they could not be seen. They were all decorated with colorful silk and bows, and they moved very quickly. Lu Róng was greatly startled and shouted. His family, including men, women, and children, all came out, and they all saw them. After some time, they passed by completely. That year, the pirate Niudongshan of Chongming caused chaos, and an imperial decree was issued to mobilize the capital troops and various guard troops to suppress them. The military campaign lasted for over a year and was finally disbanded, and the state treasury was depleted. What Lu Róng saw was likely a military omen.

The Guardian Dog of Silver My father recounted: In a household in the Chuan Gate, whose name I have forgotten, the family made a living from trade. They kept a dog, which was very strong, and it would lie beside a cage all day, never leaving for a moment. Whenever someone came to its place, it would bite them. The family warned each other not to approach it. One day, a merchant arrived at the door, unaware of the dog, and approached it. The dog bit his thigh, causing blood to flow. The merchant shouted and cursed at the owner, who also disliked the dog, and apologized, saying, "Please don't be angry, I will cook it and eat it tomorrow." The merchant returned to his inn that night and had a dream in which someone told him, "I am the father of the owner, who has been dead for several years. I buried hundreds of taels of silver beneath the cage, but I did not have the chance to tell my son about it before I died. I remembered it so clearly that I was reborn as a dog, and that is why I stayed there day and night, waiting for someone to discover the silver. I did not expect to mistakenly bite you. Now, your son wants to cook me, and I want to tell him the reason, but he will not believe me. Please go to see him and tell him not to kill me." The merchant was startled awake and immediately went to the house, knocking on the door. The owner came out to greet him, and the merchant asked, "Where is the dog?" The owner replied that it had already been cooked and was almost done. The merchant was filled with regret and told the owner about his dream. The owner still did not believe him. The merchant then asked to check, and they removed the cage, finding a clay vessel containing over four hundred taels of silver. The owner deeply regretted his actions and mourned the dog, burying it.

My father again said: In a small village, there was a villager named Yu, who once went out and met an old man who called himself the land god. The old man called out Yu's name and said, "You will die. I have come especially to inform you." Yu replied, "I am still in my prime, without any illness, why should I die?" He walked away without looking back, and the old man suddenly disappeared. After several days, when he went out again, he encountered him once more, and the old man said to him, "You will certainly die." Yu asked, "What will happen if I die?" The old man replied, "You will die by drowning." Yu refused it with strong words, but he was very afraid. Not long after, there was someone in a neighboring village with the same name as him, who died by drowning due to some other matter, but Yu was unharmed. Could it be that the gods and spirits also make mistakes, or were they just playing a joke?

Strange Creature in the Bamboo Garden** A friend of mine, whose wife's family name is Zhu, resides near Sha Lake. Several years ago, in the bamboo grove behind his house, something resembling a human suddenly appeared. It had a partially formed body, with a head that looked like it was wearing a straw hat. When it was cut, there was a slight amount of blood, but it was unknown what kind of monster it was. **The Recluse of Mount Kongtong** Lu Chuan, a man from Sichuan, passed the local examination at the beginning of the Hongzhi era and graduated from the Imperial Academy. He was of noble character but poor. He befriended Cheng Gongshi, a scholar from my hometown. There was a Taoist whose origins were unknown; he introduced himself as surnamed Da and nicknamed himself the Recluse of Mount Kongtong. He lived in the same lodging as Lu Chuan and had a particularly close relationship with him. The Taoist was tall and slender, with a handsome and distinguished appearance, but he was dissolute and fond of drinking. He sang in the market every day and returned home at dusk with his pockets full of money, giving it all to Lu Chuan. Lu Chuan relied on him for support and spent over a year with him. One day, the Taoist wanted to leave and return to the mountains. Lu Chuan came to tell Cheng about it, and they prepared a feast to send him off. At that time, Lu Chuan was suffering from boils all over his body, which had not improved for a long time. He asked the Taoist to treat him, and the Taoist said, "It's easy." He took out a small amount of medicine, mixed it with wine, and gave it to Lu Chuan to drink. He heated the kang to extreme heat, made Lu Chuan lie on it, covered him with a thick blanket, and placed the small gourd he wore at the corner.

Chuanru was crushed and could not rise, breaking out in a sweat that soaked his robe completely. The Taoist priest slowly lifted the robe and called him to rise. As he rose, all the sores fell off, and his skin became as clear as jade. The Taoist priest looked at Chuanru and said, "Just parted, truly very lonely in a foreign place, and also worried about your poverty, unable to sustain yourself. I have a pill that can turn copper into silver, now I will share it with you. One day, try it out, perhaps it can suffice for several months. He poured out a small amount of medicine, a ladleful, and gave it to Chuanru. After the wine was finished, he left. Shortly afterward, Chuanru found himself short of funds, and Cheng asked to take out the pill to test it. They found a copper ladle weighing four ounces, melted it over a burning fire, and threw the pill into it. A short while later, five-colored flames rose, with a clanging sound, and it had already become pure silver, not losing a single bit. They looked at each other in amazement. Cheng asked for a small portion, and has kept it ever since. ○ Zhangqiu Silk Burn During the reign of Hongzhi in the year Renzi, the Yellow River broke through the sandbank at Zhangqiu. The emperor ordered the Right Deputy Censor of the Ministry of Justice, Mr. Liu Daxia of Huacr, to go and manage the situation, and appointed the eunuch Li Xing and the Pingjiang Prince Chen Rui to oversee the work. During the ritual of burning silk to worship the gods, the silk ashes formed a complete figure, with a face, hands, and feet. Suddenly, the figure rose from the smoke and disappeared into the air. At that time, Li Xing and Chen Rui were causing much trouble for the people. The Deputy Censor of Shandong, Yang Maoyuan, submitted a memorial criticizing them, which also mentioned this incident. Yang was subsequently demoted. [This was told by my father-in-law, Mr. Sheng.]

A Student Named Wang Xing Wang Xing, a student from Changzhou, took the provincial examination in the early years of the Jiyao period during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Ming Dynasty. At the time, an educational official entrusted a close relative to sell an examination pass in Suzhou, but there were no takers, so he immediately wanted to sell it cheaply. A fellow student named Xi Xuan came to recruit Wang Xing to help him, and the matter was almost completed. One night, Wang Xing dreamed that he passed the provincial examination as the 67th candidate. Just as he passed the exam, his father died, followed by his wife, then his wife's father, and soon after, he himself died. Upon waking, he found it strange and went to see Xuan, keeping the dream secret. He excused himself by saying he was young and not well-versed, did not want to gain fame suddenly, and refused to accept. Xuan became angry, rebuking him for valuing profit over reputation, saying, "I will do it myself, and it will cost only a few dozen gold coins." Soon enough, he indeed passed the exam, and the rank matched his dream exactly. Wang Xing thought it strange, and soon after, his father and his wife's father both died, which made him find it even more strange. Not long after, Xuan eventually died. Wang Xing then told others about his dream, saying, "If I had done it then, I would already be in the Book of the Dead." The fact that one cannot obtain a degree by chance is evident in this case.

**Lingzhi** In the year 1493 of the Hongzhi era, a lingzhi mushroom grew at the base of the wall of my fellow villager Lu Zhong's house. The following year, it continued to grow nine more, some with double-layered structures, all brightly colored, but they were all later removed. I once obtained one dried stem and kept it. **Horse Ghost** Among my maternal relatives, there was an elder named Que who said that a neighbor had a good horse grazing at Shahu Lake. The horse fell into the water and died. Afterward, every rainy, stormy, or overcast day, a horse would often be seen galloping on the lake's surface, its fur color vividly alive. When looked at closely, it would disappear immediately. People all said: "This is the ghost of the horse." **Three-Legged Turtle** In the summer of 1490, a villager from Taicang saw a fisherman holding a turtle with three legs. The villager bought it and had his wife cook it. After it was cooked, he called his wife to eat it together. The wife did not want to eat it and sat outside. After a long time, he did not hear his wife's voice and went inside to find that she had disappeared. On the ground, only a single strand of hair remained, and her clothes, hat, and shoes were all present, as if she had shed her form. Frightened, she screamed, and the villagers heard her. They suspected that the wife had killed her husband and lied about it, and took her to the authorities. The magistrate of Taicang, Huang Tingxuan from Putian, investigated the matter and found the truth, believing that such a physical anomaly might exist. He released the wife from prison. He summoned the fisherman and ordered him to catch the three-legged turtle within a set time. After several days, the turtle was caught and presented.

In the government office, the woman was summoned and cooked as before, and a serious prisoner was released and ordered to eat her. After he finished eating, he was led back into prison, but by the time they reached the gate, she had completely transformed. The remaining clothes and hair were the same as those of ordinary people. The woman's crime was then pardoned. The fishermen said: "At first, we were ordered to fish in the river. When we lifted the net, we were surprised by its great weight. When we reached the shore and looked at it, it was a meat block shaped like a human, with all five senses present but no hands or feet, and with closed eyes, moving clumsily. The fishermen were greatly frightened and threw it back into the water. Then, in another net, they caught something similar. The fishermen were afraid and collectively bought sacrificial meat and wine to offer to the water god. They prayed, saying, 'We have been ordered by the government to find a three-legged turtle, but instead, we have caught monsters. If we fail to meet the deadline, we will surely be punished. Only the gods can help us.' After finishing their prayer and casting the net again, they finally caught the turtle. They still did not know what the previous two things were. According to the *Er Ya* (Dictionary of Ancient Terms), it says: 'A three-legged turtle is called "Neng." The commentary says: 'Now, in the pool on Jun Mountain in Yangxian, there are three-legged turtles.' The *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) says: 'The *Congshan* (Mount Cong) has many three-legged turtles.' This thing should exist in the world, but when eaten by humans, it transforms, and there are no records of this in history. However, catching two such anomalies at once is particularly unprecedented."

Second Volume of the Gengsi Bian* The Flute Record** **The Temple of Universal Light** **Fangxue** **The Commander of the Seven Generals** **Zhou Qifeng** **The Postmaster of Chai** **The Shrine of Luojiang** **Qi Bianxiu** **The Fox by the River** **Retribution** **The Transformation of the Chicken** **The Sword Pool **The Flute Record** Xu Ao, courtesy name Chaoyi, was from Changzhou, living in a house at the eastern city gate. He was handsome and well-mannered, with a particular aptitude for music and melody. Despite living in the marketplace, he had the refined demeanor of a scholar. In the year Xinyou of the Hongzhi era, he was nineteen years old. His uncle, Zhang Zhen, was a wealthy man who invited Xu Ao to manage his money house, and he provided a small room to the east of the hall as Xu Ao's bedroom. That year, on the seventh day of the seventh month, the moon shone brightly like daylight. Xu Ao played the flute to entertain himself, and as the second drum strike approached, he lay on his bed with a quilt, still playing softly. Suddenly, he smelled a strong, unusual fragrance. The double doors of the room opened on their own, and a large dog with a golden bell around its neck suddenly entered, circling the room once before leaving. Xu Ao was startled by this, and he heard soft voices in the courtyard. A young lady appeared, carrying a plum blossom lamp and ascending the stairs. She was followed by sixteen others, arranged in two rows. The last was a beautiful woman, about eighteen or nineteen years old, wearing a jade crown, phoenix-shaped shoes, and a decorated rhinoceros belt. She wore a square-sleeved brocade robe, with sleeves nearly two feet wide, resembling the attire of a palace lady. Her skin was as smooth as jade, and her beauty shone in the moonlight, truly a heavenly being.

The maidservants' attire was largely the same, but their shapes were slightly smaller, and their appearance was not ordinary. Upon entering, each brought out red candles from their cages and placed them on silver stands, and the room became bright, and the four walls suddenly felt spacious. Ao Gu's knees trembled, and he did not know what to do. The beautiful woman slowly walked to the bed and sat down, extending her hand into the quilt and caressing Ao Gu's body almost entirely. After a long time, she left hurriedly without speaking a word. The maidservants followed her out, and the incense and candles all went out at once. Ao Gu was surprised and confused, and his thoughts and intentions were in turmoil for several days. After three nights, the moonlight became even brighter, and as Ao Gu was about to sleep, he again sensed an unusual fragrance. He thought of the beautiful woman from the previous night and wondered if she might come again. After hesitating for a moment, the maidservants brought the beautiful woman into the room, setting up a feast of wine and delicacies, as if the tables, chairs, and racks had appeared by themselves, though no one was seen carrying them, and everything was fully prepared. The beautiful woman sat facing south, looking around, and her radiance was dazzling. She had the maidservants call Ao Gu, and Ao Gu straightened his clothes and stood up to bow to her. The beautiful woman then gestured for him to sit beside her. The maidservants presented jade cups of wine, which had an exceptionally strong and pure flavor, and the dishes were extremely refined and exquisite, with a variety of land and sea delicacies that were indescribable. The beautiful woman said to Ao Gu, "Do not doubt or be surprised, I am not here to harm you."

The predestined bond between us, I believe, should lead to harmony. Though I may not be able to greatly benefit you, I can ensure that your resources are never in short supply, and that food and drink are always available. You will also have access to exotic delicacies, fine fabrics such as silk and brocade, and all the desirable things of the world. If you desire something, it will not be difficult to obtain, but I worry that your fate may be thin. I then personally offered you a toast, inviting you to sit closer and join in the laughter. My words were warm and gentle. You could only nod in agreement, unable to speak, and focused on eating and drinking. The lady said, "I heard the sound of a flute yesterday and knew your interest in music was not shallow. I myself have a slight understanding of stringed and wind instruments, and I would like to hear you play." She asked her servant to fetch a flute and handed it to you. After you finished playing, the lady followed with a piece of her own, her melody clear and resonant. However, you could not comprehend it. She then smiled and said, "A woman from the Qin family can only play the simple tunes of the world. How could she attract a phoenix? If that flute player were here, he should not be ashamed to serve as a servant to Mr. Xu." With that, she left quickly.

The next evening, she arrived again. As the drinking feast neared its end, a maidservant reported, "The night is deep." She then swept the bed and urged him to sleep. The woman smiled hesitantly and lingered for a long time before they ascended the bed together. The curtains and bedding were of the utmost beauty and splendor, far more luxurious than what he had slept on before. The protagonist thought, "I will try to fall to the ground as a test, to see what she will do." As he thought this, the floor below the bed was already covered with brocade rugs, leaving almost no empty space. The woman undressed, wearing only a red silk wrap around her waist. They lay down together and met, and soon their passion was intense, with sweat and exertion making them turn and twist, unable to bear it. At this moment, the protagonist's emotions and thoughts were flying wildly, and he was almost crazed, yet he could not utter a word. As dawn approached, the woman rose first and lifted the curtain. More than ten maidservants brought water and washed his hands and face. After a long time, she finished dressing and said goodbye, telling the protagonist, "Grateful for the time and circumstances, we are able to be together, which is truly not easy. From now on, our joy and affection will be without interruption. If you have a thought, I will come back immediately. But I worry about your heart, it is easy to change your mind. And many words are to be feared. I truly do not want to let the common people know. You must keep it a secret." She then departed. The protagonist was suddenly lost in thought, lingering and gazing for a long time. During the day, people noticed the unusual strong and cool fragrance on his clothes, and many found it strange.

From then on, every time he thought of her, a sudden fragrance would appear, and the beautiful lady would come. When she arrived, she would bring wine and they would enjoy a feast together. She frequently told him about celestial matters and the transformations of various immortals, her words were wonderful and not heard in the world. He wanted to ask her directly about where she lived, but whenever he saw her, he would stammer in words. So he wrote a short note to ask her, but she never answered, saying, "You have a good wife, if you are content, why bother to ask further?" She occasionally said, "I came from Jiuhu, I heard that Suzhou and Hangzhou are famous prefectures with many beautiful sights, so I am temporarily traveling. In this world, everywhere is my home." Although she was gentle and self-satisfied, she ruled her subordinates very strictly. The attendants around her trembled and bowed with utmost respect, and she made them serve him as if they served themselves. One attendant served soup, slightly hesitating, and she immediately pulled her ear, making her kneel and apologize until it was over. Whenever he needed something, it would arrive as desired. One day, when he went out, he saw oranges by the roadside and very much wanted them. By evening, the beautiful lady took out hundreds of them and left them. If something was not obtained in the market, she would certainly go to great lengths to obtain it. He had several pieces of fine cloth, sometimes cutting six feet and hiding them. He was just diligently searching for them when the beautiful lady came, told him where they were, and ordered him to collect them.

Lost gold jewelry was found missing in the pawnshop, and the beauty ordered a search for it in the money shop on the west side of the Yellow Ox Lane. The thief had exchanged it for some amount of money and left. The next day, they went to visit the shop, and the object was still there. They took it directly and returned, and the owner just stared blankly. Ao often had disputes with others, and when he was slightly defeated, the other person either fell down inexplicably or was unjustly humiliated for other reasons. The beauty would then tell him, "These servants are rude; I have already reported it for you." This went on for several months, and outside, people sometimes heard about it. Those who loved Ao suspected that she was a demon and advised him not to get close. The beauty already knew this and said to Ao, "You foolish servant is speaking nonsense; how could there be a demon like me in the world?" Ao once went out on business and fell slightly ill in the inn. The beauty suddenly came and sat beside him, meeting with him as usual. There were many people sleeping there, but she was completely unaware of it. She repeatedly warned Ao, "Do not casually tell others about it, for it might not be a blessing for you." But Ao could not keep quiet and often leaked out the information, causing the rumors to spread widely. Some secretly watched and waited, and the beauty became angry. Coincidentally, Ao's mother heard about the matter and sent for Ao to return home, planning to marry him off to cut ties with her. Ao could not disobey. One night, the beauty appeared and said, "You have an outside heart now; I dare not follow you anymore." She then stopped coming altogether. Ao missed her, but in the end, he could not bring her back.

On the day after the full moon of the eleventh month, Zhuo dreamed at night that four soldiers came to call him. They passed by the alley where he lived, standing outside the land temple. One soldier went in to call the land god, who emerged as an old man in a square cap and white robe. Another soldier said, "The lady has summoned you." Zhuo followed them out of the city gate, walking on water to cross, and arrived at a grand mansion. The walls were surrounded by hundreds of tall trees that shaded the sky and sun. They passed through three sets of gates, each painted red with animal rings and golden nails, guarded by people. They entered the hall, which was about eight or nine ren high, with steps leading up in dozens of layers. Below, a crane was resting with its neck bent, its colorful embroidery and vermilion and green colors shining brightly. A young girl in green clothes saw Zhuo from afar and ran in to report, saying, "The unfeeling husband has come." Inside the hall, there were many women holding incense, playing with parrots, playing the pipa, singing, and dancing, too many to count, taking turns looking at Zhuo through the window cracks. Some were old acquaintances who called out to him, while others muttered insults. Soon, the sound of jade pendants tinkling and the fragrance of incense rising like clouds filled the air. Inside the hall, they reported one after one, "The lady has arrived."

The old man led Aò to kneel, peering through the curtain to see a large golden hearth burning animal charcoal. A beautiful woman sat by the hearth, holding a fork and stoking the fire, often sighing and saying, "I always said he had no luck, and it's true." After a while, she called for the curtain to be drawn back. The woman saw Aò and reproached him, saying, "You are very unfaithful. What did I tell you before, and yet you have betrayed me! Today, what shame do you feel?" She then wept and said, "We were supposed to be together forever, who would have thought it would end like this?" Some of the women and attendants around her said, "Lady, do not harm yourself. This man is unrighteous, and he should be killed. Why are you still talking about it?" She gestured to the guards to beat Aò with a large stick, up to eighty blows. Aò cried out, "Lady, I truly am unfaithful, but I remember your kindness and the deep feelings we shared. That flute is still there, why no affection?" The woman then called for the beating to stop and said, "I really wanted to kill you, but remembering our past, I will spare your life today." Aò rose and prostrated himself in gratitude, and she let him go. The old man still escorted him back, and as they climbed the bridge, Aò lost his footing and woke up. His thighs were severely injured, and he could not get up. A few days later, he saw the woman again, who reproached him as before, saying, "It's your own bad luck, not my fault." After she left, his injuries began to heal. Later, he visited the place near the bridge, but could not find any trace of her. Finally, it remained unknown what kind of person she was. I heard about Aò's story when I was young and once confronted him directly, learning the full details. I wrote this account, calling it the "Flute Record."

The Temple of Pu Guang Mr. Shi Jian Gongfu and I lived in the same village. When he was not yet successful, he studied with several friends at Pu Guang Temple in the east of the city. Once, during the day, he took a nap and felt as if someone was calling him. The voice said, "Hurry up and read; you will become a censor, so work hard and take care of yourself." He then awakened with clarity, remembering that what he had seen resembled the statue of the temple guardian. He went to the temple and silently prayed, "If I achieve what the divine has said, I will have the temple and its statues renewed." From then on, every morning when entering and every evening when returning, he would bow every time passing by. His friends would look at him and laugh, but Jian did not care. On the first and fifteenth days of the month, when all his friends had left, he would carry a small offering alone to pay respects, continuing tirelessly despite great busyness. In the year 1499 of the Hongzhi era, Jian passed the imperial examination and became a jinshi, being appointed to his current position. Upon returning home, he set up a vegetarian meal to express gratitude and built a small hall to honor the temple guardian.

#### Fang Xue Fang Xue of Wuxi was pre-selected as a student when he was young. One night, he dreamed of a person who gave him a peach and a pear, saying, "The fate of two people hangs on your hands." He woke up feeling strange and remembered the dream. Later, he passed the provincial examination in the year 1509 and took the imperial examination at the Ministry of Rites. At that time, Jiangyin scholar Xu Jing had a close relationship with the chief examiner, and he was reported by Hua Chang, a censor, who had him sent to the imperial prison for investigation. Hua, being from the same hometown as Fang Xue and having a close relationship with him, cited Fang Xue as evidence and intended to bring him to the imperial court for trial. On the way, Fang Xue met a fellow townsman, the official Gong Anfu, who gave him one peach and one plum each, saying, "The truth or falsehood of the matter depends on your words; the fate of those two people is in your hands." Fang Xue suddenly remembered his earlier dream and was deeply shaken. The peach and plum given by Gong Anfu matched the dream, except that the dream had a pear instead of a plum, but it was still remarkable. Fang Xue testified in the legal case, and many people knew about it, but this account is not detailed here. [Provided by You Si Shao Xiong.]

The Seven Generals' Attendant During the Chenghua period, there was a man named Zhang Wenbao from Suzhou who had a son who died prematurely at a young age. One day, a friend of Zhang encountered his son on the road and, unaware that he was dead, brought him back home. They climbed the stairs, called the family to prepare a feast, and invited them to drink together. The family was surprised to see no guest when they entered, and upon looking upstairs, they saw nothing, but the son spoke and bowed as if talking to someone. Startled, they spat at him, and then he vanished. The friend realized that he had already died. A few days later, when the friend left through the Qi Gate on some errand, he met the son again. The son said, "Why have you become so harsh? I am not one who would bring harm to you. I am now under the command of the Seven Generals, and the temple is not far from here. Could you visit me?" The friend then accompanied him to the temple, where they entered a room in the side hall and sat to talk for a long time. The son then said, "There is a family in a certain place who is ill, and their many rituals and offerings are of no use." Pointing to the hall, he said, "This is just lacking one sheet of paper for my master. You should tell them this, and the matter will be resolved." The friend returned home and informed the family as instructed. They performed the sacrifice as directed, and the illness was cured. The Seven Generals were a local figure named Jin Yuan, worshipped privately by the people of the seven towns.

○Zhou Qifeng Zhou Qifeng, originally named Feng, was from Qingyang, Jiangyin. He was exceptionally intelligent, unparalleled in his abilities, and possessed a hundred skills; his poetry, prose, and calligraphy were also noteworthy. Throughout his life, everything he used was made by himself. Once, he and his servant each bought a hat. Later, he said, "Is my hat really no different from yours?" He then divided it, and the servant went somewhere. A short while later, the servant returned, but Zhou Qifeng had already sewn gold thread into it and was wearing it. His dexterity and ingenuity were of this kind. However, he was cunning and deceitful, using evil arts to commit immoral acts, and thus was disgraced among people. He stayed at a wealthy family's home, drinking heavily with the host, and went to bed. In the middle of the night, the hostess was restless and unable to sleep, as if she heard someone calling. She opened the door to go out, hesitated and doubted herself, woke her husband up, and told him the reason. The husband went to investigate and found Zhou Qifeng naked and disheveled, jumping and dancing in a ritual. He grabbed and beat him severely, nearly killing him.

The magistrate prayed for rain in the temple. Feng Qi, dressed in Daoist robes with a braided bun and carrying a sword, went to pay respects. The magistrate did not recognize him. They spoke, and Feng Qi gradually became more intimate. Soon, he threw his sword, stepped, and flew into the air. The magistrate was greatly surprised, thinking a true immortal had arrived. When Feng Qi left, the officials talked and laughed about it. Later, the magistrate heard about it, intending to capture him, but he had already escaped. Feng Qi later became a guest at the Lu family in Xintang. The Lu brothers were named Ji Fang and Ji Yuan. When Ji Yuan died, Ji Fang divided the property unequally, and Ji Yuan's wife, He, was angry about it. At that time, the Chief Justice Xiong Gai was touring and inspecting Jiangnan, wielding great power and cruelty. When he reached Jiangyin, He listed Ji Fang's illegal actions and went to the water's edge to appeal. Xiong Gai did not accept it, and He jumped into the water. Xiong Gai then accepted it.

Ji Fang, fearing for his safety, entrusted ten taels of gold to Qi Feng to resolve matters in the capital. Qi Feng squandered it almost entirely, and Lu Shu was ultimately seized and confiscated. Resenting this deeply, he implicated Qi Feng in his accusations. After Ji Fang was already punished by law, Qi Feng changed his name and hid in the rivers and lakes, having no fixed residence, living on a boat, and indulging in extreme luxury, with all his tableware made of gold. Once he arrived in Suzhou, a Suzhou man named Qian Ye sent him a poem saying, "Hearing that you are talented but have not yet met, where have you been these years, seeking your whereabouts? A guest in a foreign land like Zhang Jiean, who in the world is like Kong Rong? In the marketplace, under the spring blossoms and singing birds, drinking wine; at the river bridge, in the wind and rain at night, pushing open the door. All schemes are left to the east-flowing river, returning to the dream of my homeland." Qi Feng was deeply moved upon receiving the poem. Later, he went to the capital to clear his name, but ultimately died of illness in a guesthouse. Liu, an official, bought a coffin and buried him. After his death, in the Three Wu regions, there were those who summoned him as a spirit, and when Qi Feng appeared, his writings often resembled his works in life, and his predictions were often remarkably accurate. One day, he wrote a poem: "The moon over Chang'an, a thousand miles away, the spring at Du Ling in the third month."

With one cup of tea and the scent of incense, the gentle breeze brings an old friend. It also says, "A solitary journey abroad, the meeting of influential people in the autumn of life. I will carry spiritual virtue to the afterlife, causing even ghosts and gods to be troubled." He wrote after the poem, "If you set up tea and incense and recite this poem, I will come." Later, when they tried it, it proved true. The governor of Songjiang lost some gold jewelry in his office and consulted a spirit. The spirit wrote four lines: "Finding relief after a long drought, meeting an old friend in a foreign land. The night of marriage, passing an exam and being listed on the imperial register." When asked to explain the meaning, the spirit did not respond. When asked to write his name, it wrote, "Zhou Qifeng." The governor was displeased, thinking that the ghost's words were not reliable. He mentioned this to a scholar, who replied, "This is the four-joy poem that people commonly talk about." The governor was surprised and said, "I have a female servant named Si Xi (Four Joys), could that be the case?" He arrested and questioned her, and indeed, the stolen items were found, still hidden in the ashes behind the office building. He then understood the previous statement. My great-grandfather also knew Qi Feng, but he disliked his character and would send him to a summer house whenever he came, not allowing him to visit the main house.

**○柴驿丞** In the year Jingtai, during the reign of Emperor Jingtai, Sheng Chang of Wujiang passed the provincial examination and traveled north with Chang Biaoda, a senior official from Changshu, his brother Li Ge, and two other scholars. They reached a post station in Shandong, where Chang and his companions arrived first, leaving Sheng Chang slightly behind. The postmaster, Mr. Chai, came out to greet them and stared at the group for a long time before asking, "Where is the fifth person among you?" The others replied, "He is coming soon." The postmaster then asked, "Is he not wearing green?" The others were surprised and asked how he knew. The postmaster said, "Last night, I had a dream of an old man with a white beard who said, 'Tomorrow, five scholars will come here, and one of them wearing green will be your future benefactor. Do not treat him rudely.' That is why I had a slight hope." A short while later, Sheng Chang arrived, and the postmaster was very happy. He invited the five to stay and provided them with very rich accommodations. He personally performed menial tasks for Sheng Chang, working diligently in many ways, which made the others smile secretly. Upon reaching the capital, Sheng Chang was eventually selected. Soon after, he became a censor and was sent to distribute horses in Shandong. When he arrived, the postmaster was just being sued by the circuit inspector and was about to be demoted to commoner status. Sheng Chang went to visit him and pleaded on his behalf, but the circuit inspector did not listen. When Sheng Chang got the court documents, he tore them up by hand and then took a pen to write a separate case, completely clearing the postmaster's guilt. The circuit inspector could not help but accept it, and the postmaster was released. The dream of the benefactor was not false after all.

### 罗江神祠 Chang, who was demoted from the position of censor to an official in Gǔtián, soon after received imperial favor and was appointed to govern Luojiang County. Behind the government office, there was a local shrine dedicated to the land god, established by the previous magistrate. It was very famous for its miraculous nature, and the magistrate would always pray there for important matters, holding worship and offerings without fail every month. Since Chang took office, this practice no longer continued. One day, the chicken kept in his private residence went missing, and upon searching, it was found in front of the shrine, with its wings spread and head down as if nailed to the ground. When he asked the servants, they all said the deity was punishing the shrine because it had not been worshipped for a long time. Chang became angry and went to the shrine to scold the deity, expressing his intention to destroy it. That night, he dreamed that the deity came to him, apologizing sincerely and saying, "I have been worshipped here for many years and have never done anything wrong. Yesterday, the chicken was nailed down because the ghost soldiers were suffering from hunger, so they did it. It was not I who dared to do such a thing. Please have mercy and do not destroy it." Chang did not agree and dismantled the shrine the next morning. The former magistrate, whose term had ended, immediately moved his family behind the county office. That night, he dreamed that the deity came to him, pleading for a new shrine to be built. He questioned the deity, saying, "Why didn't you appeal to the new magistrate?" The deity frowned and said, "It must be done by you. Magistrate Sheng is strict and authoritative, and we dare not interfere." The magistrate then built a shrine near his residence to worship the deity.

○Qī Biānxiū During his youth, Qī Lán from Yúyáo suffered a serious illness, his breath had ceased, and after some time he revived. He claimed that he was taken by someone to an official place, where a noble person sat on the dais, introduced him, and asked about his hometown, name, and age. Qī answered thoroughly. The noble person said, "It is not so, you have made a mistake." He looked at the official and ordered him to release him, and Qī was released. Returning to the halfway point, he encountered rain and rested in a temple. Entering a room, he found the floor full of hat forms, which he tried to lift with his hand but could not. A person nearby said to him, "These are not your possessions, what you have is here." Pointing to a rack, he ordered Qī to take it, and Qī took it easily, seeing that it had the word 'seven' written on it. Later, Qī indeed became a Jinshi and ended his career as a Hanlin Academy editor.

A Fox from Linjiang A wealthy man named Chen Chonggu from Linjiang had an orchard behind his residence, and he entrusted one person to guard it, handling all profits from selling and buying. The man was around forty years old, well-groomed, and did not look like an ordinary person; he lived alone in a small house in the garden. One night, a beautiful woman came to him, saying she could drink, asked for wine to drink together, and sought pleasure. The man doubted her, asked about her residence and name, but she never answered, saying, "I have a predestined relationship with you, so I follow you; don't ask." So they became intimate. From then on, she came every night, and after a long time, their relationship became as close as husband and wife, and he no longer asked about where she came from. The neighbors were surprised by the frequent sounds of voices in the garden and saw it, reporting it to the owner. The owner was concerned because he was spending money, called him to account, and the man initially denied it. Then he asked the owner to check and verify, and there was no loss or damage. The owner asked more deeply, and the man then revealed the truth, and the owner also accepted it.

That night, the girl came and said, "Do you think I am luring you for your wealth?" Then, speaking calmly, she said, "I am not one who brings calamity to you. In this world, there are no fewer than a thousand like me, all of whom pursue the Dao of immortality. My matter is nearly complete; I merely borrow your yang energy to assist me. In a few more days, when the number is fulfilled, I shall no longer remain here, and you will suffer no harm." On another day, he came, drank heavily, and became drunk, speaking jestingly and growing more intimate with her. He then tried to test her, saying, "Is there anything in this world that you fear?" The girl, drunk and forgetful of caution, and trusting their long acquaintance, answered frankly, "I fear nothing. When I sleep, light surrounds my body. If anyone wishes to harm me, the moment they approach this light, I become aware, and they will never be able to do me harm. What I most hate is if a person stands far away and opens their mouth to slowly inhale this light — then he gains longevity while I suffer calamity." He merely nodded in agreement. After she left, he watched her go, and from afar, he saw her stagger and fall into the field. He went to see and found the girl sleeping soundly, with light shining on the ground like the moon. Following her words, he inhaled it, feeling a faint warmth in his chest. The light was completely absorbed, and then he returned home. The next day, he returned to the place and found an old fox dead there. During the Jingtai period, Sheng Yungao was in charge of the salt tax in Yangzhou. A merchant from the Chen family in Yangzhou recounted this story, saying that this man was still alive, now over ninety years old. [The above four stories were told by Sheng Yungao's son, Yong.]

Fruit and Retribution Sheng Dong of Wu, the ninth of his name, had long been known for his intelligence and authoritarian rule in his village. There was a man named Yu Ying from the same village, whose concubine had an illicit relationship with a slave. When the affair was discovered, Yu Ying killed the slave and secretly ordered a man from Changshu named Gu to transport the body elsewhere and burn it. Instead, Gu secretly buried the body beneath the city walls and deceived Yu Ying, saying, "I have already burned it." Later, Gu offended Yu Ying in some matter, fearing punishment, and planned to exhume the slave's body to threaten him. He consulted Sheng Dong about this. Sheng Dong saw this as a valuable opportunity and pretended to agree, but secretly revealed the matter to Yu Ying. Yu Ying, fearing retribution, offered a substantial bribe to Sheng Dong. Sheng Dong then devised a plan, had Gu killed, and burned his body. The matter remained secret, and no one knew what had transpired. Several years later, both Sheng Dong and Yu Ying fell ill. Yu Ying, delirious and seemingly seeing something, recounted the earlier events and said, "Now I am being sued by Gu, and I will surely go with the Nine Old Men." Both men died on the same day. Sheng Dong's illness worsened, and he repeatedly called for his horse. At the time, the horse he rode was being grazed at a nearby temple. When Sheng Dong's spirit departed, the horse suddenly leaped and died. As Yu Ying was dying, he called to his family, "Has the Nine Old Men gone?" They replied, "He has died." Yu Ying then said, "In that case, I shall go as well," and closed his eyes. [Reported by Yu Chui.]

### Chicken Transformation In the year 辛未 (1621), one of my hens, which had already incubated eggs multiple times, suddenly developed a red crest and a long tail. It could flap its wings and crow loudly, and it also interacted with other hens. Soon after, there was a serious illness in my family, which seemed to be a sign of impending misfortune. ### Sword Pond The water in the Sword Pond at Tiger Hill is clear and cold, and even during drought, it does not decrease much. On 辛未 (1621) December 20th, the pond suddenly dried up, revealing the bottom. Elderly people in their eighties and nineties said, "We have never heard of such an event." The pond is not very deep, and a cave is exposed at the side of the cliff, which can accommodate several people standing. There is nothing of significance inside the cave; it just has several layers of piled stones, looking like a horizontal board. ### Conclusion

General Gou Bi Gu Zhen Wang Luan A Pig and Dog Giving Birth to Offspring The God of Zitong A Woman Growing a Beard A Black Calamity A Fire Disaster ○ The Fox of the Western Mountains Fan Yi, an expert in pulse diagnosis and medicine, served as a medical officer in the Yuan Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Taiding, and was seventy years old. Once, an old woman came to his door and said, "My two daughters are ill, and I wish to ask you to come and treat them." When asked where her home was, she replied, "The Western Mountains." Fan Yi was hesitant due to the long distance and his age, and he declined, saying, "If it is absolutely necessary, you may bring them here for treatment." The woman left and returned after a long time, bringing the two girls with her. Both were young and beautiful. Fan Yi was startled upon examining them, saying, "How can both of them have the pulse of non-humans? They must be of a different kind." He then said to the woman, "Do not hide anything from me; you should tell me the truth." The woman, trembling with fear, knelt and confessed, "I am not human; I am an old fox from the Western Mountains. I heard of your divine skills and your ability to heal my daughters, so I came to seek your help. Now that I have revealed myself, I hope the kind-hearted will show mercy and accept me." Fan Yi said, "It is my wish to help all beings, so I would not refuse you. However, this is the imperial city where the emperor resides, and it is guarded by myriad spirits. How could you, an ugly creature, come here?" The woman replied, "The true emperor is currently in Huaizhou, and the city deities and local officials have all moved there. This place is now empty, so we can come and go without hindrance." Fan Yi was surprised by her words and gave her medicine. The woman and her two daughters bowed in gratitude and left. At that time, the Hongwu Emperor was still in hiding in the Huai River region. Fan Yi was the maternal grandfather of Liu Yuanbo, a scholar from my hometown. Liu's ancestor was able to recount this story.

Master Cheng's Descent to Write In the year 1499 during the reign of the Ming Dynasty Emperor Zhengde, Master Cheng of Huangduan, a prominent scholar, served as the chief examiner for the imperial examination. Due to his remonstrations, he was dismissed from his post and not long after, he died of an illness on his back. That same year, there were those in the capital who sought the company of immortals on a snowy night. Master Cheng appeared and wrote down the following: "At night, I traveled with Su Dongpo, and heard that there were those who sought the company of immortals. I too am of the same ilk as exiled immortals, and my misfortunes have been great. I shall record this in verse." He then wrote a quatrain: "When will I return to these mountains and rivers? The white bones and green forests are a matter of sorrow. My comrades do not pity my distant clear dreams, but the eastern sea leads to the mythical island of Penglai. [The original text reads '海东东去去蓬莱,' but it is said that the library edition reads '海东东去是蓬莱.']" He also wrote two couplets: "The glory of the purple hall has nearly faded, and my writings alone dominate the scholarly world. A lone boat dares to hang the sun in the sky, and my noble spirit should still reach the stars. Su Shi, like a pine tree in hibernation, suffered many slanders, and Du Fu, in the wilderness, called out in despair. The vast heavens and earth cannot contain the meaning of the flowing river, and turning back, I see a solitary hill of green." "The literati of the past and present are all worthy of pity, and my true teachings have turned to ashes. The wild geese have not yet risen high enough to escape the nets, and the qilin is occasionally seen but often misunderstood. The swift thunder does not arise from the golden seal, and who knows the purple lightning is the talent of the military arsenal. This spirit cannot be compared to the fragrance of grass, and the cyclical nature of life and death." Readers were moved by these verses, and considering the spirit and style, they seemed to reflect Master Cheng's character and disposition.

Mr. Jiang Mr. Jiang, whose given name is Huan, was a native of Wu. He was a handsome young man with a gentle and refined nature. In the year Xinyou of the Hongzhi era (1501 AD), he passed the county examination as a student and was recommended for the provincial examination. On his way north to take the metropolitan examination, he traveled through Linqing. At dusk, he rested at a civilian's house by the roadside and admired the elegant appearance of the house. He lingered for a while, and in the hall, he saw a young lady reflected in a square window. She quietly stood alone, noticing Jiang's demeanor, and her emotions stirred. She called a servant to invite him into the main hall. The young lady changed her clothes and came out to bow. She had a beautiful face and youthful teeth, almost like a celestial being. Jiang was immediately captivated by her. They hesitated before setting up wine and food, inviting him to sit, and their conversation became slightly intimate. Until nightfall, they entered a small pavilion together, and they became intimately entangled. At the time, his father was away on other business, and after three days, he returned and was informed by the family. The father heard this and was very angry, intending to apprehend them. After thinking for a long time, he looked at Jiang and said, "You are a fine young man from a good family. My daughter, whom I have always cherished, has now come to this situation, and there is nothing we can do about it."

The husband said, "Even if I am willing to sacrifice myself for you, it is not enough to redeem my shame. But my daughter still has no husband. Can you marry her? Otherwise, I will take you to the authorities." The man obediently agreed, and they lived together as husband and wife, staying for over ten days. Soon the exam date approached, so he bid farewell and set off on his journey. As they parted, they looked at each other with tears streaming down their faces, got on the carriage, and left. Upon arriving in the capital to take the exam, he failed, and returned to the father-in-law's house. The father-in-law wept as he welcomed him, saying, "Since you left, my daughter grieved day and night, fell ill, and has now died." He showed him the daughter's bedding, and the husband's tears welled up in his eyes; he fainted to the ground. That night, he set up an altar and wept loudly. He said goodbye to the father-in-law and boarded the boat, and the daughter was already there. From then on, they traveled by boat for over a month, often feeling her presence nearby. When they arrived home, she was already in the room. From then on, they never parted, even when drinking tea, he saw her in the cup. The husband became bewildered, disoriented, and emaciated, and fell ill with a wasting disease. Family members questioned him, and he began to recount the events. His illness worsened, so he moved to a rented house in the city, and the daughter followed him again. Soon after, she died, and she was only twenty-three years old. My sister's husband is related to the husband and can tell the story.

**The Strange Phenomenon of the Sheng Family** The county physician, Sheng Zao, was summoned to oversee prison affairs. Several prisoners died in ways that defied logic. In the fourth month of summer in the year Ren Shen, Sheng ceased his duties and brought several prison instruments home with him. The prisoners, relying on these instruments, transformed into spirits that plagued the household. At first, a dog brought a dead fox into the house from outside, placing it on the ground. The fox suddenly leaped up, and the dog stood on its hind legs to fight it. The family struck and chased the creatures away, and they immediately disappeared. From then on, strange phenomena began to occur: objects and tables hit each other, beds and mats moved by themselves, and a girl dressed in green was often felt in the room, only to suddenly burrow under the bed and vanish. A man wearing a single garment was seen going back and forth in the corridor, and suddenly turned into a large pig, disappearing in an instant. The women often sat at night and saw a strange object standing outside the window, about a zhang tall, and they all fled in fear. The ghost entered, raised its hand and shook the lamp, its shadow covering the entire room. On the Dragon Boat Festival, a doctor gave a pig's head as a gift, which was placed on a meat stand. It made a continuous, long cry, and when cut into four parts and hung, it continued to cry. Another gift of vegetarian steamed buns was brought, and as it was held, a sound like a ghost was heard inside. Such events continued for several months, and despite many attempts to ward them off, they were ineffective. The family moved to the city, and the strange phenomena gradually ceased. However, afterward, Sheng's three sons died one after another, and the family members also fell ill, leading to a disarray of deaths and illnesses. Eventually, peace was restored.

A Human Turned into a Cow In the Da Lushan Alley of Suzhou, there was a family of tofu makers named Tang who made a living by grinding flour. His wife, Lu, had a younger brother who had died four years ago. One day, the youngest son of the Tang family took a nap during the day and dreamed that his brother-in-law came to him and said, "I was unfortunate to die and was punished by being turned into a cow. Now I am sold to your household. Since we are related by marriage, please treat me kindly. Look for the one with a white film over the eye; that will be me." He awoke in alarm and asked his household workers. They told him that two days ago, they had exactly bought two cows, and the smaller one indeed had a white film over the eye. Later, the seller came and said this cow was exactly four years old. It was known that Lu usually traded with Tang, owing him money, not paying on time, and once swore, saying, "If I owe money, I should be reborn as an animal to repay you." Thus, people believed this was a case of karma.

#### Wang Quan of Jia Ding Wang Quan, a wealthy man from Jia Ding, once dreamed that his deceased father said: "When I was alive, I owed money to someone named Jiang Yin. Now I have been reborn into their family as a cow to repay the debt, and it is almost complete. You should redeem me back. Among all the cows, only my body is white. Remember this well, and do not mention the price." Wang Quan went to the family and found that there was indeed a white cow in the pen. He asked to buy it, but the family cherished the cow because it was strong and good at carrying loads, so they did not agree. Only by offering double the price could he bring it back. He covered it with a canopy and fed it the best fodder and beans. After several years, the cow died. #### Yuan Shangbao Yuan Shangbao, a native of Yin, inherited his father's legacy and was skilled in physiognomy. Once, he traveled through Wu and passed by the house of Shen at Chang Gate. Shen had a son who was just one year old, and Wang Quan carried him to be examined. Yuan Shangbao smiled and patted his head, saying: "Cut head, cut head." He said no more, and Shen thought it was a joke. The son grew up and became a nameless Shen Hong, who was cruel and unscrupulous, and eventually faced a serious crime. That year, the prison records showed only this one person. The Wu proverb still has the saying "Shen Hong exits Chang Gate alone and kills." Yuan Shangbao once entered the home of Xu Shengyao in Nan Hao. Xu's son was just three days old and was crying while being bathed. Yuan Shangbao and the others heard the cry and said: "This is a bandit." Xu heard and became angry, almost wanting to beat him. The child later died due to a ball game incident. In ancient times, there were those who could tell of clan destruction by looking at a bear's appearance and recognize the loss of their clan by hearing a jackal's cry. Yuan Shangbao's ability is not far behind.

○ The Child Who Returns Gold When Yuan Shangbao Zhongche lived in his hometown, there was a young boy at his friend's house who was both handsome and shrewd. Shangbao observed the boy and thought he might be a threat to his master, so he ordered him to be expelled. Although the friend had always been in awe of Shangbao's skills, he felt pity and repeatedly advised him, but finally, he reluctantly agreed. The boy was eventually sent away and had no place to go, so he wandered from place to place, eating at others' homes. One night, he stayed in an ancient temple and could not sleep for a long time. He saw a bundle of gold and silver, wrapped in a tattered robe, in the corner of the wall, and was tempted to take it. Suddenly, he sighed and said, "I am unfortunate and cannot please my master. I was unjustly sent away. Now, if I were to take this treasure, it would be unjust, and the heavens would not tolerate it even more. I should guard it until the rightful owner returns." By dawn, he stayed in the temple without leaving. Soon after, he heard crying and saw a woman come, wiping her tears. She looked around hesitantly. He asked her, and she replied, "My husband is a soldier. He was imprisoned for a matter and should be sentenced to death. The commander, someone, should deal with it."

A woman, in dire straits, sold her property and borrowed money, collecting a certain amount of gold and silver, which she intended to offer to him. She wrapped the money in a tattered robe and carried it to a temple for a brief rest, but unconsciously left it behind. Unable to find it, she exclaimed, "My husband will surely die!" The child carefully counted the number of ingots, and they all matched, so he immediately returned it to her. Grateful, the woman wanted to share some as thanks, but he refused and took it away. The husband was thus released, and remembering the child's virtue, she told everyone about it. The commander heard about it and was amazed, so he ordered someone to find and bring him home to raise. As he grew older without children, he liked the child's beauty and wisdom, and adopted him. After several more years, he retired. This adopted son then inherited the position, returned home, and paid respects to his former master. The master sighed and said, "Was Master Yuan's method so careless?" He kept him waiting until Master Yuan arrived, and had him return in his old clothes, serving tea. Master Yuan saw him, jumped up in surprise, and said, "Is this the same person? How could this be?" The master falsely claimed that he had been expelled and had no place to go, and now he had returned. Master Yuan laughed and said, "You are not joking with me; I am no longer your servant. I am a third-rank military official now, and my appearance and spirit have changed greatly from before. How could I have done good deeds to reach this point?" This adopted son recounted the previous events, and his friend then marveled at Master Yuan's skill.

### The Official of Household, Marriage, and Kinship In Suzhou Prefecture, there was a student named Wu Zhao. He was magnificent in appearance and had a loud, clear voice. One night, he dreamed that swift messengers presented him with a tablet, upon which were inscribed several lines of red characters. They knelt before him and said, "We are commanded to invite you to serve as an official of household, marriage, and kinship disputes. Please proceed quickly." Wu Zhao did not have time to ask detailed questions in his dream, but upon waking, he found the dream repulsive.
