Later, while visiting the Temple of the Water God, he recalled his dream and asked a Taoist priest, "Are there any references to an official of household, marriage, and kinship disputes in Daoist texts?" The priest replied, "Yes, it is mentioned in a certain book." Upon hearing this, Wu Zhao became even less happy. Shortly after, he fell ill, and his condition worsened. He spoke as if giving orders to subordinates, and eventually died. It is suspected that he took up this post in the afterlife. ### The Ancient Bronze Basin with a Duck In the right chamber of Zhu Chang's house in Men Village, there was an ancient bronze basin. Inside, the shape of a duck was faintly visible, and initially, it was not considered unusual. One day, a farmer plowed the fields and found a bronze duck. Not recognizing it, the farmer sold it at a low price to Zhu. When the duck was placed in the basin, it perfectly matched the shadow inside, without a hair's breadth difference. When water was poured into the basin, the duck immediately floated and bathed itself. Zhu then considered it a rare treasure. Later, Zhu's house was burned down, and the basin was reduced to ashes. [These three stories were recorded by Xu Er Yuan.]

**Stone Figure of a Standing Attendant** A local physician named Zhu Ji was constructing a garden island and purchased stones from the western mountains. While digging, they discovered a stone lying in the earth. The workers broke it off with shovels. When planted and observed, the top of the stone resembled a woman's bun, the lower part below the shoulders looked like sleeves hanging down to the waist, and the smaller parts on the sides resembled flowing ribbons. It appeared as if a standing attendant from a painting. When struck, it produced a clear and resonant sound like a jade chime. Unfortunately, it had already been broken, and they merely sighed and placed it aside. **Madness Caused by a Snake** In Jiading, there was a man named Wang, whose family was quite wealthy and who was around forty years old. He contracted a form of madness. He once shouted at his family, saying, "My waist feels heavy; why don't you take it off?" The condition lasted for several years without improvement. One day, Wang's nephew came to visit and pitied his condition. He asked to go back with him to let him wander and enjoy himself. They stayed for several days, but his illness remained the same. The nephew often had someone accompany him. One day, Wang was strolling in the backyard, where a flock of cranes was kept. The cranes rushed around him, pecking at his waist. Just then, the attendant arrived and drove the cranes away. Wang broke out in a sweat and felt the illness disappear. He returned to tell his nephew, "I am cured. I just encountered a flock of cranes. A sudden shock made me feel my waist suddenly lighten, and it felt very good." The nephew was delighted and went to check the backyard, where he saw a snake as big as a beam, dead in the bamboo grove, still with blood on it. From then on, Wang recovered. He suspected that the illness was caused by the snake, as the cranes had been pecking it. [According to Wu Yue's account.]

○苟毕元帅 A Taoist priest named Guo Yuansheng, a descendant of a noble family, was highly skilled in Taoist practices. A resident of Yimaqiao named Li Xu contracted an illness and invited Guo to perform a ritual. At dusk, Guo held a sword and a water basin to ward off evil spirits at Li's sleeping quarters. Afterward, Li asked his wife, "Who was that person?" She replied, "It was Master Guo." Li was surprised and said, "I just saw a man with long hair tied on his forehead, with a rope on his left and a mallet on his right, resembling a divine being. Who is he?" His wife thought he was delirious and did not understand. Li's illness suddenly disappeared, and he went to Guo to describe what he had seen. Guo said, "I invoked the Thunder and Lightning God, Spoiled General, in my heart." Li then realized and bowed deeply in gratitude. ○顾镇 In the summer of 1511 during the Ming Dynasty, an epidemic spread widely. The family of Gu Zhen, a resident of the western part of Fongmen, were all infected with the disease. They prayed to the gods and vowed to eat only vegetarian food to ward off the disaster. Just as the governor opened the granaries to distribute relief, Gu entered the city gate to collect rice. He accidentally forgot his vow and bought three fish and a pot of wine at a shop, drank and ate them, and returned by boat without telling his family. That day, he fell ill and died shortly after. His family saw three small fish on his back, and when they prepared the coffin, they saw the three fish jump into the coffin. When they searched for them, they were no longer there. They asked others who had entered the city with him and learned that the fish he had eaten were the same ones. The gods must have used this to warn him.

### 王栾 葑门人王栾，于辛未年冬至日前往玄妙观的高真殿烧香。途中见到渔夫手持一只非常肥大的鳖，王栾平素就喜欢这种食物，便命令随从买下它，先回家烹制。进入庙中后，他心中只想着那只鳖，丝毫没有虔诚恭敬之心。回家吃完饭后，到了傍晚，他的阴部一侧突然肿起一块，疼痛难忍，几天后几乎丧命，尽管医生和祈祷者尝试了各种方法都未能奏效。于是请来巫师周道虎附乩召将，判词说：“温元帅降临报坛。申时玄天亲自降于东南方，以黑云为证。”到了约定的时间，黑云从西南方向升起，隐约可见一位披发持剑的人站在云际，整个殿堂中弥漫着檀香和麝香的香气。不久，乩板剧烈移动，进入王栾的卧室，判词命令他的妻子扶着病人用热水清洗肿处，肿块破裂后露出一根骨头，首尾形状宛如一只鳖，伤口愈合后痊愈。从此，王栾一家对真武非常虔诚恭敬。【以上三事由道士陈然裔讲述。】 ### 猪犬生儿 壬申年春天，【原本作“今年春”，据说库本改为“壬申春”。】 长洲阳城湖旁的一户人家的母猪生下一只幼崽，长着猪头和人的手足。十二月十六日，嘉定二十二都的一户人家的狗生下一只幼崽，形状完全像人，只是脚跟较短，背上有些许毛发。有人认为可能是人与动物交配所生，这种说法或许有道理。

The God of Zitong Chen Ximin Gong Yi's father, Meng Yu, was a simple and honest man, well-regarded in his village. Once, while traveling and using the restroom, he saw a piece of leftover rice at the bottom of a pot near the latrine. He picked it up, washed it in water, and ate it, demonstrating his usual frugality in daily life. That night, he dreamed of a divine being who told him, "Your kindness is so great, you will receive blessings. I am the God of Zitong, and I will be born to greatly benefit your family. I am currently residing in a house on the upper floor of Xianxiang Bridge in Xumen, but the family there does not know how to worship me. Go quickly and bring me back." Upon waking, he told his wife about the dream, and she had had the same dream. They immediately went to the house, and the lady of the house invited them up to the floor. There was a dusty and detached deity image hanging on the wall, so they asked for it and took it home, decorating it and worshipping it with great reverence. Soon after, his wife became pregnant and gave birth to Ximin, who rose to the position of Grand Tutor and Left Censor-in-Chief, and was posthumously honored with the same rank as his official position, with his mother being ennobled as a First-Rank Lady. From my perspective, Ximin Gong's great virtue and remarkable achievements, his contributions to the western lands, and the people's worship of him as a deity, make him a renowned statesman of his time. It seems that his birth was predestined. [This was told by Ximin Gong's cousin Bian to Wu翁.] A Woman with a Beard At the end of the Hongzhi era, a woman from Yingshan County in Suizhou grew a beard that was over three inches long, which was reported in the official gazette. One of my fellow villagers, Zhuo Si, who was a merchant in Zhengyang, once saw a beautiful woman with three twists of beard under her chin, about several dozen strands long, each about an inch, and she was called the Three-Beard Lady.

#### Black Omens In the year of the Water Monkey, there were creatures in Shunde, Zhuozhou, and Hejian in the north. These creatures were a mix of green, red, and black, sometimes appearing like dogs, other times like cats. They moved as swiftly as the wind, descending from the night sky. Some clawed at people's faces and foreheads, others bit their hands and feet. When pursued, they left no trace, being of the nature of black omens. #### Fire Incidents In March, at the Qin Shi Huang Temple in Shandong, the bells and drums rang spontaneously at night without any apparent cause. A fire broke out on a mulberry tree, burning the trunk but leaving the branches and leaves unharmed. The temple was completely destroyed by the fire, but the statues of the gods remained unscathed. In the same month, at the Xianju Camp in Yudan, Jiangxi, there was a severe thunderstorm with a northwest wind. A streak of fire fell like an arrow onto a flagpole, illuminating the surrounding area like a lantern. A soldier shook the flag, and the fire flew straight to the top of the pole. The soldier then fired a cannon to extinguish the fire, causing it to scatter in all directions. The muzzles of all the guns in the camp glowed like stars, and the phenomenon disappeared shortly after. In May, similar glowing phenomena were observed on the muzzles of the guns at the Fangchun North Camp in Guangxi. These events were also reported in the official bulletins. #### Volume IV of *Gengsi Bian* Wang Shinen Wang Zhubu Soul Departure Meat Fungus Zheng Hao Jiang Zixiu Black Fish Green Tiger Huang Changzi

A Rooster's Egg Se Tan Chicken Essence Like the Gentleman The Widow Saw the Dead Child Black Tiger on the Dark Altar Money Snake ○ Wang Shen Neng In Jining, there was a man named Wang Shen Neng, originally from Haizhou. Born in the year Jia Chen of the Zhizheng era of the Yuan dynasty (1354), he entered the country's official calendar in the Gui Mao year of the Chenghua era of the Ming dynasty (1473), having reached the age of one hundred and twenty. He had resided in Jining for sixty years. From his youth, he aspired to the art of longevity and left home to wander the world, seeking a master but finding none. He traveled to Sichuan and heard that there was an extraordinary person on the Snowy Mountains, so he went to seek him out. He saw an old man wearing a felt robe, lying on a stone bed in a deep cave, measuring over three feet in length, with all his senses, hands, and feet resembling those of an infant. Shen Neng bowed to him, but the old man did not respond, so Shen Neng served him on his left and right. The old man did not eat or drink, but he had a bag beside him, which contained something like dry flour, and he would occasionally take some to eat, or scoop up a few liters of spring water to drink. Shen Neng stayed for several days, and the rice he had brought was all gone. He knelt and begged for food, and the old man gave him some of the contents of his bag, which was bitter and hard to swallow. Shen Neng then gathered wild fruits and vegetables to fill his stomach. After three years of diligent and tireless effort, the old man pitied him and one day said to him, "You can now be told the way, but when you have learned it, you must leave the mountains, and in the future, do not lightly impart it to others." He then taught Shen Neng the essentials of shaping his form and refining his energy. After mastering the teachings, Shen Neng bid farewell and left. After a long time, he returned to reside in Jining. His daily diet consisted of eating a few dates, or a few stems of vegetables, and drinking a little water. He had white hair covering his chin, and his skin was like that of a young boy or young woman. At first, people did not recognize him, but later, they began to see him as somewhat unusual.

Commander Wang Xuan of Jining was also from Haizhou. Upon meeting, he asked about his name and was greatly surprised, saying, "I have heard from my ancestors that in my distant past, there was an uncle who was a true scholar named Shi Neng, who liked the Dao and left home, and no one knows what happened to him. Are you him?" He asked about family matters, and everything he said matched. Thus, Wang Xuan visited him daily. People in the state heard and brought gifts, but Shi Neng refused all of them. Wang Xuan had a colleague who went to see him, wanting to learn his techniques. Shi Neng saw him and said, "You have music and entertainers all around, and daily engage in frivolous activities. You are not one of us." The colleague apologized to him. The person was greatly ashamed and then submitted a memorial detailing the situation. The court ordered the governor of Shandong, and he was to ride a sedan chair to the capital to meet the emperor, who granted him treasures and sent him back. When Shi Neng was summoned, Mr. Cheng from Huangdun happened to be passing through the area. He heard the people in the state describe his background like this and then went to pay him a visit.

The recluse, known as Shiren, lived in a secluded part of the city east of the walls, in a deep alley lined with old trees and surrounded by a few dilapidated houses. Inside, his dwelling contained only a sleeping area and nothing else of value. When speaking with guests, he often discussed the virtues of stillness and minimal desires, and after sitting for a long time, he would close his eyes and hold his breath, saying, "An old servant like me has no great accomplishments; the court summoned me through excessive listening. How could I know anything? I have only practiced stillness for a long time. Recently, I have begun to interact with others, and this has greatly disrupted my affairs." When asked about events from the late Yuan and early Ming dynasties, he replied, "I know nothing beyond myself." Three years later, in the year of Bingwu, Yang Nanfeng, a scholar from Suzhou, traveled to Jining on official business and, disguised, sought out Shiren. He found Shiren sitting on a wooden bench, wearing a white robe [which some editions refer to as a "white single-layered robe"]. Yang asked him about the secret to his longevity, and Shiren responded, "There is no other method. I simply do not eat meat, do not marry, do not count or calculate, and do not strive for success." Yang sighed and returned. In summary, Shiren was a person of virtue, a sage.

○Wang, the Clerk During the time when Zhang held power in Suzhou, there was a clerk named Wang who lived opposite the city gate. He was an official from the Yuan dynasty and had accumulated a considerable amount of salary and wealth over the years. Wang died of a severe cold after seven days. After his burial, his two sons divided the estate. They could not find the wealth and suspected that their mother had hidden it, blaming her for it. The mother could not prove her innocence and they argued and quarreled all day. Opposite the Wang household lived a man named Xu, a merchant who traveled far away. He returned to Jinshan and moored his boat at the foot of the Wu Saints Temple. At dawn, he woke up and saw five men in official attire sitting on a boat, all dressed in white. One of them was Wang, the clerk. Xu was acquainted with Wang's family, and Wang was like a father figure to Xu. Xu did not know that Wang had died. He bowed and asked, "Why are you here?" Wang stepped forward and said, "It is good that you have come. I have something I wish to ask of you."

I have been here for several days, arriving in haste and not having time to settle family affairs. I have some meager wealth hidden under the board of my bed, and my two sons do not know about it, so they are competing with the mother. There is also a letter of division hidden in a box, placed on the door curtain and lintel, which you should tell them for me. He then secretly told, "When you return, tell my family that there will be a large army coming to Wu City soon, and most of the people in the city will die. It is advisable to quickly move to Hangzhou to avoid it." Xu nodded and then suddenly boarded the boat and left. Upon returning to the household of the county magistrate, Xu met his wife and told her about the previous encounter. The wife was angry and thought it was nonsense. Xu explained the reason in detail. When the floorboard was removed, eight hundred taels of silver were indeed found, and the box on the door curtain and lintel was also as he said. The family was amazed. Therefore, they all moved their families to Hangzhou together. Within two months, the heavenly army surrounded Wu. [As told by my father.]

**Human Soul's Journey** In the district of Fong Gate, there was a commoner who served as the village headman, tasked with collecting labor fees. He headed towards the Qimen Bridge at Qianwan Li Bridge [Note: The library edition reads "Qimen Ten Thousand Li Bridge"]. During the hot summer months, he set out early in the morning accompanied by a young servant. They took a brief rest under the eaves of a house. The servant sat on the steps and soon fell into a deep sleep. The master also felt drowsy and, in a half-asleep state, saw a small child playing and dancing on the servant's body. The child then descended to the ground and crossed the stream to a vegetable plot using a board. After a long while, the master kicked the servant, who did not wake up. He reached the stream and pulled the board away. The child was unable to cross back and stood by the water, hesitating. The child then placed the board back in its original place and crossed. The child returned to the servant's body and disappeared, causing the servant to wake up. The master questioned the servant, who said, "I just had a dream where I crossed a bridge into a garden filled with tall trees and enjoyed playing. When I tried to return, someone pulled the bridge away, and I almost couldn't cross." The master then realized that the child he saw was the servant's soul.

During the reign of Emperor Ling, there was a scholar in Jiading who once visited a monk. When he happened to be sleeping, the scholar sat in front of the monk's bed and waited for him. Suddenly, he saw a small snake emerge from the monk's nostril and slither to the ground. The scholar was surprised and took a small knife from the table and stuck it in the ground. When the snake reached its side, it seemed to be afraid. When the scholar pulled the knife up, the snake continued its way. The scholar spat on the ground, and the snake licked it up. It then went out to the outdoor water pool, lay and stretched out for a long time, and slowly passed by the flower and medicine stand. It returned to its original path and climbed back onto the bed, entering through the monk's nostril. The monk woke up and spoke to the scholar, saying, "I just dreamed of going out and encountered a thief who planted a blade on the road, almost unable to escape. I saw water by the roadside that tasted like nectar, drank it and found it very delicious, bathed in the sea, and was very happy. I then entered a garden, wandered around, and returned, not knowing what I was feeling." The scholar nodded in agreement but finally did not tell him what he had seen and left. He had heard that human souls could leave the body, and these two events confirmed this belief, indeed.

**Meat Fungus** In the spring of the year Gengyou (or Kuiyou, depending on the calendar system), by the shore of Cao Lake in Changzhou, a peasant woman was tending her fields when she saw an object on the lakebank, white as snow. She hurried to examine it and found it to be a child's hand, with arms connected, about a foot long, and making a chirping sound from below. Alarmed, she ran to tell her husband, who also went to look at it and was greatly puzzled. They dug into the ground, but its root could not be traced, so they broke it off and threw it into the lake. I once read in the *Records of Immortals and Their Encounters* that in the time of Xiao Jingzhi of Lanling, a man unearthed an object while digging, which resembled a human hand, was fleshy, moist, and red. After cooking and eating it, he regained his hair after a month, became strong, and appeared youthful. Later, he met a Taoist named Gu Jingzhi, who said, "With such spirit and energy, you must have taken elixir of immortality." The Taoist pointed to his pulse and said, "What you ate was meat fungus, which grants a lifespan as long as that of turtles and cranes." Thus, the object from Cao Lake was indeed of this kind, but it was unfortunately discarded by the hands of a foolish man. How regrettable!

○ Zheng Hao There was a man named Zheng Hao in the village who had once married a second wife. After a banquet, he lost a silver cup weighing several taels. His family included dozens of silk weavers and silk-drawing servants who wanted to prove their innocence, so they wrote a statement as an oath and threw it into the city god temple. Zheng Hao could not stop them and no longer searched for the cup. One day, Zheng Hao stood at the door, and after a short while, he entered the house. Suddenly, he fell to the ground. His family members supported him onto a bed. His limbs were cold, but his heart was slightly warm. They surrounded and guarded him until midnight when he woke up. He asked why he thought he had died, shaking his head and not answering. At dawn, he spoke: "At first, I saw a black-clad person rushing from the west, looking very fierce and evil. I thought the officials were pursuing someone, so I intended to enter and avoid them. The black-clad person reached the door, grabbed me, and said, 'I am here by order to summon you.' He then tied a rope around my neck and dragged me for several hundred steps to the city god temple. There was an elderly man in white clothes standing outside the door. He saw me and called my name. The black-clad person ordered the elderly man to guard me, rushed in to report, and then came out again to lead me inside. I knelt in the courtyard."

The god sat on the throne and sternly scolded and questioned me about the reason for the vow. I kowtowed and apologized, saying I did not know, but the god became even angrier and said, "Do you remember the matter of the lost silver cup? This cup was stolen by your grandson. How can you falsely accuse others, causing interference with the government?" I bowed twice and fully stated that it was not my own intention. The god called me forward and said, "Your grandson stole the cup to pawn for money at the silver shop east of your house. It is still placed on the shelf. Do you want to see it?" He looked at a soldier and ordered him to fetch the cup and show it to me. It was indeed a household item. After a long time, the god's anger slightly subsided and said, "I will temporarily release you. On the 26th day, I will issue an order to bring these people for interrogation." I continued to bow without stopping. Suddenly, I heard a voice from the hall saying, "Since there are many people, and we won't investigate further, just go and tell the others, so they know they have made a mistake." Therefore, I let you out, and you were saved. Immediately, someone was sent to the silversmith's house to check, and the cup was indeed on the shelf, pawned by his grandson. The others heard and were terrified, quickly going to the temple to apologize. They still felt anxious, and it took a month to feel secure. [Xu said.]

**Chiang Chih-hsiu** Chiang Chin, a censor in the capital Nanjing, was known for his integrity and courage. During the early years of the Zhengde era, he joined thirteen other officials in submitting a memorial to the emperor regarding current affairs. Late at night, as he was drafting the document by the lamp, he heard a rustling sound in the bamboo basket, which he interpreted as a ghostly presence. Chiang Chin thought to himself, "If I submit this memorial, I will likely bring about a great disaster. Could the one making this sound be the spirit of my ancestors, warning me to reconsider my actions?" He stood up and said, "If it is indeed my ancestors, why don't you speak up and warn me more clearly?" Before he finished speaking, the sound echoed through the walls. Chiang Chin sighed and said, "I have already committed myself to this cause. I cannot prioritize personal safety over my duty to the country. To remain silent and betray the nation would be a dishonor to my ancestors and a form of filial impiety." With determination, he wrote, "It's death, and nothing can change that." The sound then ceased. The memorial was submitted, and Chiang Chin and his colleagues were all arrested and subjected to severe punishment. While others sought medical treatment and care, Chiang Chin alone said, "I have found my place of death." He refused treatment and died as a result. The whole country mourned his death. Chiang Chin was from Changshu in the region of Wu and passed the imperial examination in 1496 during the Hongzhi era.

A Black Fish Liu Hao from Xiangcheng once took a daytime nap and dreamed of a black-clothed figure standing before him, followed by several white-clothed figures. They bowed and said, "We have lived here for forty or fifty years, but now we are captured by your family. Please show mercy and let us go." Awakening in shock, Liu Hao doubted the dream greatly. That night, a household servant who was fishing caught a large black fish weighing several dozen pounds, along with several smaller white fish, and presented them to Liu Hao. Realizing his previous dream, Liu Hao immediately kicked the fish into the water and released them.

Blue Tiger** Liu Han, the younger brother of Liu Hao, had never eaten vegetarian food in his life. One night, he dreamed of a tiger with deep blue fur that came to chase him. It bit him on the waist, causing him pain, and he woke up drenched in sweat. By morning, he saw five tooth marks on his waist, which were blue and swollen, and had bled, forming a wound. He then observed vegetarianism and performed rituals for three years, and his condition improved. [The above two stories are told by Liu Han's son Kai.] **Yellow Longzi** A small person from the fourteenth district of Changzhou named Huang Changzi suffered from a disease of the diaphragm. He could not eat or drink and did not feel hungry, and the condition worsened over several years. One day, he went to the Qi Gate to seek a doctor. On his way, he rested briefly at a suspension bridge. A hermit also came and sat on the bridge. The man, exhausted, vomited messily. The hermit pitied him and asked about his illness. The man described his condition, and the hermit said, "I can cure your illness." He poured a red pill from a gourd, as large as a mustard seed, and ordered the man to swallow it. A short while later, the man felt his chest and diaphragm very comfortable. He divided several cakes from the container he carried to thank the hermit, who accepted them. The hermit then patted the man's back, causing him to vomit the medicine again, which the hermit put back into the gourd and went away along the side of the bridge. When the man reached the doctor's house, he felt very hungry and asked for food. He looked into the container and saw that the cakes were still there, greatly surprised. From then on, his chronic illness suddenly improved. He thought the hermit was a deity, or at least a remarkable sorcerer. [The above story is told by the scholar Gu.]

### ○雄鸡卵 In the city of Jiading, a commoner named Chen Chang's rooster laid an egg, as large as a sparrow's egg. Chen Chang considered it very ominous, but there was no further incident. ### ○沈镗 Shen Tang, a person from Jiangdong, during his final illness, a person emerged from behind his buttocks, about an inch long, with both eyes, hands, feet, and limbs fully formed. A few days later, Shen Tang died. ### ○鸡精 Chen Yuan Shan, a resident of Suzhou's Loumen district, was a person of broad-minded character and had a particular interest in Daoist practices. He often engaged in rituals such as summoning spirits and was known by his Daoist name, Dongzhen. He frequently traveled to Jiading, where he was well-acquainted with the children of prominent families. Once, Chen Yuan Shan stayed at the home of the Tan family. They had a rooster that had lived for eighteen years. While talking with the host, the rooster flew from the courtyard to in front of Chen Yuan Shan, spread its wings and stretched its neck, and then died on the ground. One night, Chen Yuan Shan was staying in the study when a woman knocked on the door, smiled, and entered. She claimed to be the host's daughter and admired his broad-mindedness, so she came to be with him. Chen Yuan Shan saw that she was exquisitely beautiful. When he asked her age, she replied, "Eighteen years old." He then kept her with him for intimacy. From then on, the woman came to him every morning and left in the evening, and this continued for nearly a year. She occasionally mentioned that her life was associated with the rooster. Whenever she came, Chen Yuan Shan felt drowsy and dreamlike, but felt refreshed upon her departure. He told the Tan family about this, and the host was shocked and said, "My family has no such daughter, nor does any neighboring household. This must be a spirit or ghost."

Moreover, he said that she is eighteen years old and belongs to the Year of the Rooster. If we calculate by this year, her zodiac sign does not match, except for the rooster kept in my family, which is of this age. Could it be this? Chen used his skills, wrote talismans, and cast spells with water, intending to ward her off, but the woman came as usual. Someone secretly hid the talisman in their sleeves, and the woman always knew about it. She became angry and said, "You are doubting me!" She grabbed it with her hand and repeatedly hit it. When the talisman fell to the ground, she took it away. Someone taught him to place a copy of the *I Ching* in his stomach. When the woman arrived, she hit it multiple times, but it never fell, so she left. One night, he stayed with several friends at Wang Jia's place. They warned each other not to sleep, to see if she would come. In the middle of the night, everyone heard Yuan Shan's shouting and cursing. They got up to look and saw her body leaning on the bed, looking like a sexual position, and then he ejaculated on the bed. Yuan Shan woke up from his dream, and the others loudly chased her away. They saw a black ball on the ceiling making rooster sounds, flying out the window. They then invited a magician to set up a ritual, summoning officials to send her away. The woman saw Yuan Shan and apologized, saying, "Do not chase me; I will be reborn in Wuxi in a few days. You should not take me to Jingting; I fear the well god will take me, so you should take me to the wilderness." Following her words, they sent her to a desolate place outside the city with talismans, water, and offerings, and from then on, she was never seen again.

A Monk Named Ru Gong A monk from Jia Ding named Ru Gong once took a nap during the day and had a dream in which he arrived at a place about a mile north of Suzhou's Fengqiao Bridge. He crossed a wooden bridge and entered a house. The house had three tiled rooms, and the table was filled with food and drink. He sat in the middle seat. In front of him stood a woman, about forty years old, who bowed and wept. A few young people stood behind her. After a while, dumplings were brought in, and the woman took some paper money from the table and burned it on the ground. When he woke up, he felt full and had the smell of dumplings in his throat, finding it strange. Later, when he went to Fengqiao for business, he visited the place along the way. Upon arriving, he found that it was just as he had seen in his dream. Entering the house, the tables and chairs were arranged exactly as in his dream. A young man came out to greet him. When he inquired about the family matters, the young man said, "The father has died." The day of the father's death was the same day the monk had his dream. Thus, he realized that it was the time when the family was holding a memorial service. [The above four stories were told by my aunt's brother Xu Laifeng.]

The Wife of Dai Sees the Dead Child A man named Dai, a traveler from the village of Lu Mu in Changzhou, made a living by selling pottery. He was well-off enough to support himself and his family, but he only had one son, whom he loved deeply. He indulged the boy with fine clothing and lavish feasts, fulfilling all his desires. When the son was sixteen, he fell ill and lay bedridden for half a year. Despite medical treatments and prayers, all efforts to cure him were in vain, and the boy eventually died. The parents were heartbroken and lavishly buried their son, reciting scriptures and holding rituals. The expenses were enormous, depleting all their household belongings. They still thought of their son incessantly and cried all day long. One day, an old woman arrived by boat, tied it to the shore, and knocked on the door. Unable to bear the couple's sorrow, she said, "Life and death are natural laws; why grieve so much? However, your deep love for your son is hard to part with. If you think of your son, would you also want to see him again?" The couple, tears streaming down their faces, replied, "The departed person is forever lost in the darkness, and the boundary between the living and the dead is vast. How can there be a time to see him again? As you say, it is not what we dare to hope for." The old woman said, "If so, it is an easy matter." Surprised, the couple asked about her words. The old woman said, "I will lead you to a place where you will see him. You do not need to both go; one person can go." Dai was happy and immediately sent his wife to go with him. The old woman warned not to look around casually.

Rowing like the wind, they arrived in a short while at a bustling market where the residents were densely packed. The old woman guided her to climb up, and she saw her son standing in a rice shop, just using a measure to weigh rice for others. Seeing his mother come, he immediately rushed out to greet her, clearly very happy. The son said, "I am currently running this family's shop. I have been thinking of you and wanted to see you. Please stay here, mother. I will go inform the master of the house to come and greet you." He immediately rushed in, and the old woman invited the mother into the boat, covering it with a thick layer of bamboo matting. They drifted the boat in the middle of the stream, allowing the mother to secretly observe. Soon, the son came out, dressed in a frightening manner, looking like a fearsome bull-headed demon. He looked around and shouted, "Where is the old beast? He owes me a debt of twenty years, still owes me four years. Now that he's here, I'm just about to have someone arrest him. I regret not acting sooner, letting him escape." Entering with anger, the mother crouched in the boat, too afraid to breathe. The old woman asked, "Have you seen him?" They released the boat and returned to their original spot, where the mother described what she saw to her husband. From then on, her sorrow and longing subsided. Soon, the old woman's boat was also nowhere to be found. [This is the story from Wenling.]

The Black Tiger of the Dark Altar In the Wu region, there is a custom of enjoying cricket fighting, often deciding to gamble on property. A man named Zhang Tingfang from my village was fond of this pastime, but always lost, to the point of selling his furniture to repay his debts. This happened year after year, and he eventually squandered all his possessions. He had always respectfully served the dark altar god, so he prayed sincerely, recounting his hardships. That night, he dreamed that the god said to him, "Do not worry; I will send a black tiger to help you. It has now transformed into a form under the tree at the southeast corner of the Tianfei Palace. Go and retrieve it." Zhang went there, dug the soil, and obtained a cricket that was very dark and very large. Using it to fight, he never lost, and within ten days, he gained profits double what he had lost. By winter, the cricket died, and Zhang cried bitterly, making a silver coffin to bury it.

○Money Snake** Fengdu's Xiong Cun, my disciple far away, told me: In his village, there was a snake that appeared as a pest, no one knew where it came from, it was as big as a bowl, several zhang long, only biting young chickens and stealing food without harming people. People sought to kill it but could not. There was a vacant plot of land in a village temple, one person rented and cultivated it as a garden for many years. One day, while wielding a hoe to weed, he saw a huge snake winding its way, quickly swung the hoe to chop at it, the snake burrowed into a hole, only injuring its tail, and made a clanging sound like striking metal. Looking closely, there were thousands of scattered coins at the entrance of the hole. The person suspected that the snake had been transformed into money, called his wife and brother to dig together, about one zhang deep, found a vat of money, about several tens of thousands, carried it all back home, suddenly became a wealthy person. Since then, it was never seen again. **●Volume Fifth of Geng Si Bian** **Tales of the Supernatural** **Bamboo Woman** **Giant Clam

**Strange Stones** Official Longevity Office of Reporting Celestial Physician Ox Giving Birth to a Qilin Ling’s Dog Professor Xu Two Scholars of Jinhua ○ On Spirits The folk religion of Wu province venerates a spirit known as the Five Saints, also called the Five Manifestly Powerful Lords. In the countryside, they are called the Five-Lang God, and are said to be spirits of the deep mountains, ancient forest dwellers, or wind spirits. 【As stated in *Yijian Zhi*, they are also known as the One-Legged Wu Tong. I believe this refers to the figure described in the classics as "Kui, one-footed." In other regions, they are called "Xiao Gong," which takes its meaning from the term "mountain wind."】 All five spirits are addressed as Lords or Kings; their female counterparts are called Lady, and their mothers are called Grand Lady or simply "Great Mother." The people revere them greatly, building shrines in every home, adorned with five figures dressed like kings, and their wives dressed as empresses. The poor paint images of them on wooden boards and worship them, calling these boards "Holy Boards." When they offer sacrifices, they mix in the gods of Guanyin, the City God, and the Land God, and hold a separate rite for the horses, whom they consider the attendants of the spirits. At each ritual, they slaughter animals and set up music, while the shamans sing and chant, reciting the origins of the gods. It is said that the gods take pleasure in hearing these songs, and this is called a "Tea Feast." The most elaborate of these is called "Burning Paper" 【In the *Shuo Ku* edition, it is written as "Burning Large Paper."】. Even among the gentry, this is practiced, and the common people exhaust their resources to perform it, even taking loans to do so. For every matter, they must pray, and when they pray, they promise a Tea Feast, seeking divine protection. If they receive help, they attribute it to the gods; if misfortune befalls them, they blame themselves for lacking sincerity, and even in death, they dare not utter a word of complaint or blame.

There are those who, when suffering from illness, are accused by shamans and diviners of having offended the Five Sage Deities, and are warned not to take medicine. Foolish people believe this, and some refuse medical treatment, waiting for death. There are also old women who can perform rituals to calm fears and exorcise ghosts, claiming that these practices are secret teachings of the Five Sage Deities. These women often conspire with demons. The Western Lingga Mountain is a den of demons. The people of the mountain say that they often see torches appearing and disappearing in the lake, or see five men accompanied by attendants and concubines entering an ancient tomb or ruin, where they play music, hold banquets, and throw people to the ground, staying all night before dispersing, which they consider normal. Demons often cause mischief when people are in misfortune, moving beds, damaging doors, secretly stealing property, and even setting fires to people's houses. They have a penchant for seducing women, and those who involve themselves in evil or are destined to die young often suffer from their influence. Those affected faint as if drunk, and upon waking, they claim to have seen a nobleman in a tall hat and elegant clothing, with splendid attendants, in a palace as bright and magnificent as a king's residence. Women sit in rows and attendants stand by, numbering in the hundreds, all dressed in fine and beautiful attire. Between them, there is noisy music, and their clothing and use are extremely luxurious. During intercourse, there is something like a board covering them, which is as cold as water. Married women avoid sleeping with them, and if forced to lie beside their wives, they are always moved to the ground by the demons. Their magical, deceptive, and licentious evil is beyond description, and I have recorded more than ten such incidents here:

A scholar named Xu Qi's father once visited a temple, and a companion playfully doused a little child with water [Originally, "小鬼" was written as "小儿," but it was changed to "小鬼" in the library edition]. Upon returning home, a spirit pursued him, pounding on the door and scattering filth everywhere. The family did not know what kind of monster it was and called it a demon bandit. The spirit once took away a box of money, and when Xu Qi scolded it, the spirit threw the money down from the air, scattering it in the courtyard, where the coins were still hot. Small paper figures were placed in the gaps of the windows, all with complete facial features, and sometimes a hand appeared through the window, with fingers bright red like fire. They heard footsteps and spread sand on the ground to verify the tracks, which were several dozen in number, all about a foot long.

Medical student Chen saw a hand appearing on the beam during the day, dripping blood to the ground. Just at mealtime, there was a person with a face like a cartwheel, big hairy hands, grabbing and taking away things, eating behind the bed, and making chomping sounds. Scholar Shen Liu's sister-in-law died due to disappointment. After death, she saw strange phenomena and claimed to be under the command of five sages, disturbing her family day and night. A gong moved on its own and struck, stacked a hundred steps without falling. Two rope nets hung in the air, with rope as thin as human hair, storing two bowls of water that did not leak when shaken. Several houses more than ten rooms were burned. She was disturbed for many years in this way. In the household of Scholar Zha, there were two trees in the temple where offerings were made, which were occasionally cut down for other uses. The ghost became angry and did great evil, fire starting everywhere. When extinguished, it moved away, but not burned. The clay figurines in the temple all rose and moved on their own, climbed onto the house and squatted, looking like living beings, and finally destroyed his house and then stopped.

Hóng, known for his strict demeanor, once saw a monk with loose robes and wide sleeves, slowly walking on the roof, treading on tiles and making a sound like "la." He rushed to chase after the monk, but it suddenly disappeared. In the pot used for cooking rice, everything turned into mud. The Taoist priest Zou Yingbi set up an altar to investigate and punish the ghost, swearing not to accept bribes and dismissing the ghost, and the ghost then left. Se Sheng's wife, Lady Lü, was a woman of a distinguished family, known for her exceptional beauty and grace, and was nineteen years old. She suddenly fainted and died, but after two days, she revived and said: "I was summoned by the Five Saints and the Spiritual Public to a feast, where they showed me a chest of golden jewelry and sixteen chests of clothes, all dazzling and beautiful, though small in design. The deity said to me, 'If you can stay here, all these things will be yours.' I wept and bowed to request to return, and the lady persuaded me, so they let me go, saying, 'I will allow you for ten years.' [The original text does not have the character '云' (yún), but it is said that the library edition adds it.] After that, the ghost frequently came to their home, calling the wife 'young mistress.' She occasionally heard a strange fragrance that filled her nose, and a handsome man in fine clothes would come, and they would sleep together. After ten years, she died again, but soon revived, saying the deity had told her, "I will give you one more year." Before and after, she gave birth to five sons, and when pregnant, she would see a man carrying a child and leaving it. At the time of childbirth, there was no blood, only black fluid, and the child was extremely beautiful. When the child was one year old, she said, "I will now take the child away." Like this, the children died young. Finally, she gave birth to a daughter, just delivered herself, blood rushed upwards, and she died. From the time of her previous revival, exactly one year later.

A man named Xia married a woman named Li, who was a descendant of Si Tu Bo Sheng from the false Wu state. On the day of her wedding, as she stepped down from the carriage, she suddenly began to dance wildly and shout, calling herself the Five Saints. The family, fearing for her, hastily prepared an offering. The woman rushed out of her room, singing praises as if she were a witch. At the altar, there were dozens of wine cups, and she walked on them as if flying, without spilling a drop. At times, she would cut herself with a knife, yet remained unharmed. This woman still comes to my house, and her spirit is already mad. A woman named Zhang, dressed in red, passed by the shrine and went mad. She danced and sang all night with the doors closed. Later, she married a scholar named Zhu Yu, and the spirit followed her. Zhu Yu's mother was originally a concubine, and whenever the woman saw her, she would scold, saying, "Old maid, old maid." In her responses, she spoke entirely in the dialect of the capital city. [Originally, "尽" was written as "盖," but it was corrected in later editions.]

Se Ning's wife, who was in her thirties, was slightly attractive. She often saw hundreds of torches arranged in the air, and a person dressed in a red robe and a three-peaked hat descending from the sky. The lights in the hall went out, and they engaged in sexual activity. Afterward, they ate and drank and left. Gold, silk, hairpins, and earrings came as desired. Her husband, benefiting from these gains, invoked the deity to bring them here, thus becoming wealthy. Chen Wu had a virtuous daughter who was seventeen years old and was about to be married. She was possessed by a demon, who said, "I am the third among the five saints, and I have a connection with your daughter, so I have come." He gave her the name Wu Bao Nü. From then on, the girl could predict people's fortunes and misfortunes. Those who had illnesses or lost items consulted her, and her predictions were often remarkably accurate. Chen painted images of the five saints and placed them in the hall. After a long time, the demon became tired and left, and the girl is still unmarried. A person named An Song, who lived near my house, had a sister named Liu Fu. The girl said that there was a black person who looked like a servant. Every time she slept, he would come to have intercourse with her. She often dreamed of following him to his home, where she saw luxurious furnishings and a grand house, much like what was described earlier. One day, while she was wandering in the hall, a nobleman suddenly appeared and shouted at the black person to come out. The person seemed frightened. The nobleman saw him, called him to kneel, and scolded him, saying, "I used infinite wealth to do things, but you stole my name and acted recklessly outside." He was furious and angry, and the black person bowed his head in fear and did not dare to respond. The nobleman then sent the girl back, and the black person did not return. It was likely a lesser spirit or ghost.

Generally speaking, ghosts and spirits are caused by people. Today, the common people admire and follow in this way, the evil and delusional atmosphere mutually calls to each other, it is appropriate that they gather for a long time and do not disperse, acting wildly and recklessly. The former governor of Xincai County, Cao Gong, once strictly enforced prohibitions, burning down all their shrines and statues without leaving any. After he left office, they began to slowly reappear, and soon everything returned to its former state. If those who come later can revive his good policies, to make the ghosts and spirits dissipate, it would indeed be a great pleasure. ○ Banana Leaf Woman Feng Hanzi Tianzhang was a student from Wu, living in a small study in the Stone Gate Lane of the West Gate. In front of the courtyard, he planted various flowers and trees, creating a refreshing and lovely setting. One summer evening, after bathing, he sat on a couch in his study when he suddenly saw a woman in green clothes and emerald skirts, standing by the window. Han shouted and asked her, "Who are you?" The woman bowed and said, "I am Jiao." After she finished speaking, she suddenly entered the house. Upon closer inspection, her skin was fresh and beautiful, her movements light and graceful, truly a stunning beauty. Han was surprised and doubted that she was not human. He got up to grab her clothes, but the woman hurriedly tore them and ran away. He only managed to grab a corner of her skirt, which he placed under the mat where he slept. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a banana leaf. Previously, Han had once studied in a nearby temple and moved a banana plant to his courtyard. The place where the leaf was torn, he took the one he had kept and matched it perfectly, without any difference in size. He then cut it down, and when he cut its root, there was blood. Later, he asked the monk, who said, "The banana plant has caused many strange occurrences, and several monks have died because of it." [According to the monk's account.]

### 巨蚌 My family lives by the shores of Tanchu Lake. There is a stream that flows from the north of Jaitan Lake, reaching the tomb of Han Yongxi, the Governor-General, and forms a large pond, deep and unfathomable. In the pond, there is an old clam as large as a boat. One October, the clam opened its mouth on the beach. A woman was washing clothes and thought it was a sunken ship, so she stepped on it with one foot. The clam quickly closed its mouth and sank, splashing water onto her face, cold as ice. The woman was startled and fell down. Once, a dragon descended to play with its pearl, fighting with the clam for an entire day. The wind and waves grew fierce, and the dragon lifted the clam several zhang high and then dropped it, but eventually could not overcome it and left. In the seventh year of the Jingta period, the lake water merged completely, and the clam emerged from the southwest of the lake, causing the ice to break apart and push and gather the sides like accumulated snow. From then on, it never returned. ### 怪石 My family's villa near Maple Bridge has a harbor that connects to the canal. There is a square piece of blue stone, about four or five feet long, that was washed up here. After many years, it became a wonder. Every autumn, it could move out of the river on its own, and whenever it emerged, there was a risk of capsizing boats. One year, a timber merchant moored his raft at the harbor and passed beneath it. The wood was lifted up a foot or so, and the merchant was greatly startled. There was also a report of a grain boat capsizing. After a short while, the stone re-entered from outside, and the wood rose as before. It is still in the water, continuing to be a wonder.

○官寿 A local man named Guo had a son named Guan Shou, who died at the age of several years. Guo and his wife grieved deeply for him, and at the time of burial, they wrote his name on his back with ink. According to custom, this was done in the hope that he could be recognized in his next life. The following year, he had another son, whose back also bore the characters "Guan Shou," clearly visible. People all said the child had been reborn. ○见报司 Wu Xuesheng Jixian was a man of great integrity, straightforwardness, and keen intelligence. Last year, he was a guest at the home of a local man named Han Xiang. One day at noon, he leaned against a desk and dozed off. Vaguely, he saw two attendants come in from outside and say to him, "We are here to invite you." He got up and followed them to the door, where there were carriages and horses, with attendants and guards, all resplendent and filling the road. Soon, someone came in carrying a red robe and a gold belt. He put it on and climbed into the carriage, with attendants shouting ahead of him. They arrived at a large official building, where several officials in gold and purple robes came out to greet him, and they entered bowing to each other. They sat him down in the hall and said to him, "There is a vacancy here, and we have been waiting for you for a long time." They then urged him to go quickly, and he was sent out the door before he woke up. It turned out he had just bent his elbow on the desk and fallen asleep, but he was very surprised by it.

That night, he felt unwell, returned home, and lay ill, never to recover. As he was dying, he said, "I am now going to the East Peak to serve as an official of the Reporting Office." Several days later, his wife dreamed that he came home first, dressed in official attire like a high-ranking official. He told her, "I am at the Reporting Office, where the duties are very numerous and arduous. Thanks to a fellow townsman, Mr. Jin from Changshu, who serves as a colleague, assisting with official documents and is very helpful. Please convey my thanks to his family." His wife relayed his words to her family. Subsequently, Mr. Jin sent someone to visit the family, saying, "Our master, Mr. Jin, a student from Changshu, died of illness this year. At the time of his death, he said he became an official of the underworld and serves together with Mr. Ji from Suzhou." All of what he said matched what Mrs. Ji had dreamed, and they finally believed it was true. From then on, the two families became close friends and visited each other frequently. [The above two stories are told by Ma Qi.]

**Heavenly Physician** A local man named Gu Qian Chunji, in the second month of the fifth year of the Hongzhi era (1489), contracted a severe case of typhoid fever. He sought treatment from the physician Du Xiang, but after seven days, his condition worsened with increasing confusion. That night, he dreamed of an old man who said to him, "You have been misled by Doctor Du. If you do not quickly seek another physician, you will die." Gu Qian asked who he should consult, and the old man replied, "The physician Liu Zongxu from Fongmen is excellent." Startled awake, Gu Qian immediately went to see Liu Zongxu, took his medicine, and his illness began to improve slightly. At midnight, he got up to eat porridge when he raised his head and saw a man wearing a golden crown and a green robe sitting cross-legged on the beam above. In the room hung hundreds of medicinal gourds. The man called out to Gu Qian by name, "Do you know me? I am the Heavenly Physician." He then explained in detail the cause of Gu Qian's illness, and all his words were logical and profound. He also gave him hundreds of words of advice, saying, "If you can follow this, you can become a famous physician. Remember it well and do not forget." After speaking, he disappeared, and from that day on, Gu Qian's condition improved rapidly, though he suffered from deafness. By winter, he went to visit the physician Ling Hanzhang from Huzhou to seek acupuncture treatment. Ling Hanzhang performed acupuncture on both of his ears, and after some time, he recovered. Ling Hanzhang then said to him, "Have you ever been treated by a Heavenly Physician who gave medicine to your ears?" Gu Qian was startled and asked how he knew this. Ling Hanzhang replied, "Generally, when a Heavenly Physician treats a disease, they transmit medicine into the ear. The medicine enters and blocks the air, thus causing deafness." Gu Qian then explained what he had seen, saying, "Sir, you are a divine being." However, after his illness, Gu Qian carefully recalled the conversation with the divine being and found that all the words were clear and distinct, except for the essential words given, which he could not remember a single one, and he regretted this to this day. Ling Hanzhang, from Huzhou, was renowned for his divine acupuncture skills, and the details should be recorded separately. [Gu Qian himself said this.]

#### Ox Giving Birth to a Qilin In the village of Wu Xiang in Changzhou, a commoner named Zhuang Menghe made his living by grinding flour. During the Hongzhi era, one of his female cows gave birth to a creature that resembled a deer, with scales all over its body like a qilin. The creature leaped about uncontrollably and ate a hoe leaning against the cowshed wall. Zhuang was greatly disturbed by this strange occurrence and did not know how to care for it. The creature died of starvation in three days. Some people thought it was a qilin. [Narration by Lu Yun'ang.] #### Ling Family Dog In the home of the grain chief in Fuli, there was a white dog that had been kept for several years. The dog was very strong and well-trained. Previously, a tenant farmer had died owing several stones of grain. One night, the farmer suddenly appeared in a dream to his son and said, "I was born owing the Ling family grain, so I was reborn in their home as a white dog to repay the debt. Now there is still a small amount left. You should return it and use money to redeem me back." The son followed his words and brought the grain to pay, then asked to buy the dog, but was not allowed. So he told them about the dream. The family did not believe him. The dog had already jumped into the boat and sat down, refusing to get up. The Ling family was amazed and allowed him to take it home, refusing payment. [Narration by Sheng Zhi Rong, the brother-in-law of the narrator.]

In Zhenjiang, there was a retired official named Xu who lived at home and supported himself by teaching students. Two brothers from the Yan family came to study with him. The elder brother was named Jiang, and the younger brother was named Hai. They claimed that their family lived by the banks of the Yangtze River and presented a generous offering. They returned home every ten days or so. After three months, they had studied all the classic texts somewhat thoroughly. As they were about to return home, they asked their teacher, "Tomorrow, our grandfather will prepare a cup of wine to celebrate your longevity. Could you grace us with your presence?" The teacher agreed, and the two brothers left to return home. The next day, they brought a servant and a horse for the teacher to ride. They said, "The horse is quite wild and unrestrained. Whenever it sees someone open their eyes, it will kick or bite. Please close your eyes for a short time." The teacher followed their instructions. He heard the wind whistling, and the horse galloped very fast. After a short while, they said, "We have arrived." The teacher was helped off the horse, and they entered the house. They saw a grand courtyard that looked like the residence of a king. Soon, they heard the sounds of whips and commands. From a distance, they saw a person in elaborate and magnificent attire sitting at a desk and handling official business. The person appeared to be in his early forties. The atmosphere was strict and solemn, with guards and many people bound in chains and shackles, numbering about a hundred. The teacher was very surprised. The two brothers led the teacher through a small side door to the back hall.

A lavish banquet was set up, and an elderly man, clad in a square headpiece and black robe, came forth with his staff. The two youths said, "This is the patriarch of the house." The old man bowed before him and said, "My two grandsons have long benefited from your guidance and kindness, yet I have no way to repay your virtue. Today I have prepared this modest banquet to invite you, but my son has urgent official business and could not personally receive you. I have therefore sent my old man to welcome you, which is indeed a breach of proper etiquette." Afterward, he took his seat at the table, and the food and dishes were all exceptionally delicious. By evening, after the drinking had ended, two attendants carried a jade tray and placed within it gold, silver, silk, and brocade as a gift. The official refused it many times before finally accepting it. Then he took his leave, and the old man saw him off to the middle gate, where they parted. He ordered the two youths to escort the official, and they left through a different gate. On the way, he passed a room and saw someone bound to a tree. Upon closer inspection, it was his relative by marriage. Surprised, he asked why this was so. The bound man replied, "I have been bound by the master because of my crime. I heard you were at the banquet, and I hoped you would speak on my behalf." The official pointed to the two youths and said, "This is my relative-in-law. I do not know what crime he has committed against your esteemed father, but I would be grateful if you would speak in his favor and show him mercy." The two youths replied with polite assent, and then asked the official to proceed ahead. The official gave them a few parting words, mounted his horse, and departed. As he slowly made his way home, he felt a sense of unease and suspicion. The next morning, he went to visit his relative-in-law, who was just then in a critical condition. Seeing the official, the man thanked him, saying, "You truly saved my life. I died suddenly yesterday and appeared before Lord Yama, where I was bound to a tree and was about to be cast into prison. It was thanks to you that my two sons spoke on my behalf, and so I was released and returned." The official was greatly shocked, and only then realized that the two youths were the sons of Lord Yama, and that the place he had visited was the realm of the dead. After this, the two youths never came again.

Two Scholars from Jinhua During the Hongzhi era, there were two scholars from Jinhua who traveled to take the imperial examination at the Ministry of Rites but failed to pass. They boarded a boat to return home. There were four men who resembled public servants and also traveled on the same boat. Whenever they passed through a town, they would go ashore for a long time, return drunk and full, and then fall asleep, leaving no one to guess who they were. When they reached Shandong, the two scholars privately thought that their suspicious and mysterious behavior must indicate that they were thieves. Zhang took advantage of their quiet sitting and suddenly entered to grab them. The four men were reading a document together when they saw Zhang enter, but they did not seem surprised. Zhang asked to see the document, and they showed it to him. The content of the document was similar to official documents issued by the government, listing over a hundred people to be pursued, including the two scholars. Zhang was greatly shocked and exclaimed, "Who are you? What official document is this, and why do my names appear on it?" The four men were startled and asked, "Whose names are these?" Zhang pointed to them, and the four men looked at each other and said, "We have made a big mistake." They then told Zhang, "We are actually messengers from Fengdu, just now received orders from Lord Yama to pursue this group of people. Unintentionally, we were discovered, and you are also destined." Upon hearing this, Zhang became even more terrified and knelt down to beg for mercy.

Four men initially refused, but Zhang persisted in pleading with them. The four men said, "If you truly have the heart to seek help, we have traveled together for so long, how could we be heartless? Now we have a plan to resolve this crisis, but only the two of you should know it. Do not tell even your wives and children, for if it leaks, the matter will fail." Zhang asked for the plan, and they said, "When you return, on a certain day drive your family out of the house, set up the thrones of the gods of the Three Realms in the hall, with a chair and a rope ready, and we will come, and when we arrive, we will explain." They then listed the names of the gods, instructing Zhang to buy paper horses, which Zhang wrote down one by one. They warned him again not to reveal the plan and then boarded a boat and left. Zhang told Wang about it, but Wang did not believe and laughed at it. Zhang was filled with doubt and fear, so he finally boarded another boat and hurriedly returned home. On the appointed day, he pretended to have other business and sent his family out. He set up the thrones and the chair and rope as instructed, locked the door, and sat alone waiting. Soon, four emissaries descended from the sky, saw Zhang, and said, "You truly have faith." They bowed before the gods and knelt to present their words, but it is unknown what they said. They then tied Zhang to the chair with the rope and whipped him a hundred times. After untied him, they said, "You are now safe." Zhang prostrated himself in gratitude, and suddenly, they disappeared. Wang Sheng died that very night. [These two stories were told by Yan Zhixian, the magistrate of Jinhua.]

**Volume Sixth of the Jīngsì Biān** Xu Wugong** **Zhang Daoist Priest** **A Woman Giving Birth to a Snake** **River God's Fish Head** **Zhao Gong's Wife** **Spirit Ship** **Ghost Returning Home** **Ox Repaying the Debt of Money** **Wang Duanyi Gong** **Yue Buming Shrine** **Frenzied Dog** **Verification of the Nine Immortals' Dream** **Divine Pill** **Parrot Mountain Tea **Chapter on Xu Wugong** Xu Wugong, the Marquis of Wugong, was a genius unparalleled in his time. His knowledge spanned astronomy, geography, and the teachings of Buddhism and Taoism, encompassing all fields of learning. Several months before the disaster of the Jīsì year, the planet Mars entered the Southern Dipper. Privately, Xu Wugong confided in Liu Yuánbó, who was also skilled in divination. Liu Yuánbó replied, "I know this as well. If the state is fortunate, the emperor may recover from illness and thus avert the punishment of the time." After a long while, Mars did not retreat. Xu Wugong said, "Disaster is not far off." He immediately ordered his wife and children to return south. They were reluctant to move, showing signs of distress. Xu Wugong became angry and said, "If you do not leave quickly, you are merely wishing to become the wife of a Tartar [the original character '达' has been changed to '鞑']!" They then set out. By the time they had passed several stations near Linqing, news of the collapse of the Tǔmù campaign reached them.

After gaining control of the state through the emperor, he was determined to achieve great accomplishments, yet he was often unhappy in his position. He would often say to his close associates, "The planet Mars is very urgent; I will wait until it retreats slightly, then I can act." Soon after, he was ultimately suppressed by Cao and Shi and was never able to fulfill his ambitions and retire. In the lunar month of July in the year 1451 (Xin Si year of the Tian Shun era), the official lived in his hometown. One day, he told his guests, "Have you seen the celestial phenomena? The disaster caused by eunuchs is about to occur! I was陷害 by Jixiang, and now he is suffering a worse fate than I did." Within a few days, Jixiang's son Qin was executed. In the spring of the year 1454 (Jia Shen year), Mao Ling succeeded to the throne. The official predicted the course of events and foresaw that he would have 24 years. A relative resented the official for other reasons and was about to reveal his prediction, but the official apologized and managed to avoid the consequences. When the Chenghua era began in 1465, and counting from the year of succession, it was exactly 24 years. In the year 1461 (Xin Mao year), accompanied by the prefect Lin, he entered the prefectural school and pointed to the roof ornament of the Great Hall of Confucius, saying, "There is a green vapor reaching high into the sky; this is a sign of cultural prosperity. Will there be a scholar from Wu who will become the top scholar in the next year?" The following year, Wu Wending passed the imperial examination.

The subject highly esteemed Wen Ding, even during his time out of office, predicting a period of great influence. He once told his guests, "After Wu Jun entered the cabinet, many significant events began to unfold." By the end of the Hongzhi era, Wen Ding became a high-ranking official, and half a year after his death, Tai Ling was completed. Shortly thereafter, the eunuchs gained power, leading to significant changes in the political landscape. This was followed by disturbances in the Huangchi region, causing unrest in both the court and the country that lasted for many years. Initially, the subject was imprisoned under imperial decree and looked at himself in the mirror, saying, "My face is pale and dispirited; I am certainly not spared." He then daily bowed his hands and silently recited the spell he recited to the Mother of the Great Dipper. After several days, he looked in the mirror again and said, "I now know I am spared." As the trial was about to be decided, there was a great wind and thunder, and a disaster occurred at the Gate of Heaven. In the midst of darkness, someone saw seven objects like pigs squatting on the brocade hall, which must have been done by the star gods. The subject devotedly served the North Star, bowing northward forty-nine times daily, without interruption even in cold and heat.

The master did not eat pork and kept his secret technique to himself, not easily revealing it to others. Shen, a reclusive scholar named Zhou, was favored by the master. Once, during a private meeting, Zhou calmly asked the master for his secret technique. The master smiled and said, "Do you wish to try it?" Looking at the dog lying in the courtyard, he took a hair ring from his arm, placed it on his arm, and rotated his finger to the left. The dog seemed to be strangled or tied up, twisting and turning as if about to die. Then, he rotated his finger to the right, and the dog lay calmly and peacefully as before. The deputy magistrate of Changzhou, Xue, had his ancestral tomb at Yiting. The master passed by in a boat and pointed to the place, saying to someone, "This place should produce someone who will wear a golden belt." At that time, Xue had not yet achieved prominence, but later he passed the imperial examination and is still in office. The students at the Jinchishui military academy rarely became famous. The master was exiled and lived there, observing the place and said, "To plant trees to the west as a screen will be beneficial." The authorities followed his suggestion, and the success in the imperial examinations became abundant from then on. There were many other instances of skillful and accurate predictions, but they cannot all be remembered.

Zhang Bixu the Daoist Zhang Bixu, a Daoist from the village of Shatou in Taicang, had traveled widely in his youth and acquired unusual skills. In the village where he lived, there was a scholar who taught in the village. Once, five men docked their boat at the scholar's door, dressed in the attire of wealthy and aristocratic young men. They invited the scholar aboard their boat and provided a lavish feast, and the scholar reciprocated with a meal as a gesture of gratitude. From then on, the five men became regular visitors, always inviting the scholar to drink, which gradually led to significant expenses for the scholar. The expenses became so great that the scholar could no longer afford them, leading him to sell his clothes to continue the hospitality. The scholar's courtyard was filled with bottles and jars of wine, and his family suffered greatly but could not distance themselves from the visitors due to social obligations or fear. Neighbors were curious and inquired about the identities of the five men, but the scholar claimed he did not know them, suggesting they were supernatural beings, and recommended summoning Zhang Bixu, a skilled exorcist. The scholar sent someone to invite Zhang Bixu. Zhang first instructed the scholar's family to welcome and worship the image of Wang Lingguan, a deity, in their home, which was then taken by the supernatural beings. Zhang then hung the image of Zhenwu, another deity, and attempted to confront the beings with his talisman and staff, but was again captured and placed on the beam. Zhang became angry and prepared his own incense, paper, and talisman to confront the beings, performing rituals for several days until the captured items fell from the beam.

Zhang still used the imitation books written by the students of the Confucian school to wrap the gift. Zhang was pleased and said, "This plan has come to an end." Soon after, a group of children from his family ran in and reported, "There are hundreds of ghosts with red hair and blue skin, fierce and evil in appearance, wandering cautiously on the field." Another report came that "A general in a red helmet, accompanied by hundreds of followers, all dressed in red, stood on the field, directing the red-clad men to seize each ghost and throw them into the wine bottles. The ghosts wandered aimlessly and struggled, creating a very turbulent scene." Zhang knew that the general was the Spirit of the Spirit Official, so he instructed the children to watch as each ghost was thrown into a bottle. Whenever a ghost was captured, the children would bring the bottle, write a magical symbol on it, seal it, and throw it into the water, where it would sink. When all the bottles were thrown into the water, the ghosts were gone, and the general and his followers disappeared at once. Zhang then set up an offering to thank the general. Before the offering was completed, Zhang's eldest son suddenly went missing. They searched everywhere but could not find him. After several days, he returned. When asked about his experience, he said, "I was seized by five people and thrown into a boat. I felt disoriented and wandered for more than a hundred miles. Suddenly, I found myself on the shore, as if waking from a dream, in the area near Wu Mountain in Suzhou. I asked the locals for directions and returned home." Wu Mountain is close to Lengya, and it is suspected that the five ghosts were the Five Passes.

A Woman Giving Birth to a Snake There is a snake temple on a small hill outside the city walls of Loumen. Before the temple, there was a servant named Gong Cigu, whose daughter-in-law once visited the temple and was deeply moved by the image of the deity. Upon returning home, she became confused and dizzy, as if drunk, and from then on, she no longer slept with her husband. She often saw the deity come to her and be intimate with her. After a year, her belly swelled as if she were pregnant, and at the due time, she gave birth to a dozen small snakes, filling a tiger's cub. Gong Cigu realized this was the work of the snake deity, so he rushed to the temple and smashed the deity's image and the sacrificial altar. He then returned and invited a doctor to treat her daughter-in-law. After a long time, she recovered. [The above two stories are said by Deng Kai.]

#### The Fish-Head River God The maternal relatives of Zhao Gong, who lived by the river, were trading in the eastern bank of the Yangtze River, carrying goods downstream. In midstream, the boat suddenly started leaking. In a hurry, the heavy goods could not be moved, and the boatmen were terrified and had no plan, only praying to the river god for help. Soon, the water stopped entering, and the boat sailed safely to its destination. It turned out to be a fish head. The divine craftsmanship was indeed remarkable. #### Zhao Gong’s Wife Zhao Gong of Changzhou Shahu had a favorite concubine. The principal wife was very jealous and did not allow her to see him at night. Often during the day, she took advantage of the opportunity to have secret meetings. Later, she became pregnant and gave birth to a son with a short fleshy horn on his head, whose face was blue and whose cry sounded like a ghost. They found him repulsive and killed him. All three births were the same. According to the *Yueling* (Monthly Ordinances): "In February, thunder begins to sound. Those who do not restrain their behavior will give birth to children who are not complete." The explanation is that "behavior" refers to sexual intercourse, which is considered profaning the heavenly mandate, so the children born will have physical defects. Nowadays, people have sexual intercourse under the sun, moon, and thunder, and the children they produce often have strange physical appearances. Such as in the case of Zhao Gong’s concubine, many people attribute this to supernatural calamities, or believe it is caused by their profession. This is probably because they have not fully understood the reason behind it.

○ Divine Ship Yangshan Huirao said: A neighbor of his, a commoner, went to the capital on some business and returned to Zhangjiawan to board a ship. It was just dawn when he saw a very large ship in the river, with a nobleman dressed in fine robes sitting inside, accompanied by about ten attendants. The commoner hurried to bow before the ship and asked to be taken to Suzhou, seeking passage. The nobleman said, "Our ship has already arrived in Suzhou." He immediately ordered the commoner to be taken aboard. The commoner sat at the stern of the ship. After a long time, he felt tired and took off his straw sandals, placing them beside him, and used his clothes as a pillow to fall asleep. He did not realize he had fallen into a deep sleep. When he woke up and opened his eyes, he saw himself lying in the grass, with his clothes still under his head, but his sandals were gone. Startled, he looked around and noticed that the sun had not yet reached the late afternoon. He walked out onto the official road and asked someone, "Where am I?" The person replied, "At the Maple Bridge." He was even more terrified and followed the path to the Chuan Gate, entering a temple to rest briefly. He raised his head and saw a divine image, which looked exactly like the nobleman from the boat. On the side of the temple, there was a ship, not much different from the one he had seen before, but it was smaller, and the bottom of the ship and the oars were wet. He reached out to the back of the ship and found his sandals there. Shaking with fear, he bowed down and asked the soothsayer, who said, "This is the Temple of Song Xiang Gong."

A Ghost Returns Home A wealthy man from Wu had been dead for more than a year. After his burial, his son went to the tomb on Qingming Festival to offer sacrifices. While he was mourning, a voice from the tomb suddenly responded, saying, "You need not grieve so deeply; I am returning with you now." The son, overwhelmed with sorrow and longing, no longer felt afraid. He called out to the voice, and the ghost began to recount his life story in great detail. When the son returned home, he heard a voice in the hall, which was his father's, confirming that his father had returned. The son called out to his wife and daughters, who came out to greet him warmly and intimately, just as they would have done when he was alive. His wife asked, "Have you been gone for so long, and do you still want to eat?" The ghost replied, "Very good." They then placed chicken on the table. Although the ghost was not visible, the food was quickly consumed. By evening, the ghost said, "I must return now. Send a servant to accompany me."
