The servant took the ghost back to the tomb, where the ghost instructed, "I will return on a certain day. You can wait for me in advance." When the day arrived, the ghost returned as promised. From then on, the ghost would come and go each day, gradually managing household affairs with great order. Whenever the family engaged in buying and selling goods, merchants would arrive, and the ghost would negotiate and conduct transactions. Initially, they found this strange, but they eventually accepted it. The ghost was afraid of dogs, and the servant always drove the dogs away to keep them from approaching. One night, as the ghost was about to leave, there was no one to send him. He was then bitten by a group of dogs, shouted, climbed a tree, and disappeared. After that, the ghost never returned again.

A Cow Repays a Debt A farmer from Yangshan had been raising a cow for two years; it was strong and good at plowing. One evening, the cow suddenly disappeared. The farmer went out to look for it but could not find it. He arrived at the edge of a field and saw a man in black standing in the water. The farmer asked, "Have you seen my cow?" The man replied, "I am the cow. I owe you money and have agreed to work for two years to repay it. Now the time is up, and I will go to Mr. Huo Qing's house in the western mountains. You go there, get 5,000 coins, and you can sell me." The farmer, hearing this, was greatly startled and ran away. Later, he looked back, and it was a cow again. He called his family and went together to bind it and bring it back. The next day, they took it to Mr. Huo Qing's house to sell it. Mr. Huo Qing saw it and gladly agreed to buy it. The price was exactly 5,000 coins. [These two stories are also told by Hui Yao.]

Wang Danyi of San Yuan Wang Danyi of San Yuan, known for his clear loyalty and firm integrity, bore the heavy expectations of the world and was the foremost among the famous officials of recent times. He retired at the age of seventy-eight and passed away at the age of ninety-three. On the day of his death, after moving to the main chamber, he warned his family, saying, "My breath is about to cease, and there will surely be wind and thunder surrounding my residence. You all should be cautious and not cry, but wait quietly." When the master was just closing his eyes, a short while later, thunder roared, the wind blew fiercely, and rain poured down. The family members warned each other to hold their breath. After a long time, the sky cleared, and only then dared they mourn. When he was dressed in his shroud, his appearance was still as if he were alive. This occurred on the twenty-first day of the fourth month of the year Wu Chen during the Zhengde era. I have heard that when Mr. Xue Wenching of Hejin passed away, there were also unusual phenomena of wind and thunder, with white vapor filling the sky, and it took a long time to dissipate. Righteous and virtuous people, whose spirits resonate with the heavens and earth, are indeed like this.

Yue Wumu Temple in Tangyin The Temple of Yue Wumu, where he is worshipped in Tangyin, is his birthplace. In the third year of Hongzhi (1486), my uncle, the Gongbao, who was an inspector of justice touring Henan, was nearing the end of his term. On his journey to the county, he passed the temple and saw a stone inscription on the wall with the four large characters "Loyalty and Service to the Country," each about four feet in diameter. He intended to pay his respects. That night, he stayed at the inspection office and dreamed of entering the temple to pay respects. The god stood up to greet him, and they engaged in a long, friendly conversation. The god said, "When I was relieved of military command, I enjoyed the scenery of West Lake, finding great pleasure in the mountains and waters, but it was too short-lived." The official asked, "Historical records say that Wang was plotted against by Qin Hui, is this true?" The god replied, "Indeed, but the one who caused harm was Zhang Xiyou." The god then requested of the official, "I rest here, but the buildings are dilapidated. Please renovate them." The official declined, saying his position was not to govern the land, and he would soon be replaced, making it difficult to manage. The god said, "Only your word to the governor and the inspector is needed." The official agreed, looking at the god's eyes and nose, which had four wounds, bowed, and left, then woke up. In the morning, he went to pay respects to the god's statue, which was similar to what he saw in the dream. The temple buildings were leaking, and the god's face was eroded by heavy rain, with four places damaged. The official found this strange. After reviewing the county treasury, he found surplus silver of eighty taels and entrusted it to Deputy Inspector Baoyu. He also wrote to Governor Chen, a censor-general, to renovate the temple. The temple was completed, twice as grand and spacious as before. [The official personally told this to his uncle, a senior official in the Ministry of Works.]

### 瘈狗 Once I heard that a rabid dog biting a person could cause a puppy to grow inside the person's abdomen, leading to death without giving birth. I found this quite doubtful. Recently, there was a dog belonging to the Zhou family near the Cross Bridge. One day, the dog was licking the ground, suddenly went mad, and started rushing around biting people. The Zhou family had a stepson who was fifteen years old. He was bitten and died. After his death, his body was burned, and it was found that his entire abdomen was filled with mud. The dog also died. When its abdomen was cut open, there was a bundle of a small snake wrapped in mud, about the size of a finger. People say that snakes leave their semen on the ground, and the dog ate it, which is why this happened. However, a person's abdomen also produces mud, which is indeed strange. [According to Wan Yue.] ### 九仙梦验 In Xianyou County, Fujian Province, there are the Nine Immortals, known for the miraculous nature of their dreams. It is said that during the Han dynasty, there were nine brothers of the He family who studied Daoism and ascended to immortality in that place. Therefore, the mountains and waters in the area are often named after the Nine Immortals. There is a temple on the mountain, inside which there are statues of the Nine Immortals and a dream-transmitting judge. Those who pray for dreams first pray before the dream-transmitting judge, offering white chickens as sacrifices, and stay overnight in the temple. They will definitely have a dream at night. In the morning, they use the divination method of the yarrow stalks to determine if the dream is valid. If the sign is favorable, then the dream is valid. Otherwise, this dream is not reliable, and they pray again at night. If there are servants accompanying, their dreams are the same, often in the form of riddles, and only to be verified later. I remember several stories from my hometown: 1. A man from my hometown dreamed that he was given a magical sword by one of the Nine Immortals. The next day, he found a sword in his garden, which turned out to be a real sword with a sheath. He used it to defend his village from bandits, and it proved to be very effective. 2. A woman from my hometown dreamed that she was told by one of the Nine Immortals to plant a special seed in her garden. She followed the dream and planted the seed. The next year, the seed grew into a tree that bore fruit, which cured many illnesses in the village. 3. A merchant from my hometown dreamed that he would find a hidden treasure. Following the dream, he traveled to a remote mountain and found a cave. Inside the cave, he discovered a chest filled with gold and precious stones. He used the treasure to help his family and the poor in his community. These stories illustrate the power and reliability of the dreams associated with the Nine Immortals.

A man from Shuangfeng Township in Changshu, Gu, had an elderly mother who inquired about her lifespan. In a dream, a deity threw her a cloth robe, which is the colloquial term "liao xi" (撩膝). [This term is named for its length, which covers the knees.] Later, his mother fell ill with a knee abscess and died, and he realized that "liao xi" is a pun on "liu xi" (了膝), meaning the end of life. A student from Changzhou named Xu Hao, through the family of the school official Zhu, inquired about his lifetime. He was told that in a dream, he was at the foot of a high mountain, hearing only the sound of strong winds. Ten years later, Xu Hao died from a disease called "gǔ" (蛊). After his death, someone interpreted the meaning as: "In the I Ching, a mountain under the wind is the hexagram 'Gǔ' (decay)." When Wen Taishou was the governor of Wenzhou, he sent two servants to inquire about someone's lifespan [原本「寿数」作「数寿」，据说库本改.]. They replied, "Ask the old man Kong, who knows it himself." Previously, Wen had ordered the old man Kong to saw a piece of wood. The servants reported back, and the next day, Wen ascended the hall. The old man happened to be kneeling and said the number of boards, "Fifty-five pieces." This matched Wen's age exactly, causing him to be startled. He asked, "Can it still be cut?" The old man replied, "It is rotten and cannot be cut anymore." Wen was greatly displeased, and shortly after, he died from a boil on his back.

Wang Xianchen, the Magistrate, was originally from Suzhou but had registered his residence in the capital. After achieving high rank, he often felt a longing for his hometown and hoped that one day his daughter would marry someone from Suzhou. He also owned a house in the capital, which he left in the care of his family. When he was appointed to serve in Yongjia, Zhejiang, he sent a messenger to seek divine guidance to resolve two matters. First, he asked about his daughter's marriage, and the response was: "The one who will achieve prominence first at the white stone steps is also a member of the Dragon Flower Assembly." Second, he inquired about his new home, and in a dream, he saw a place with a Taoist talisman on the door bearing two seals. Later, Wang's daughter married the son of Zhu Xizhou, the top scholar, and this prediction came true. When he bought a house in the eastern part of the city, he also acquired a Taoist temple. Upon entering, he saw a talisman with two seals of the Celestial Master, which matched his dream, and he decided to settle there. A respected elder from his hometown, whose name has been forgotten, was serving as an official in Fujian. He passed through his hometown on official business. At that time, his wife was pregnant and about to give birth. The official had been away for a long time and had not received any news from home. He sent a message to inquire about the gender of the child. The response was: "It is Funing, not Fuqing." The official was very happy and said, "I have a son!" When asked how he knew, he replied, "When I left home, I told my wife that if we had a son, we would name him Funing, and if we had a daughter, we would name her Fuqing, taking the names from the two counties in Fujian. Only my wife knew this. Now, the divine message says the same thing. How could it not be a son?" A few days later, the news arrived that he indeed had a son.

○ The Divine Pill A daughter of a rice merchant from Jiangyin, who had reached the age of marriage and was of beautiful appearance, was suddenly possessed by a supernatural entity. She once saw a handsome man enter her chamber and have intercourse with her, who introduced himself as the Five Saints. Her parents summoned a master and a witch to treat her, but a hundred methods could not stop it. Eventually, they had no choice but to let it be. Whenever the girl had a need, even for items that were far away or out of season, she could obtain them with a mere gesture. At times, the room would be filled with gold, silver, pearls, and shells, but once she played with them, they would be taken back, and the supernatural being would not give them to her. Once, she saw thousands of ingots of gold piled in the corner of the room. She tried to take one, but as soon as she touched it, it turned into bricks and stones, or sometimes into paper. If she returned it, it would turn back into gold. One day, the supernatural being gave her a block-like object, which was of a stone-like quality, and said to her, “This is the divine pill. If used to press on the chest and abdomen of the dead, they will revive instantly. You should treasure it. It is only for your own use; even your parents may not share it with you.” The girl kept it carefully. Later, when her aunt suddenly fell ill and died, the girl wished to test the object. She took it out and showed it to her mother, who then placed it on the body of the deceased. Immediately, the aunt sprang back to life. The supernatural being came and angrily reprimanded the girl, saying, “What did I tell you? How could you use my pill so recklessly?” He demanded to see it, took it from her, and from that day on, he never came again. 【According to Chen Hong.】

The Parrot-Like Camellia Zi Yuan said: His sister's husband, Du Yuan翁, during the spring of the Ji Si year of Zhengde, traveled to Green Mountain with several friends. They entered the courtyard of a temple where the camellia flowers were in full bloom. The monk brought out a flower to show the guests. Its appearance was as if it were a parrot: two petals overlapped on either side to form wings, two petals joined to form the belly, two tendrils hung down to form feet, and the base extended sideways to form the head, with black spots on either side like eyes. The monk said, "This flower bloomed from among these trees." It can truly be called extraordinary. ● The Seventh Volume of Geng Si Bian Iron Cap Hermit Chen Zi Jing Lu Shilang

The Taoist priest with the iron crown, Zhang Jinghe, was a sorcerer from Jiangxi. His Taoist skills were highly advanced, beyond human understanding. When Emperor Taizu of the Ming Dynasty first stationed himself in Chuzhou, the Taoist priest visited the military camp to pay respects and spoke to the emperor, saying: "The world is in chaos, and it is not easy to establish peace without a ruler destined to save the world. From what I see now, it might be in Your Excellency!" The emperor asked him to explain his reasoning, and he replied: "Your Excellency has dragon eyes and phoenix eyes, an extraordinary appearance, and a preciousness too great to describe. If your divine spirit is radiant, like the wind sweeping away clouds, then it is the day of receiving the mandate." The emperor found his words remarkable and kept him in his entourage, frequently accompanying him on military campaigns. When the emperor faced off against Chen Sheng, he often ordered the Taoist priest to observe the atmosphere to predict good or bad omens, and what he said always came true. During the Battle of Poyang Lake, Liu Xiong was hit by an arrow and died, but both armies were unaware. The Taoist priest observed the atmosphere and knew of this, secretly reporting to the emperor: "Liu Xiong is dead, but his subordinates do not know, and they continue to fight fiercely. Please write an eulogy to commemorate him and send a condemned prisoner to go and cry out, then their morale will be broken, and our cause will succeed." The emperor followed his advice, and the Han forces were completely defeated. Afterward, when the emperor established his capital in Nanjing, all construction projects always had the Taoist priest choose the sites, and he was greatly trusted and relied upon.

Once, I visited the Jīmíng Temple on the mountain. At that time, the emperor intended to destroy and rebuild the temple because its high vantage point overlooked the imperial palace. He had not yet spoken of this plan when a Taoist monk suddenly said to the temple monk, "The emperor has the intention to destroy your temple. The next day, he will visit in person. You should block him on the way and plead with him; perhaps you can be spared." The monk had always revered the Taoist's magic, so the next morning, they gathered and burned incense, waiting several miles outside the mountain. When the emperor arrived, the monks repeatedly bowed and pleaded. The emperor was surprised and asked, "I had no such intention. Why are you falsely accusing me?" The monks replied, "This is what the Iron Hat Taoist taught us." The emperor was amazed and stopped the plan to destroy the temple. Initially, when Xu Wuning, the Duke of Wuning, was a general, the Taoist monk told him, "Your two cheeks are red, and your eyes shine like fire. You will reach the highest rank, but what is regrettable is that you will only attain a middle-aged life." Indeed, he died at the age of fifty-four.

A Daoist hermit built a cottage at the foot of Zhongshan Mountain. Liangguo Gong Lan Yu brought wine to visit him. The hermit greeted him in his rustic attire. Lan Yu, feeling belittled, was displeased and, during the wine service, playfully said, "I have a word for you to match: 'Wearing straw sandals to welcome guests, Your Feet are impolite.'" The hermit pointed to the coconut shell cup Lan Yu was holding and retorted, "Holding a coconut shell as a cup, In front of the wine, You are disloyal." Lan Yu, a martial man, did not understand the deeper meaning of the hermit's words, and they laughed together before parting. Later, Lan Yu was executed for treason, and his entire family was exterminated. The hermit lived in the capital for several years. One day, without any apparent reason, he jumped into the water at the Dazhong Bridge and drowned. The emperor ordered a search for his body, but it was not found. Soon after, a guard at Tongguan reported, "On a certain day, the Iron-Cap Daoist walked out of the pass with his staff." Calculating the date, it was exactly the day the hermit had jumped into the water. From then on, the hermit was never seen again. [Told by the Confucian scholar Mao Sheng of Nan Du.]

Chen Zi Jing Chen Jie, a scholar from Sizhou, once compiled the *Zongjian Xubian* (Chronicle of the Continuation of the Comprehensive Mirror), in which he recorded the events surrounding Emperor Taizu of Song's ascension at Chenqiao, stating: "Kuangyin established himself and returned." While he was in the process of writing, a sudden thunderclap struck his desk. Chen Jie remained calm and, with a stern voice, declared, "Even if Heaven strikes my arm, I will not change." Three days later, Chen Jie fell asleep during the day and dreamed of being summoned by someone to a place with grand and imposing gates, resembling the residence of a king. The gatekeeper rushed in to announce, "Mr. Chen has arrived." Chen Jie entered and stood in the courtyard. The emperor called from the hall, summoning him to ascend the steps. The emperor, seated in the center, wore a crown with jade pendants and a yellow robe, his face dark purple or black. He descended from his seat to greet Chen Jie and said, "What have I done to you? Why do you compare me to a usurper?" Chen Jie realized that the emperor was Emperor Taizu of Song and humbly apologized, "I beg your pardon, I truly know that this offends Your Majesty. However, history values truthful writing. Even if Your Majesty kills me, it cannot be changed." The emperor bowed his head, and Chen Jie descended the steps, waking up in shock. During the reign of the Hongwu Emperor, Chen Jie served as the official recorder of the emperor's daily activities and was executed for a legal offense. At the time of his execution, the emperor said, "I am especially clearing the anger of Emperor Taizu." [This story I heard from my predecessors [The original text is missing the character '予' (I), which is said to be added in the library edition]. Recently, I saw it recorded by Mr. Chen Yongzhi, who noted that the old edition referred to the Zhou ruler as the Prince of Zheng, while Chen Jie changed the character '奉' (to honor) to '废' (to depose), which differs from this account.]

Luo the Minister Minister Luo Rujing was an upright and bold man. He was from the same hometown as Yang Wenzheng and served together in the court. He was often dissatisfied with Yang Wenzheng's actions and repeatedly criticized him face-to-face, which made Yang Wenzheng quite displeased. Yang Wenzheng then recommended Luo Rujing to be the provincial governor of Ningxia, although Luo Rujing was already old. Soon after arriving at the border, the nomadic invaders launched a massive attack. Luo Rujing was directing the battle when the horse he was riding stumbled and fell into a deep pit. He could not get up and vaguely saw a person in red robes shielding him. The invaders saw nothing and left. There was a rear guard that arrived and saved him. Luo Rujing then submitted a petition requesting retirement and returned home.

**沈知刚** Shen Zhigang of Changzhou, when young, followed his father on official travels. One night, while reading by the light of his lamp, he suddenly saw someone take the lamp and walk quickly away. Shen Zhigang was startled and chased after him. As soon as his body left the bed, the wall where he had been sitting suddenly collapsed, leaving him astonished. He looked at the lamp and saw it still on the desk on the opposite wall, with all doors and windows locked, and the person had disappeared. It must have been done by a divine being. [According to Zhao Yuanbi.] **关公** Contribute Lan of Changzhou, a candidate for the imperial examination in the capital many years ago, stayed at an inn and fell seriously ill with a cold. He felt far from home, lonely, and distressed, and his illness was severe, causing him great concern. Seeing a portrait of Guan Yu hanging beside his bed, he silently prayed for his protection. In a daze, he saw Guan Yu come and call him. The figure looked like the one in the painting. Guan Yu told him, "Do not worry; by evening, you will sweat and recover." After speaking, Guan Yu suddenly disappeared. By evening, he indeed sweated and recovered. Later, he drew a picture of the image and worshipped it.

Monk Wanfeng (僧时蔚) Monk Wanfeng of the Shengen Temple in the West Mountain of Wu, also known as Wanfeng, was a son of the Jin family from Yandu, Wenzhou. He was a renowned disciple of the famous monk Changqian Yan during the late Yuan dynasty. After completing his initial studies, he asked his master for permission to travel. The master told him, "When you encounter your name, you should stop." Upon reaching Suzhou, he stopped at the eastern foot of the Dingsui Mountain and said, "Build a large temple at Xuanmu." Initially, he did not know how to read or write, but after achieving a profound insight into Chan Buddhism, he became able to write and compose verses that could be recited. Although he wore monastic robes, he kept his beard and hair, writing a self-praise that included the lines, "Binding my hair to distinguish myself as a monk, keeping a beard to show my manhood." In the 17th year of the Hongwu era (1381), the imperial court heard of his fame and sent an envoy to summon him. Seven days before the envoy arrived, Wanfeng had already foreseen this and told his disciple Wunian, "I have no karmic connection with them; you should go in response to the summons, I will leave now." He then bathed and entered Nirvana, leaving a final instruction not to cremate his body. Thirteen days later, his body was still warm, and he was buried in a clay jar. Wunian went to the imperial court and received great favor and rewards. Later, he resided at the Jiufeng Temple in Huguang, where he was equally esteemed alongside Wanfeng.

Wèi was always skilled in the art of feng shui and often spoke of the terrain of Xuanmu as resembling three dragons and three phoenixes, surpassing all others in the world. Many people consulted him for burial matters, but he never answered lightly. When he grew old and suffered from phlegm and qi issues, he told his disciples, "I should take the medicine of Shen Yiqian from the city; I have a karmic connection with him. He is in the capital and will return tonight; you should go and hurry him." The disciples followed his instructions and went to Shen Yiqian's residence, but he had not yet returned. They reported back, and Wèi sent them again. By night, they slept at the gate of Shen Yiqian's home, waiting for him. At the second drumbeat, Shen Yiqian indeed returned, and Wèi was surprised to hear this. He immediately went to treat Wèi, and his illness improved instantly. Wèi said, "I am grateful for your treatment of my illness, but I have nothing to offer in return. There is a plot of land here; I would like to offer it as a burial site for your respected wife." He then pointed to a site behind Zutu Mountain, slightly lowered by six feet, and said, "Although this site is modest, in sixty years, your family will flourish." Later, Shen Yiqian used it to bury his mother. By the time of the Chenghua period, Shen Yiqian's great-grandsons, Chen, etc., all became the wealthiest in their village, and Bu Zheng, Jie Yu, and De Tao successively passed the imperial examinations. Their family became prominent, and it was exactly sixty years since the burial. I heard this from Shen Yiqian's great-great-grandson, Zhu. However, the words about keeping a beard were actually spoken by a monk from the Stone Room. It seems Wèi did not plagiarize; it was probably a coincidence.

Pú Yìngxiáng** A man from Wuxi named Pú Yìngxiáng, during the Dingyou year of the Chenghua era, was recommended for the provincial examination. He spent thirty-eight years in the metropolitan examination hall without success. It was not until the Jiayou year of the Zhengde era that he was finally selected, obtaining the position of Deputy Governor of Gaozhou Prefecture. He once mentioned that when he was first recommended, he had a dream of riding in a sedan chair, with a monk carrying him in front. This dream repeated several times. After he obtained his official position, he brought a monk back from the capital to Wuxi. On the road, whenever they encountered danger or waded through muddy water, he would always order the monk to carry the sedan chair over. After a few days, he suddenly remembered the monk he had dreamed about before—it was this very monk. This small incident, which predicted his success three decades earlier, shows that the gains and losses of life are not by chance. **Fàn Rǔyú** Fàn Rǔyú is a descendant of Fàn Zhòngyǎn, who was posthumously honored as Wénzhèng Gōng. As a student at the local government school, he failed the provincial examination and returned home. On his way, he passed by a temple and prayed for good fortune. That night, he stayed at the temple and dreamed of his friend's son-in-law, Qín Ruì, dressed in fine attire sitting in the main hall. He did not understand the meaning of the dream. On his next attempt, he passed the examination. According to custom, when a candidate passes the provincial examination, the prefecture sends a standard announcement board. The board lists the names and ranks of the successful candidates, starting from the prefectural officials down to the individual names. After Fàn Rǔyú passed, he happened to walk around the hall and saw a board nailed to the door frame. One line on the board read: "Deputy Governor Qín Ruì." He then realized the meaning of his previous dream and was amazed.

Strange Births** 【Strange Births: The library edition reads "one birth of five sons."】 In the autumn and winter of the year 1526, Zhang Ma's wife from Wujin bore five sons in a single birth. Several years earlier, Wu Qi's wife from Changzhou bore four sons in a single birth, all of whom did not survive. Xu Wenfu, the husband of her aunt, once saw two children being carried, whose bodies were so closely joined that their skin could not be separated, appearing as if they were united in a sexual union. It was said that they had died at birth. **Mr. Huang, the Censor of Education** Mr. Huang Rujin, the former censor of education in the southern region, was from Putian. In the year 1494, he passed the provincial examination in Fujian as the top candidate. Prior to this, a servant of a scholar in a neighboring county had a dream in which he sought guidance from the Nine Immortals to determine who would be the top candidate in this year's examination. He was told, "Wu Yi and Huang Er, inside the bucket door, ask if it is Huang Rujin." Thinking that there was no one named Huang Rujin among the students of his school, he concluded that it must be someone from another county and went to Putian to find him. Early in the morning, he came upon a place along the way where two men stood at the door. The servant greeted them and asked, "Is this the home of Scholar Huang Rujin?" They replied, "This is indeed his home." When asked their names, they said Wu Yi and Huang Er, and they were both servants of the Huang family. Looking inside the door, he saw a water bucket, and then he recounted his dream. Soon after, Mr. Huang was indeed selected as the top candidate. 【According to a merchant from Putian.】

The Shrine of Eastern Jiangnan** The Shrine of Eastern Jiangnan is located beside the training ground, where 100 bamboo slips inscribed with poems are used for divination to determine auspicious or inauspicious outcomes. It is very famous for its accuracy. Here are a few recorded incidents: - **Changzhou Elder Scholar Zhao Tonglu, aged eighty-one, fell ill and drew a slip for divination, receiving a poem that read: "Three three before and three three after." That year, Zhao Tonglu passed away on the ninth day of the ninth month. Some said that two three threes make nine nines, which also coincides with Zhao's age.** - **A resident of Xianqiao named Xu served as a village head and was dispatched to Wukang in Hunan Province for military service. Fearing the long journey, he prayed for an exemption and drew a slip for divination, receiving a poem that read: "A ten-thousand-mile journey is your destiny." Later, when he arrived at the military office, there was a signpost at the gate, inscribed with "A ten-thousand-mile journey." Xu looked up and saw it, recalling the words of the divination.** - **Changzhou student Zhou Jingliang, who was mediocre and unlearned, sought fame in the provincial examination and drew a slip for divination, receiving a poem that read: "Soaring alone toward the clouds." He believed this was a sign of success. However, when he took the examination before the educational censor, he was demoted to a clerk in Songjiang Prefecture, as "clouds" actually refers to the ancient name of Songjiang.** - **Prefectural student Tao Lin failed multiple times in the imperial examinations and drew a slip for divination to decide whether to advance or retreat, receiving a poem that read: "In the end, everything turns out to be nothing." He thought he would never succeed. Later, during the initial test for the imperial examination, he received the number "empty" in the registration and was selected for the imperial university. In the year 1517, he passed the provincial recommendation, and his red scroll number was also "empty." In 1521, when he took the examination at the Ministry of Rites, the same number appeared, and he was promoted to the rank of Jinshi (imperial scholar).

My teacher, Mr. Mao, was once deeply attached to a courtesan, and their affection was very close. The courtesan planned to entrust her entire life to him and secretly gave him a hairpin, agreeing to use it as a dowry. My teacher took it home, feeling quite hesitant, and secretly went to pray. He received a poem that said: "Remembering the past, we divided a hairpin in the fragrant room." The last line of the poem read: "In the end, the matter would not come to fruition." Upon reading the first line, my teacher was greatly shocked and prostrated himself in worship. At that time, the hairpin was still in his sleeve, and he then refused her. I once read the temple records, which stated: "The deity, a Qin person, surnamed Shi and named Gu." ○Five-Legged Ox In the year Bingsi [originally written as "this year," but said to be corrected in the library edition], a monk brought a cow from the capital to Suzhou. The cow had five legs, one of which was under the hindquarters, short and did not reach the ground. Its hoof was like a human hand, with skin connecting the five fingers. The monk led it through the market to beg for money, and I personally saw it. I once heard that during the Zhengtong period, my hometown's Mr. Liu Yuanbo went to the capital, and his son Zong Xu saw a cow kept by a family on the side of the road. The cow had five legs, one of which was born on the neck, with the hoof facing upward. He told the master, who said: "The cow is of the earth element, and the hoof is especially lowly. Now it is on top. Is there not a small person in power, causing change?" Two years later, it was the year Ji Si, and his words proved true.

○ Transformation into a Ghost A monk named Yue Tang from Huayan Temple in Nanjing traveled to Guizhou for alms and food in the past. He heard the locals say that in this region, there were customs among the non-Han ethnic groups where some people could transform into ghosts. They could change into animals such as sheep, pigs, donkeys, and mules, and would bite people to death, sucking their blood and eating them. The local officials appointed by the central government imposed strict laws to prohibit this practice, but they could not completely eliminate it. They warned the monk, saying, "Be careful when you sleep." One night, the monk and several others stayed at the temple. When it was late, they heard a sheep bleating outside. Shortly after, a sheep entered the room and approached the sleeping person, sniffing at them. The monk thought to himself, "Is this not what the people said?" He immediately swung his Zen staff and struck it below the waist. The sheep fell to the ground and then returned to its original form, revealing a woman with a deformed body. The monk captured and bound her, intending to report her to the authorities. The woman wailed incessantly. The next morning, the monk asked someone to go report to her family. Her family came together to the temple, prostrated themselves, and begged for mercy, offering 300 taels of silver to the monk to redeem the woman's life. The monk accepted the silver and then released the woman, letting her go. One day, the monk went out to the outskirts and saw the local official's entourage spread across the fields. They were just about to execute a person and bury them. The monk asked the bystanders, who said, "We caught the person who could transform into a ghost." 【Written by the monk himself.】

### 李智 People in Wu who burn corpses also possess great magical arts. Among them, there was a man named Li Zhi, who was particularly skilled in this art. Once, he was rowing a boat out of Dongzhucun and saw a corpse being burned on the shore. He told the people in the boat, "I just want to play a trick on them." He had one person lie down, and Li Zhi pointed with a spear and silently recited a spell. After finishing the spell, he called the person to sit up, and the corpse on the shore also sat up. When he made the person stand, the corpse also stood. When he made the person hold their own head, the corpse also held the head of the person burning it. All the onlookers were terrified. The person burning the corpse realized, "Someone is playing tricks, trying to ruin my business." Li Zhi suddenly changed color and told everyone in the boat, "He is not good at it. You all go away for a moment. I have something to deal with." He quickly grabbed the rudder and crouched down. Suddenly, there was a loud sound like thunder, and the rudder was cut into pieces. Li Zhi stood up and said happily, "I'm saved." When asked why, he explained, "The person is also a master. It was just a play, but unexpectedly, he wanted to harm me. If my skills were slightly inferior to his, he would have already smashed my head." There was once a person who studied from Li Zhi but did not fully master his techniques and left. Later, this person burned a corpse elsewhere and the fire did not burn at all throughout the day. The person realized, "This is what my teacher did." He carried money and gifts to thank Li Zhi. When he returned, the fire immediately started. [According to Dousui.]

Several years ago, a large ship drifted from the Western Ocean to Chongming County. There were seven people on board, and the patrol officer arrested them, thinking they were thieves. The seven people said, "We are merchants from Guangdong, our boat entered the Western Ocean and was blown here by the wind, we are not thieves." They were taken to the local official for verification and then sent back to their hometown. One of the people said, "When we were at sea, we once anchored at an island and wanted to go ashore to get firewood. Suddenly, several strange creatures appeared, with human bodies and horse heads. They entered the water from the island and swam, placing their heads against the ship's side and making a whistling sound. One of us cut off one of their heads with a knife, and the rest fled. We thought they would call their kind to attack us, so we quickly untied the mooring ropes and set sail. Within the time it takes to eat a meal, over a hundred creatures with horse heads stood at the water's edge, jumping and leaping, trying to catch us. But the wind was favorable and our boat was fast, and they could not catch us. If we had hesitated even a little, we would have been caught by them."

Zhu's Herdsman At Zhu's household in Haiyan, there was a herdsman who was around ten years old and was known for his hearty appetite. One day, he led the sheep back and went for lunch. The cook deliberately withheld his meal as a joke. The herdsman left, crying in the fields. A Taoist passed by, saw him, and asked, "Why are you crying?" The herdsman told him he had no food. The Taoist took out a black pill from his robe, as big as a dragon eye, and gave it to him, saying, "If you eat this, you will feel full, but do not chew it." The boy swallowed it and felt his belly become full. The Taoist warned him, "Do not tell anyone," and then left. The boy returned at dusk without eating and did not feel hungry the next day. He went without food for five or six days. The cook suspected that the boy had stolen rice to eat and reported this to the master. The master summoned the boy and was about to whip him. The boy, out of fear, revealed the truth, and the master found it strange.

The next day, he sent his other son along, saying, "If you meet the Taoist again, one of you should try to hold onto his robe, and one should return to report." The two sons arrived at the place, and the Taoist appeared again. He said to the sons, "Why did you reveal this?" They replied, "We feared being blamed by the master." The Taoist supported the boy's chin with one hand and struck his forehead with the other. A black ball jumped out of his throat and returned to his chest. The boy tried to hold onto it, asking, "What is this?" The Taoist said, "Do you know what is called 'interlocking pears and fire dates'? This is a fire date." After a while, the owner heard the report and came. As he was about to leave several hundred steps away, the Taoist's feet suddenly sank into the ground. As he got closer, it sank deeper, only his head was visible, then his head also disappeared, and there were no holes in the ground. Looking in surprise, the Taoist was already on the other side of the river, bowing his hands and vanishing. From then on, the shepherd boy resumed eating. A person from my hometown, Su Pan, who was a teacher of rituals at that time, personally witnessed the event. [Reported by Liu Zonglian.] ### Soul and Spirit Emperor Gao once became angry with an eunuch who did not sweep properly and ordered him to be taken out and executed. The eunuch was bound and led to the market, still wearing a gold-embroidered green robe. The people in the market saw a man standing in front of the eunuch, bowing his hands, who looked exactly like the eunuch in appearance and attire, which was very strange. Later, the emperor received an order to stop the execution, and the eunuch was released. The man who had been standing in front of him slowly disappeared. It was suspected that this was the eunuch's soul or spirit. ### Liu Gong's Ability to See Qi [Note: The section about Liu Gong's ability to see qi is not provided in the source text, so it is not translated here.]

The Battle of Poyang was in full swing, with both sides deeply engaged in combat. Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang sat on a folding chair at the front of his boat, giving orders to his troops. Liu Bowen, the Marquis of Sincerity, stood by his side. Suddenly, Liu Bowen's face turned red, and he spoke angrily, pulling the emperor into the boat with one hand. The emperor was startled, and just then, a cannon shot came, destroying the folding chair and saving the emperor. The day before the victory, the emperor was extremely tired and wanted to withdraw. Liu Bowen secretly advised him, saying, "Wait a little while. Tomorrow at noon, my energy will be strong." Eventually, there was a victory at noon. In the final chapter of the *Gengsi Bian*: ### Celestial Phenomena - **Zhang Zongmao** - **Chickens and Dogs of Dongting Lake** - **Flying Fish** - **Bees Transforming into Crickets** - **Human Disease** - **Fox in a Village** - **Appearance of the God of the North Pole** - **Disaster Involving Cattle** - **White Crow** - **Shaman from Chu** - **Yang Kuan** - **Square Egg and Monkey** - **Sparrow Report** - **Carpenter Yu** - **Old Thief** ### Celestial Phenomena At the end of the Hongzhi era, Dai Gong of Fuliang and Shi Gong of Yuyao both served as heads of the internal court. Shi Gong was skilled in divination and observed that Mars was in an unfavorable position. He told others, "Those in charge of justice will have troubles." In the twelfth month of that year, Dai Gong died. Someone said to Shi Gong, "Your words were accurate." Shi Gong replied, "Not yet." He had repeatedly submitted requests to return home, but they were not granted. Soon after, he became ill. The following year, in the first month of the Zhengde era, he died in office. Mars eventually returned to a favorable position.

○ Zhang Zongmao The Daoist Master Zhang Zongmao of the Xuanmiao Temple in Wu was a man of virtue and spiritual attainment, famed for the efficacy of his talismans and incantations. In the home of Scholar-official Chen of Iron Bottle Lane, there were dozens of yellow rats and jackals, gathering in packs and appearing openly during the day, attacking chickens and livestock and gnawing through clothes and tables. Divination revealed: "This is the spirit of Bian, who will come to seek out Master Zhang’s talismans to deal with it—but the details have not yet been explained." One day, as Zhang Zongmao was rising early to recite scripture, a figure suddenly stood before him in a respectful posture. Looking at it, he saw a being with a human body but the head of a rat or jackal. It bowed and said, "We have a long-standing grudge with the Chen family and wish to avenge it. He has come to seek the master, but the master has no involvement in our matter." Zhang Zongmao scolded it, and it vanished at once. He then went directly to Chen’s house, told him what he had seen, and performed a ritual with magical water to purify and a talisman to subdue the spirits. The supernatural disturbances ceased. Later, when Zhang Zongmao grew old, he died peacefully, sitting calmly without illness, and was regarded as the foremost Daoist practitioner of his time. [Reported by his maternal uncle Chen Chongren.] ○ The Chicken and Dog of Dongting Mountain In the year Ding Chou [Originally written as "this year," it is said that the Kufu edition changed it], a family in Dongting Mountain had a yellow dog born with two horns, each about an inch long. Another family had a hen whose comb and tail suddenly grew long, and it transformed into a rooster, able to raise its voice and crow loudly. The Daoist official Xue Mingjing heard this account from a county magistrate in the area.

### ○ Fly Fish In Shahu, the wealthy family of the Qiu clan owned a fish pond near an outer harbor. During the summer, a heavy rain caused the water to overflow, and several-foot-long carp, along with other fish, one by one flew out of the harbor and left. By evening, as the water gradually receded, the fish returned, with the large carp still leading the way, followed by the others, flying and leaping through the air like a flock of butterflies dancing. The author once read the *Fanyi Jing* by Fan Li, which mentions that fish can fly, and indeed, this phenomenon is true. However, the fact that they go and return is even more extraordinary. [According to Mr. Xu Hongxuan.] ### ○ Bee Transformed into Cricket Liu Hao of Xiangcheng had a passion for fighting crickets. One morning, he went out through the Loumen gate and saw a large bee rolling around several times in the mud, then gathering on a withered lotus leaf. He found this strange. When he returned to the same spot later, he saw that the bee was still there, but its body had transformed into a cricket, with its head and legs still resembling a bee. He took it home and raised it. After a few days, it shed its muddy shell and completely transformed. It was strong and good at fighting, and it never lost in any battle. The transformation of one creature into another is indeed a natural phenomenon.

○ Human Sickness During the reign of the Hongzhi Emperor, a woman in Changshu County gave birth to a child with two heads, who died immediately after birth. People competed to come and see, and some gave her money. The poor man hoped to gain more by keeping the child longer, so he hid it. The wet nurse Zhou, an elderly woman, told me that she once attended a childbirth for someone and saw a child with four heads connected by a single neck, which frightened her so much that she killed it. The elderly woman kept the family name secret and would not reveal it. ○ Fox of Gǔtíng During the reign of the Hongzhi Emperor, a canal ship from the Hangzhou garrison was returning from the capital to Shandong. It was winter, and the river was frozen, so the ship stopped at Ba Li Bay, which was eight li from Gǔtíng Town, hence its name. One evening, a woman with beautiful and alluring attire stood on the shore and called out to the leader of the soldiers, asking for shelter. She said, "I am a local from this town, on my way to my mother's house. It's getting dark, and I can't make it. If you could let me stay, I won't forget to thank you." The soldier refused, but the woman wouldn't leave. As night deepened, she pleaded more urgently, her words filled with sorrow. The soldier unconsciously replied, "Yes," and let her stay at the camp, her sleeping place separated by only a board. In the middle of the night, the woman cried out in pain, her cries were soft and plaintive. The soldier, moved by her cries, got up and made ginger soup for her to drink. He approached her, and she did not shy away. They became intimate, and the soldier considered it a wonderful encounter. At the fifth watch of the night, it started snowing heavily, and the woman said she would return. She said to the soldier, "My home is not far from here. If you are interested, I will come back tonight." The soldier replied, "That would be great."

He gave her a pair of embroidered pillow tops and the pork liver and lungs he had bought, leaving them with her, and said, "You can take them home and prepare them to serve your mother." The woman got up and left through the snow. The soldier slept, and by the time it was late, he had not woken up. At that time, everyone on the boat knew about his condition. Some got up and followed his path, and they saw dozens of animal tracks in the snow, finding it very strange. They collectively said, "She was beautiful and particularly attractive, and she came at night, leaving before dawn. How can we be sure it's not a demon?" They called the soldier to question him, but initially, he still denied it. They led him to the shore, pointed out the snow tracks to him, and he was greatly shocked and finally told the truth. Together, they went to the town to ask around, and some locals said, "There is a centuries-old fox here that often tricks people. Could what you experienced be from such a fox?" They hurriedly returned to the boat, gathered everyone with weapons and firewood, and set out. They followed the tracks to the wilderness, turned into a deep, secluded place, and the tracks ended. They saw a large tree, several people could embrace it, with a hole in the middle. The pillow tops and pork liver were hanging on the branches. The crowd was delighted and said, "This must be a fox den." They surrounded it, threw firewood into the hole, and burned it for a long time. A fox suddenly emerged from the smoke, and the crowd killed it. The soldier was in a daze for ten days and then recovered.

**True Martial Manifestation** A wealthy man named Ding from Songjiang, in his prime, had no son. His concubine was pregnant, and Ding prayed to the deity Zhenwu whom he served, saying, "If a boy is born, when he grows up, I will personally carry him to Mount Tai to burn incense and thank the divine favor." Soon after, a boy was indeed born. When the boy reached six years old, Ding and his wife and concubines planned to fulfill their vow. They all felt that the child was too young and the journey too dangerous, and they wanted to wait a few more years. However, Ding believed that his initial promise could not be broken, so he insisted on going, accompanied by two servants, and took his son with them. As soon as they arrived, they stayed at an inn. The boy suddenly fell ill and died within a few days. Ding was deeply grieved and complained, "My father and son are sincere, traveling thousands of miles, and even if the god does not bestow blessings, but why should we suffer this misfortune!" After several more days, the pain subsided slightly, and he ascended the mountain, leaving the boy's coffin at the inn and instructing the innkeeper to guard it well. After three nights, the two servants came to see the innkeeper, following the master's command, loaded the coffin, and left. The next morning, Ding arrived and asked where the coffin was. The innkeeper explained the servants' story. Ding exclaimed in surprise, "The two servants followed me up the mountain, they are still behind, how could there be such a thing?" When the servants returned, the innkeeper confronted them face-to-face, and they too were surprised and alarmed, suspecting that the innkeeper had another reason, and swore by heaven to clear their names.

Ding expressed great grief, saying, "I went against the words of my wife and concubines, forcing my son to come. Now that he is dead, I have also lost his remains. How can I face my family when I return? I will only die!" After boarding the boat, he wept daily and refused to eat, nearly dying from grief. Those who returned with him often comforted him and made him eat. They arrived at Song, not far from home, and sent a servant back to report. Upon entering the house, the mistress of the house came out, angrily scolding her husband, saying, "You have only one son, who traveled thousands of miles, and you allow others to take him back. How can you be at peace with your conscience?" She also blamed the servant for not advising his master. The servant was shocked and did not know what to say, so he ran to tell his master. The master was greatly surprised and immediately left the boat, rushing home. The wife and concubines scolded him together, asking him the reason. He then said, "A few days ago in the evening, a boat docked at the shore, and two guests brought my son inside, saying they met us on a pilgrimage to Wudang Mountain. The master was slightly detained and entrusted this child to be sent back first." Ding was greatly startled, called out to his son to see him, and found the rash and scars still on his face. But when he recounted the previous events, no one believed him. He asked the fellow travelers to confirm it, and they finally realized it was not false. When asked how his son survived, he was completely unaware. 【 Right here, Lu Baiguan said.】 ○ Cow Disaster In the sixth month of Dingchou (which was originally written as "this year's sixth month," but was reportedly changed in the library version), the governor-general of Huai and Yang regions, Cunlan, reported that a cow belonging to Bao Qianguan in Chuzhou gave birth to a calf with two heads, eight legs, and two tails on the same back. The calf died immediately after birth. According to the official gazette.

#### White Crow In Yancheng County, Daming Prefecture, there lived a wealthy man whose courtyard was very spacious, with over a hundred jujube trees planted. There were hundreds of nests on these trees. In the year 1494 (Jiazi of the Hongzhi era), a white chick hatched in one of the nests and accidentally fell to the ground. The man's family took it in and raised it. As it grew older, it was as pure and white as snow, gentle and lovable. At the time, Empress Dowager Xiaoshu had recently passed away, and this white crow was seen as a sign of Emperor Jiajing's filial piety during his mourning period. The court was encouraged to present the crow as an offering. However, the court initially refused to accept it, and the crow died on the way back. [According to Zhang Gang, the governor.] #### Shaman from Chu The customs of Chu favored the worship of spirits and ghosts, and there were many sorcerers who practiced varied and unpredictable magic. People called these sorcerers "Shigong" (teacher-father) and greatly respected and feared them. In Wugang Prefecture, there was a particularly clever sorcerer named Jiang Cong, who served as a priest at the City God Temple. The temple was close to the Nanwei Prince's mansion. One day, the prince's foot wrap was blown to the temple by the wind. Jiang Cong found it and told his wife, "Our food and clothing are secured." He killed a duck, took its head, wrapped it with the foot wrap, nailed it down, and placed it under the god's seat, performing a spell. The prince immediately experienced severe foot pain that prevented him from sleeping or eating. He invited many sorcerers to pray and make offerings day and night, but the pain did not stop. Later, Jiang Cong claimed to be able to cure the prince and went to inquire about his condition. An eunuch used his own money to prepare sacrifices and invited Jiang Cong to perform a ritual for the prince. Jiang Cong removed the nail from the prince's foot, and the pain immediately disappeared, earning him a large reward.

Another ten days later, the ritual was repeated in the same way, but the king's illness remained unchanged. The king then called for Zong to pray, and the illness stopped. From March until nearly the end of the year, the illness would flare up occasionally, and the king would definitely order Zong to pray. After the prayer, the illness would immediately subside. The king began to suspect Zong and told him, "Next year will be a major ceremony for the city god, and I will certainly put you to great effort." However, the king deliberately delayed the ceremony. The pain would suddenly intensify, and he sent someone to arrange a specific date for the ceremony, after which the pain would then suddenly disappear. The king saw through Zong's deceit, summoned him, and detained him. He ordered an officer to set a trap for Zong's wife, who obtained three items to present. The king personally interrogated Zong, who at first resisted. When shown the items, he then fully confessed. The case was concluded, and a fast horse was sent to report to the emperor, who issued an edict ordering the arrest of the witch and sending him to the capital. Zong was killed in Linqing. At that time, most of the witches were generally arrogant and domineering. If a family had a little wine and food, and a witch passed by their door, they would certainly be invited to enjoy it. If they refused to go, the witch would take the food to their home, otherwise, they would suffer misfortune.

If one goes out seeking profit, encountering a sorcerer on the road and humbly requesting a good word, one will surely gain much, otherwise suffer great loss. In a moment of anger or a glance, one can bring either good or evil. The group itself is rife with internal strife, and they use their skills to compete against each other. When the newly deceased have not yet been buried, sorcerers are invited to perform rituals, using the hem of clothing to catch the corpse's energy and disperse it in the wild. The corpse remains uncorrupted for months, called 'temporary stench.' Those who come to break the spell, upon entering, the corpse's stench immediately appears. Those who know say: in their teachings, the corpse is transformed into something, like transforming into a carp and placing it on a cliff covered with ice. Those who break the spell simply use fire to melt the ice, and the corpse naturally decays and stinks. Only if transformed into agarwood, nothing can harm it, but fire can still burn it. When the Mian王府 funeral procession began, the coffin was so heavy that even adding dozens more men could not move it. They called upon a shaman to perform rituals, and after a moment, the procession moved forward.

The witch said, "A certain witch, due to a long-standing grudge, moved a mountain onto a coffin, but it has just been moved away." Such is her deceitful and disgusting nature. After the defeat of Jiang Cong, these types of people began to be somewhat restrained. A local man named Wu Yong served as a teacher in the Min state and often recounted this story. At the time, there was a school official named Li Wu who also performed many transformations. Wu Yong tried his skill and saw a magpie on the roof, commanding it to be fetched. Li Wu silently recited a spell, causing the magpie to fly to him, and then caught it by hand. Li Wu said, "It is necessary to use it by chance; if one has the intention to catch birds beforehand, then one cannot catch even one or two in a day. Other things are all like this." He also said, "His magic does not work well when it crosses Dongting Lake, and he also does not enjoy doing this. It is roughly like the southern methods used in Fujian and Guangdong and the carpentry methods of厌胜术, because it is passed down from previous generations, he cannot help but do it." Wu Yong is quite good at talking about strange things, and the last four events are all his stories.

A Tale of Elan In the Xianning County School of Zhending, there was a servant named Elan. Once, during an official banquet, he was in charge of serving wine and saw two whirlwinds swirling at the corner of a wall. Elan believed that the whirlwinds contained many spirits and decided to pour some wine from a ladle as an offering. One whirlwind suddenly stopped, and after pouring another ladle of wine, the second whirlwind also ceased. One day, Elan and four of his peers went to Mount Tai to burn incense. They encountered two figures at the foot of the mountain, dressed in green and white robes. The figures invited them with a bow and said, "We have been greatly favored by you for a long time and have not yet had the chance to repay you. Would you like to join us for a drink at an inn?" Elan did not recognize them and thought they must be mistaken. He casually agreed and they all went to an inn to drink, then parted ways without inquiring about their names. When his peers asked him about it, Elan replied that he did not know them, and they all laughed. Later, they climbed the mountain and visited a shrine. To their astonishment, the statues of two attendants in the shrine looked exactly like the figures they had seen earlier. Elan and his companions were greatly alarmed. Elan felt that he had encountered a ghost and was unhappy. When they returned to their original place, they saw the two figures again. They told Elan, "You need not doubt us; we are not here to harm you. Do you remember the events of the past? We are attendants of the Lord of Mount Tai, on a mission to this prestigious county. In our journey, we were hungry and thirsty, and you gave us two ladles of wine, which greatly satisfied our wishes. Yesterday, we had business in the Western Mountains and coincidentally met you, so we offered you a cup of wine as a token of our gratitude. There is no other reason." After they finished speaking, they suddenly disappeared. Elan returned home and personally told others about the encounter.

**○方卵猕猴** During the late Hongzhi period, Ai Gong from Nanchang served as an imperial commissioner in Jiangnan. In Chongming, a county under Suzhou, there was a report that a household had discovered a square egg, which they found strange and broke. Inside was a creature called a "mihou," only as big as a date. Ai Gong reported this to Chen Gong, the imperial censor from Changzhou, and suggested they both present it to the emperor. Chen Gong replied, "It is indeed necessary to report this strange occurrence, but the creature is very strange, and I suspect it no longer exists. If a powerful minister who likes to take credit for things were to order it presented to the emperor, how could we respond?" Ai Gong then decided not to proceed. Wu Yong saw this document and commented on it. **○雀报** A military officer named Fan from the left company of the Zhenjiang Guard had a wife who was suffering from a wasting disease and was near death. She encountered a Taoist who gave her medicine and said, "Use one hundred sparrows, feed them with medicated rice, and after one hundred days, take their brains and consume them to cure the illness. However, do not reduce the number of sparrows." Fan bought the sparrows as instructed and kept them alive. Whenever any died, he would buy more to keep the count up. After ten days, Fan was sent on a public mission, and his wife, upon seeing the sparrows, sighed, "Because of me, the lives of a hundred creatures have been harmed. How can I bear to do this? I would rather die than continue." She opened the cage and released all the sparrows. When her husband returned, he became angry and scolded her, but she did not regret her actions. Soon after, her illness improved. Initially, she had been unable to bear children for a long time, but that year she unexpectedly became pregnant and gave birth to a son. The boy had black scars on his arms, shaped like sparrows, one flying and one crouching to peck, with feathers that were clearly visible, as if carved. This was apparently a sign from the spirit world, showing her reward for releasing the sparrows.

A Carpenter Named Yu Yu, the carpenter, was from Wugang, a place in the Huguang region. His father once dreamt of the god of carpentry, and as a result, he became skilled in sculpting divine images with great precision. Yu, when he was seven or eight years old, had eyebrows and eyes as beautiful as a painting, and he was naturally intelligent and quick-witted. The prefect of his region admired him and named him "Yu" after his father's skills. [The local records say "Zi Ren," which might be incorrect.] As he grew older, Yu displayed exceptional talent and possessed many unusual abilities. He passed the imperial examination in 1375 during the reign of the Hongwu Emperor and served as the governor of Dengzhou Prefecture. One day, a citizen reported that his family had been injured by a tiger. Yu ordered a soldier to carry a document into the mountains to capture the tiger. The soldier wept and refused to go, so Yu lashed him. He then ordered two other soldiers, saying, "Just burn this document, and the tiger in the mountains will come by itself." The two soldiers, having no choice, went into the mountains and burned the document. As soon as the fire went out, the tiger appeared, with its ears lowered and tail tucked. It followed the soldiers back into the city, where crowds gathered to watch. The tiger lay motionless at the courtyard gate, and Yu sternly scolded it, striking it a hundred times before letting it go. The tiger then returned to its original path and left.

Soon after, the local people accused him of using witchcraft to deceive the people, and an imperial decree was issued to arrest the carpenter and bring him to the officials for punishment. After several months, he died of illness in prison, and his body was discarded. His family mourned and wore mourning clothes. One night, they suddenly heard knocking on the door. When they asked who it was, he replied, "I am the carpenter." The family was startled and said, "It's a ghost!" He said, "I actually escaped in the meantime. They said I was dead, but don't doubt it." The family didn't believe him, saying that ghosts' clothes have no seams, but upon checking, it wasn't so. So they let him in. The carpenter did not hide himself and daily socialized with his old friends. Sometimes he would sail a boat without a rudder or oars, going upstream against the current for fun. A local man named Liu, who was his enemy, seized him and tied him up, then reported to the governor Wu Fang, asking for a report to be made to the emperor. Governor Wu Fang found the matter strange and did not allow it. Liu then went to the imperial court to report it. The imperial court sent a judge to the prefecture to investigate, but before the judge arrived, one day the carpenter suddenly disappeared. Only a chain remained. Liu had no way to prove himself and was ultimately punished severely for false accusations. And the carpenter was never seen again. The carpenter called himself the "Seventy-One Peaks Hermit," and his writings were forceful and graceful, worth reading. Wu Yong kept one copy of his poem "Traveling on Mount Tai," and I have seen it before.

○ The Old Bandit Jin Sheng of Jiaxing served as an official in the Ministry of Justice during the Yongle era. At that time, several bandits from Huguang were chained and brought to the ministry. Sheng interrogated them, and their ringleader was found to be 125 years old, with the face of a child. Sheng did not believe it, so he sent an official document to verify the claim, and it was confirmed to be true. When asked how he had lived so long, the old bandit replied, "When I was young, I lived in Jing Mountain, where I once met a man who roasted grass over my navel, saying, 'This will grant you a long life.' I have lived until now because of that." The court, considering his great age, ordered him to be executed by beating, and the rest were all put to death.

**Chapter 9 of the Gengsi Collection** **Strange People and Star Divination** The emperor of the Ming dynasty, residing in the Xiaoling Mausoleum, often engaged in secret travels to observe the public sentiment. One night, he ventured out and temporarily stayed at an inn, resting his head on a stone and his feet on a mat. In the middle of the night, two people got up and began to converse. The emperor listened covertly. One person in the courtyard called out to someone in the room: "This old man has come out again tonight. I observed the celestial phenomena and predicted that he would be in a commoner's house, with his head on a stone and his feet on a mat." The person in the room laughed and said, "Could you be mistaken?" The emperor was surprised by this and immediately changed his sleeping position, placing his head on the mat and his feet on the stone. Soon after, the person in the courtyard arrived and said, "You were indeed mistaken! This old man has his head on the mat and his feet on the stone." The emperor, upon hearing this, was so frightened that he broke out in a cold sweat and immediately returned to the palace that night. He tried to find the two people but failed. After this incident, his secret travels became less frequent. This event is similar to the story of Emperor Wu of Han, who also encountered a scholar during a secret journey.

Gold Leaf Zhang At the beginning of the dynasty, there was a man named Gold Leaf Zhang, from Shanxi Province. From a young age, he was skilled in many things. Once, because the people in his village were not adept at working with gold leaf, he went to Hangzhou to learn the craft, and upon returning, he taught them, gaining fame for it. One day, while passing through Jiyuan in Henan, he encountered a spirit known for its supernatural powers. People who asked for cloth would see what they needed float out of the water. Zhang saw this and said, "How can this be called a god? It's just a hidden mechanism." Upon returning, he dug a pool and imitated its design, and it worked as expected. Whenever guests came, he would play with it as a game. Once, he met a Taoist priest, who led him to see the pool. The Taoist said, "I also have a small skill. You should come to my place to see it." The next day, before dawn, Zhang saw two children riding a dragon, which then descended to his home. The Taoist asked Zhang to climb the dragon, but the dragon did not obey. The two children whipped it, and Zhang finally climbed on. Soon, they arrived at a mountain with three thatched houses, where the Taoist sat. Zhang bowed twice and asked to be taught. The Taoist pointed to the courtyard and said, "There is elixir here. You can take it." Zhang looked around but saw nothing. He was ordered to search again, but he still found nothing. The Taoist asked, "Isn't there something in the southeast corner of the courtyard?" Zhang replied, "I only saw a large pile of excrement." The Taoist then sighed and said, "You have no karmic connection. You will just have to remain in this form in the world."

The Taoist master said, "This place is very quiet. If you wish to avoid fame, you can move your family to live here together." After living there for more than a month, Zhang gained a deep understanding of the Taoist master's inner essence. One day, he went out for a walk and, after a short while, looked back to find only an empty mountain. He asked someone and learned that he was in the area outside the Great Wall city of Datong. Zhang returned home, not claiming to be a Taoist, but still performing his gold-picking act in Hangzhou. He would ride a donkey to the city in the morning and return home in the evening, instantly traversing thousands of miles. Sometimes, he would tie grass into a dragon and ride it, hanging it on the eaves of his house upon return. He often performed tricks to entertain people, and whenever he visited the market, people would gather to watch. The emperor of the Mausoleum of Taizu heard of him and summoned him to the palace, accusing him of using magic to gather crowds. Zhang replied, "Your servant does not use magic; it is just a trick." The emperor wanted to test him and asked Zhang to produce a small copper bottle from his sleeve. Zhang poured hot water over it, and a five-colored cloud emerged from the bottle's mouth, filling the courtyard. The emperor was pleased and wanted to see all of his tricks. It was then the twelfth month, and the emperor ordered him to make lotus flowers bloom. Zhang asked the emperor to go to the Jinshui River, where he requested dry lotus seeds, scattering them randomly in the pond. In an instant, lotus flowers bloomed across the pond, fragrant, beautiful, and lovable, and the emperor exclaimed in admiration. Zhang then asked for paper and cut it into a boat, placing it in the water. He stepped onto it and boarded, rowing and singing as he wandered among the flower clusters. Suddenly, the boat turned towards the shore, and Zhang immediately disappeared, and the lotus flowers also vanished. The emperor immediately ordered a search in all directions, but ultimately, he could not be found. Later, no one knew his final destination.

Sheng Yiyi, also known as Yin, with the courtesy name Qidong, was from Wujiang. In his youth, he studied medicine under the reclusive scholar Wang Bin. During the Yongle era, he traveled to the capital as a tax collector. At that time, the emperor suffered from weakness and numbness in his limbs, and the court physicians treated him for a condition called "wasting syndrome," but after many years, there was no improvement. Someone recommended Sheng Yin, who was then summoned and awaited orders at the imperial palace. A eunuch with a minor illness requested that he examine his pulse. Sheng Yin replied, "I have not yet seen the emperor, how can I treat you first?" The eunuch admired his words and reported to the emperor, saying, "This physician has great insight." The emperor then called Sheng Yin to the private hall, where Sheng Yin examined the emperor's pulse and bowed, saying, "This is a case of rheumatism." The emperor agreed, saying, "I pursued the Huns beyond the border, and for many years, I was afflicted by the cold and dampness, which led to this condition."

I say it is dampness, all the doctors were wrong, your words are correct. The medicine was administered and immediately effective, and he was then appointed to an office. Later, he served during the reigns of Emperor Renzong and Emperor Xuangzong, and both were favored. Emperor Xuangzong especially loved him, once in the presence of the emperor, he was ordered to play chess with his colleagues, and a special poem was bestowed upon him to show his unique favor. On another day, at dawn, he entered the imperial pharmacy, suddenly suffering from a severe headache and dizziness. All the doctors were at a loss, unable to diagnose the illness. The emperor ordered someone to be sought out for treatment, and a village doctor requested an audience. After administering a dose of medicine, he recovered after a short while. The emperor was surprised, summoned and asked what formula was used. He replied, "At dawn, he entered the pharmacy on an empty stomach, suddenly poisoned by the drugs. The one that can harmonize all drugs is licorice, using this to make a decoction and administer it, there is no other trick." The emperor questioned him, and indeed, he had not eaten breakfast before entering, and then greatly rewarded the person. ○ Odd Disease At the temple of Lin Dian near the Gate of Qi, there was a monk who was in his twenties and suffered from a disease caused by poisoning. He died after five years without recovery. The monk was young, handsome, and of pure and cautious character. His master deeply regretted his passing and gave him a grand burial, and even burned his remains. As the fire was raging, there was a sudden explosion, and the monk's abdomen split open, revealing a small person inside, about several inches tall, with a complete face, limbs, eyebrows, and hair, and a beautiful beard hanging down to his belly. The onlookers were amazed. His master personally told the physician Lu Du about the incident.

○ The Craftsman of Huangcun To the west of Wu Mountain lies the village of Huangcun. One night, a craftsman named Wang returned home and met a man clad in a blue robe and a white sash, resembling a low-ranking official. When Wang asked where he was going, the man replied, "I wish to go to Huangcun." The craftsman was pleased and said, "I too am returning to Huangcun. It is very good that we have found each other as companions." So they walked together for several miles. The man then pointed to a house by the roadside and said to the craftsman, "Do you also wish for food and drink? I can obtain some from there." The craftsman replied, "Very well." The man entered the house, and after a short while, came out carrying a small jar of wine and a roasted chicken. They sat on the ground and ate together. When they had finished, the man said to the craftsman, "Please stay here for a moment. I must enter this house to settle a small matter." The craftsman immediately took the jar of wine and placed it among the pile of firewood. He stood and waited. Soon, he saw a man being thrown out of the window, his hands and feet bound. Shortly after, the man leaped out of the window himself, carrying the bound figure away. His movement was as swift as flight, and soon after, the sound of weeping was heard from inside the house. The craftsman realized this was no ordinary man, and in alarm, ran back home. The next day, he went to investigate and learned that the household's master had died the previous night. He asked if anything had been lost, and they replied, "Yesterday, we held a ritual to honor the Five Saints, and we lost a jar of wine and a roasted chicken." When the craftsman told them what he had seen the previous night, they did not believe him. But when they searched the pile of firewood and found the jar, and saw the chicken bones still scattered on the ground, they finally realized that the man had been a soul-guide from the underworld.

The Townsman Wu Xun The townsman Wu Xun borrowed fifteen hundred taels of silver from his wife's maternal aunt, Zhou, without establishing a contract or deed, which was, in fact, a deceit. After a long time, he defaulted on the debt and did not repay it. Zhou reported the matter to the authorities, and on behalf of Wu Xun, bribed officials in an attempt to win the case, but failed to achieve justice. Zhou became angry and offered to the city god, daily praying and saying, "Let those who seek wealth have red worms crawl out and white worms gather around." This was a curse phrase from the Wu region. Subsequently, Wu Xun's wife fell ill, often feeling an unusual itch on her face. When she scratched, she found something like tendons, which moved slowly and wriggled, happening several times a day. The color was not uniform, sometimes red, sometimes white, but her face had no visible wounds. People believed it was a karmic retribution. [The above two stories are from Zi Yuan's family.] The *Beidou Jing* During the reign of Emperor Hongzhi in the year Jiwei, Wei Jingqing from Kunshan suffered from a skin disease. A doctor mistakenly pricked his leg, causing it to swell and become painful. After a long time, it became difficult for him to walk. One night, he dreamed of an old man with a white beard who told him, "Your illness is like this, do you know how to recite the *Beidou Jing*?" Wu Xun asked, "What does reciting it mean?" The old man replied, "It not only cures illness but also prolongs life." After waking from the dream, Wu Xun did not believe it much. The next morning, a Taoist priest came to inquire about his illness. He asked Wu Xun, "What are the benefits of reciting the *Beidou Jing*?" Wu Xun replied, "It cures illness and prolongs life." The Taoist priest's words matched the dream. Wu Xun then realized and began to recite the *Beidou Jing*, doing so three times a day. After a long time, his foot healed. This year, he reached eighty years old, and his teeth and hair were like those of a young man. He still recites the scripture daily without stopping. [This is said by my sister's husband, Yao Sichen.]

### 人瑞 Yan Sheng, a scholar from Ruyi, had a wife who gave birth to three sons from one breast. When Lu Zhong Renjie, the governor of Guangzhou, was passing through the county on official business, he stopped by Yan Sheng's home. Yan Sheng called out his three sons to greet him. They were all graceful and handsome, and their appearance and clothing were almost indistinguishable. One had a hair bun on the left side, one on the right side, and one in the middle of his head. Yan Sheng explained, "They are all twelve years old now, and because they look so similar, I had their hair cut into different styles to tell them apart."
