Later, Yan Sheng brought his three sons to the governor to pay their respects and said, "I heard that there is a woman in this area who gave birth to three daughters at the same time as my sons. I would like to arrange marriages for them." Governor Renjie found this remarkable and arranged a meeting with the woman. He then arranged the marriages. ### 犬精 During the Hongzhi era, a man in Yutai County, Yanzhou, kept a very tame white dog. Whenever the man went out, the dog always accompanied him. One day, the man went to trade in a distant place and left, but the dog disappeared. After a couple of days, the man returned home. His wife asked him what had happened, and he replied, "I met bandits on the road, and they took all my wealth. Fortunately, I managed to escape with my life." His wife did not suspect anything and continued to live with him for a year. Then, his true husband returned, and both men looked exactly the same, making it impossible to tell them apart. The two men argued over who was the real husband, and the wife and neighbors could not determine the truth. They reported the matter to the county magistrate. The magistrate arrested both men and brought them to court, but he had no idea how to proceed and placed both men in jail. A low-ranking soldier heard about the case and told his wife. She said, "It is not difficult to distinguish. The one who returned first is probably the dog spirit. To verify this, you should look at the marks of claw injuries on the breasts of the wife. If there are any, then that is the sign."
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

Zeng Ziyuan Zeng Ziyuan from Tahe was old in the examination hall. In the year 1478 (Chenghua Wuxu), he was almost sixty years old when he finally became the top scholar in the world. During this examination, the officials in the palace examination hall spent the entire day reading the exam papers but did not find any outstanding works. Finally, Wan An from Meizhou obtained Zeng Ziyuan's paper and quickly praised and admired it, showing it to everyone. Everyone who saw it was impressed and admired, saying that the text should be ranked first, but they had not yet seen his appearance. According to the old custom, the day before the results were announced, all the candidates were gathered in the hall of the cabinet of the Ministry of Rites, where their names were called and their appearance was examined. When Mr. Wen Kang looked at Zeng Ziyuan closely, he found him to be tall and handsome, especially different from others. Happily, he said to the officials, "We have found the right person." When the announcement was made, Zeng Ziyuan had an unattractive appearance with many whiskers, which was different from before. Mr. Wen Kang was surprised. He took Zeng Ziyuan's paper and read it, which was also quite ordinary without any remarkable statements. He showed it to the officials, and they all agreed. Then they greatly admired and exclaimed, thinking that it was helped by a divine force. This story was told by Zhang Tai, the son of a magistrate from Changzhou, because the top scholar had studied under the magistrate before.

○腾冲龙 During a certain year of the Zhengde era (the exact year is unknown), an earthquake struck the Dragon Guard in Tengchong, Yunnan. Initially, the ground moved several dozen degrees per day, gradually reducing to several dozen degrees, and later to one or two degrees, lasting for half a year before it ceased. A mountain collapsed into a plain, and dozens of households in a village were all buried underground. The number of people who died from the earthquake was too numerous to count. The people had no safe place to live, so they built temporary shelters in open areas to protect themselves. A scholar named Wang Cheng had all his family members stay in the garden behind the house. At midnight, a dragon appeared on an eight-legged table in the garden, with head, horns, tail, and claws all present. Its color was white, as if painted with powder, and touching its scales felt like they were stinging the hand, but it did not feel like it was moving. After several days, many people came to see it. The Wang family was afraid it would bring disaster to their home, so they took dog blood and smeared it on the dragon, and it disappeared. [Told by a schoolmaster from Changzhou.]

○ Meteorite During the Chenghua period, a star fell into the courtyard of Ma, the Chief Magistrate, in Juxian County, Shandong. When it first struck the ground, its light was brilliant, yet its body was soft and rotten, like powdered plaster. Ma's family struck it with a staff, and the staff sank into the ground, forming a hole. After a while, it gradually hardened and became a stone. 【Reported by Jiao, the Deputy Magistrate of Jiaxing.】 ○ You Hongyuan A man named You Hongyuan lived in the eastern part of the city. His neighbor, the Zhang family, had a female servant who was fond of him, and they exchanged secret messages, hoping to spend their lives together. Eventually, she married You as a concubine. You's wife was jealous and cruel, and she mistreated the woman daily, even resorting to various forms of malevolent magic, invoking divine curses to cause her to die quickly. Not long after, the concubine indeed fell ill and died. A year later, You's wife also fell ill and could not recover. She lay weak and listless in bed, and the family grew tired of tending to her. They then called in a village woman to stay with her overnight. That night, a woman in a red skirt and green robe slowly and gracefully walked to You’s wife’s bedside. Looking closely, she was recognized as the concubine. Pointing at her body, she scolded, saying: 【Originally, the character “谇” was missing in the phrase “指妻身谇曰,” but it is said to have been added in the Kufu edition.】 “My fate was not yet to die, yet you have cursed me greatly, causing me to die prematurely—this is cruel and unjust. I have already brought my case before the Lord of the Mountains and have received justice. I will now pursue you for your life. On the evening of tomorrow, I will cause you unbearable pain in your waist, and you will surely die.” Having spoken these words, she vanished. The woman had often visited the You household and was familiar with the concubine’s appearance. She recognized that the woman was wearing the clothes she had worn at the time of her burial. Hearing these words, she was greatly frightened and could not sleep. At dawn, she left immediately.

Two more days passed, and when news came, it was confirmed that Far's wife had indeed died the previous night, crying out in pain from her waist. The old woman then detailed what she had seen. Upon hearing this, Far felt sympathy for the concubine's injustice, but he was also worried about his wife's previous vows and written promises, which were numerous and would certainly be a burden to him. He had always practiced Daoism and thus began to recite the Jade Emperor Sutra daily, hundreds of times. He planned to conduct religious rites, choosing the person to lead the ceremonies, and prayed to the True Warrior (Zhenwu) using the yuánjiào for divination. Several famous people were consecutively named, but none were chosen; finally, the Daoist Master Shen from the Xuanmiao Temple was selected. As a result, large-scale water and land Buddhist ceremonies were conducted for several days and nights, with utmost sincerity and purity, in order to atone for past mistakes. Later, Far fell ill and, in a state of confusion, saw bailiffs holding a summons to take him away. Far followed them, and the path was dark and gloomy, leading to a grand gate where a sign read, "The Court of the Lord of the Mountains." Upon entering, the bailiffs seized Far and made him kneel in the courtyard. The king on the throne shouted and questioned, "Your wife accused you of being involved in curses, leading to her untimely death. Do you know of your guilt?" Far bowed his head and apologized, saying it was not his fault.

The king summoned his attendants to bring Yu Hongyuan's wife and concubines to testify. They obeyed the command and left. Shortly after, they arrived, all shackled and bound, kneeling at the foot of the steps. The king ordered a debate, which was very difficult. After a long time, the wife's arguments were refuted, and the concubine clearly stated that the blame did not lie with the husband. The king was greatly angered and scolded his wife, saying, "You are the principal wife; you were jealous and cruel, and you forcibly invoked the spirits while alive; after death, you lied again and falsely interfered with the officials. Can your guilt be pardoned?" He immediately ordered the soldiers to escort her to Fengdu. He still released the concubine, sentencing her to the case of rebirth. The king called Yu, saying, "Although you were unaware, this woman's actions and curses are documented in many documents. How can they be resolved?" Yu had not yet answered when a green-robed judge standing next to the king's desk said to the king, "Documents have arrived from the high realm, and they have been settled." The king ordered the officials to inspect, and they opened a box, which contained documents in piles. An official drew out a scroll and presented it to the king, who read it and ordered it to be given to Yu. In a state of fear, Yu did not have time to read it carefully, but only saw several lines of red words at the end of the paper. The king said, "Documents sent to Fengdu should say 'present,' but now it says 'consult.' It is wrong."

This is although the fault of the practitioners, but it is also your responsibility.」 Far did not know how to respond. Soon, two divine generals clad in armor appeared in the courtyard. Far looked at them and saw one was Guan Gong and the other was the divine general Wang Lingguan. Lingguan looked at Wang and said, 「This is also a minor mistake and not worth questioning.」 Wang nodded in agreement. Lingguan kicked Far's back with his foot and said, 「Go away.」 Far was then released and managed to escape. He walked through the dark and mysterious place, with the road turning several times, and entered an office. Six monks were sitting there and called out to Far to question him. They said, 「Return, you should go to the Five Pestilence Office.」 Far said, 「I do not know what the Five Pestilence Officials are, but I have only heard of the primordial qi.」 He then detailed the reason for the high-ranking deity's pardon of sins. One of the monks said, 「But do you know that following the Dao and forgetting about Buddhism is a problem? The consequences here are also not trivial. You have now returned home, you should urgently invite six highly virtuous monks for longevity, recite six volumes of the Lotus Sutra and dedicate the merit, only then can you eliminate past sins.」 They ordered him to be released, and he was able to live. He had been dead for more than a day. He immediately invited six monks, all over seventy years old, to recite the required number of sutras. To this day, every first day of the lunar month, he regularly recites scriptures and confesses sins, even when very busy. 【 Provided by Deng Kai.】

The King of Cats Zhu Zhang, the Provincial Governor of Fujian, was originally from Jiaozhi but lived in Suzhou. In the early years of the Jingtai era, he was demoted to the position of Yancheng in Zanglang, Shaanxi. An envoy from the Western regions presented a cat as tribute, passing through the postal station where Zhu provided hospitality. The envoy wrote a message asking what was special about the cat and why it was offered as tribute. The envoy suggested, "To know its uniqueness, let us test it tonight." The cat was placed in a heavy iron cage with two layers, which was then put inside an empty house. The next morning, Zhu found that dozens of rats had gathered outside the cage and all had died. The envoy remarked, "This cat is so powerful that rats from several miles away come and die at its feet." Indeed, it was the king of cats. [Recorded by Xie Rui, the Instructor.]

The Silver Rabbit of Zhaoling On the Nine-Zong Mountain in Shaanxi, lies the tomb of Emperor Taizong of Tang, known as Zhaoling. Once, a villager from Lique County went to the mountain to fetch firewood and saw a white rabbit suddenly appear from the grass, which was unusual, so he chased it. The rabbit jumped into a large hole, and the villager, unaware, also fell in. He then found himself in a tunnel, which was quite dark, with dozens of copper pots stacked on the side, all filled with oil and set up with mechanisms for oil flow. The bottom pot had a lingering fire, but the hole was blocked, so the oil could not flow down, and the fire was flickering and about to go out. The villager cleared the blockage, and the fire reignited. The rabbit he had chased was actually a silver rabbit, with an inscription on it saying, "Clear the fire, and you will be granted a silver rabbit." The villager saw a vast accumulation of gold, silver, pearls, and shells, all beautifully arranged, and he bowed twice and requested, "I am poor, and the gift is not enough to sustain me, I wish for more." He then took whatever he wanted, carrying it out, but became lost, unable to distinguish even a step, so he abandoned it, and then a new exit suddenly opened up. He carried the rabbit out, and the tunnel door closed behind him, leaving only a tiny crack. When he returned home, his neighbors, hearing about it, rushed to the tomb, following the crack and digging, but the cave was deep and endless. The matter was discovered, and everyone involved was arrested, with the villager almost being punished.

The Inspection Office in Sanyuan County was always plagued by strange occurrences, and those who lived there died, so officials dared not enter. Scholar Liang Ze, proud of his spirit, often told his friends, "I can stay here." His friends bet money on it, and Liang agreed. At night, he entered and sat in the main hall. At the third drumbeat, the moonlight was bright, and he heard someone whispering in the eaves, as if pushing each other forward, but they did not come. Liang then shouted loudly, "Why not come quickly?" Soon, three people knelt in a row in the courtyard, the one slightly ahead dressed in green, the next in yellow, and one in white, their appearance and color could not be identified. Liang scolded, "Old demon, dare to harm many people." The one in green replied, "We are not daring to do so; it is those who see who are scared to death." Liang asked, "What are you?" The one in green said, "I am a pen."

The person asked, "Where are you staying?" and replied, "In the third tile groove on the roof of the gatehouse." When asked about the person in yellow clothing, he hesitated and did not speak. The person in green clothing answered on his behalf, saying, "That is the golden hairpin, under the locust tree in the courtyard." When asked about the person in white clothing, he said, "That is my sword, under the base of the pillar on the east side of the hall." Zé asked, "Are you here to cause us trouble?" They all replied, "We dare not." They jointly presented a piece of paper and said, "This is the biography of this person; now we are informing you, so that you may know in advance." Zé received it and waved his hand, saying, "Go away." The three items were each thrown to the places they mentioned and all disappeared at once. Zé then lay down. Until dawn, his friends thought he would surely die. They came to see him and were surprised. Zé told them about what he had seen, but they did not believe him. They left and brought people with shovels. They searched for them in turn and found all three items. They took out the paper, which was like old money, with no words on it. By evening, when they looked at it under the light, the traces were clearly visible. From then on, the office was free from harm and fear. Later, Zé passed the imperial examinations and became an imperial censor. During the Chenghua period, he was sent to inspect Shandong. He was demoted for an error in supervising the examinations. In the end, it happened just as written on the paper.

Black Slave Black Slave was a servant or laborer under the Shaanxi Censorate. During the reign of Emperor Hongwu, there was an official who was due to pay homage to the emperor in the capital. He registered the names of his attendants, and Black Slave was among them. Suddenly, one night, Black Slave fell ill and died. The official was about to choose a replacement and remake the registry. That night, he suddenly saw Black Slave kneeling and saying, "There is no need to change the registry. Even though I am dead, I can still serve you. The difficulty lies in crossing the Tongguan Pass. Just shout my name loudly outside the pass, and I will come out." The official agreed to this. As they traveled, all the relay stations along the way had all necessities prepared. When asked about this, they said, "Just now, a servant reported that you were coming, so we were ordered to prepare everything." The official asked about his appearance and was told, "He was fat, short, and black." The official realized that it was Black Slave. After crossing the pass, he shouted Black Slave's name, and soon a ghostly voice said, "I have already crossed the pass." Black Slave accompanied the official from Shaanxi to Huai'an, but then refused to continue, saying, "The city god of the capital is strict, and I dare not enter the capital. I will stop here to wait for you."

The official was sent to court, and the matter was handed over to officials. After a long time, the Black Chief submitted to the residents, saying, "I, the Black Chief, will receive sacrifices here." The villagers unanimously believed him and built a temple for him. They relied on the witch's predictions of good and bad fortune, which were very accurate. Prayers and thanks were offered every day without fail, and the witch accumulated what was given up to several hundred gold pieces. After a year, the matter was cleared up, and he was restored to office. As he was about to cross the river, the Black Chief gave instructions to the witch, saying, "On a certain day, a certain official will arrive. Prepare a feast and offer rewards. Hand over everything, otherwise, I will punish you." The witch had no choice but to go to meet him, offering the gold. The day before, the official had already seen the Black Chief come to tell him, "Be careful not to reveal my name, fear that it will not bring good fortune." The witch went back to tell the villagers, and they went together to the city隍 to lodge a complaint. The temple was destroyed, and the spiritual response ceased.

The Temple of the Scorpion Demon In Xi'an, there is a temple known as the Temple of the Scorpion Demon, where a large scorpion statue is placed between the beams. It is said that at the beginning of the dynasty, there was a woman who was usually not wise. She died of an illness but came back to life, and then she became bright and quick-witted, becoming famous for her knowledge of literature and history. At that time, a high-ranking official had just lost his wife, and a guest mentioned the woman, leading to the official marrying her. After a month, while the official was handling affairs, he needed something and sent a servant to the private office to fetch it. When he called for his wife, there was no response. Instead, he saw an old scorpion as big as a carriage wheel lying on the bed. The servant was shocked and came out to report it, but the official did not believe it and scolded it as a lie. The servant pleaded, saying, "On another day when the official descends the hall, I hope you don't make a sound, cover it discreetly, and it will surely be seen."

As he spoke, indeed, an old scorpion was seen lying on the bed, and in the course of a moment, it transformed into a beautiful woman. Though she hesitated and spoke with a shy demeanor, she suddenly vanished. That night, when all was quiet, she emerged and bowed before the light, saying, "I am originally a scorpion demon. I came to see you through my connections, not to deceive or bewitch you, but to make a request. If you do not completely reject me, I dare to reveal my true feelings." He agreed, and she said, "In the past, I was a demon and offended the underworld, but I was saved by the Great Bodhisattva Guanyin, who spared my life. Because of this, I took on the form of a woman and was fortunate enough to serve you. I hoped you would build a temple to repay the virtue of the Great Bodhisattva. Now that my shameful deed has been revealed, I hope for your compassion." The provincial governor nodded in agreement, and the woman disappeared. On another day, he ordered the officials to build the temple, which still stands to this day. [This story was told by Zhang Xun Dao. Zhang was from Gan Prefecture, Shaanxi Province.]

Hu Hong, a Confucian scholar from Ningbo with the courtesy name Renzhi, learned the art of divination from Zhang Sheng, a fortune-teller from Jiangxi, in his youth. He studied diligently and worked hard without tiring. In the early years of Zhengtong, he traveled to Hangzhou and met an old man who called himself a native of Bian and was deeply knowledgeable about the principles of the Yi (Book of Changes). Hu Hong followed him and eventually mastered all his secrets, thus becoming famous for his divination skills. In the early years of Jingtai, he followed Zhang, a censor-general, to campaign against the rebel Deng Maoseven in Fujian. In the army, his predictions were often remarkably accurate. Later he went to Suzhou, where many scholars and officials sought his advice on good or bad omens. The Confucian scholar Du Qiong, in his old age, had lost several children. When he cast the divination, he got the first line of the Ding hexagram, which said: "The child line is strong; there will be two sons." Zhao, the censor, cast the divination and got the Shī line of the Kūn hexagram, which said: "You will pass the imperial examination, serve in the censorate, be dismissed from office on a certain date, and have a son." Zhu, the provincial official, cast the divination and got the second and fifth lines of the Bǐ hexagram moving, which said: "A celebration between ruler and minister; you will surely serve as a close attendant in the Yellow Gate and later be transferred to a major province." All these predictions came true. Yang, the minister, cast the divination and got the sixth line of the Fù hexagram, which said: "You will have a remarkable encounter by mid-life. If you hold a third-rank position, you will live to ninety; if a second-rank position, you will live four or five years less. Your son will also receive an official salary."

Not long after, he was indeed promoted to the position of Grand Minister of Rites, due to his former favor when the emperor was still a prince. He died at the age of eighty-five. His son was also granted the position of magistrate of Wuxian County through hereditary privilege. In countless cases, the predictions were accurate. He was especially skilled at divination through characters. Once, he encountered two scholars on the road who were about to take the provincial examination and asked him about their fortunes. He said, "One of the two will pass, while the other will be hindered by mourning." Neither of them believed him. Soon after, one of them lost his father and could not take the examination, while the other was recommended. Someone asked him about this, and he said, "When the one who lost his father asked, there happened to be someone drawing water and passing by. The water and the standing figure formed the character '泣' (to weep), so I knew that he would have the sorrow of mourning. When the one who was recommended asked, there happened to be someone standing beside him, forming the character '位' (position), so I knew that he would pass." There was also a scholar who was taking the examination and wrote the character "串" to ask him about his fortunes. He said, "You will not only pass the examination but will also be selected as a Jinshi. The character '串' means 'two passes.'" Another scholar heard this and also wrote the character "串" to ask. He said, "Do not speak of success in the examination; you should be concerned about illness." Later, one of them passed the examination in consecutive stages, while the other suffered from a serious illness. He said, "The first question was asked out of curiosity, while the second was asked deliberately, and thus the result was different. Adding the character '心' (heart) to '串' means that you should be concerned about illness." His methods were largely like this.

**Prosperous Lotus** In the year 1438 of the Zhengtong era, in the pool of the Confucian school in Wuxian, a single lotus stem bore three flowers. Zhou Dunyi, the governor of the region, observed this and remarked, "There will be someone who deserves it." The following year, Shí Pán, a student of the Confucian school, passed the imperial examination as the top scholar, fulfilling Zhou's prediction. In the year 1481 of the Chenghua era, in the pool of the Confucian school in Suzhou, a single lotus stem bore two flowers. That same year, sweet dew descended upon a peach tree in the school, and in the following year, Wú Kuān, a scholar, passed the imperial examination as the top scholar. It was also said that a stone from Qionglong Mountain moved mysteriously, and it was predicted that the状元 would return. In the year 1506 of the Hongzhi era, Zhū Xīzhōu, a student of the Confucian school, passed the imperial examination as the top scholar. The previous year, during a storm, a large stone moved on its own in Qionglong Mountain, and at that time, Zhū Xīzhōu was still a student in the Confucian school.

**Case of the Human Demon** The Censorate reported an extraordinary case involving a man disguised as a woman. The official report from the Jinzhou Prefecture of Zhending, Zhili, stated: The accused, Sang Chong, confessed to being the nephew of Li Dagang, a military officer from Dongdu, Wenshui, and was adopted by San Mao from Yuci County as a stepson. From a young age, he was sold to San Mao. In the first year of the Chenghua era, he discovered that Gu Cai, a deceased resident of Shan County, Datong Prefecture, had disguised himself as a woman and taught women's ways of life everywhere, secretly engaging in sexual acts for eighteen years without being exposed. Chong wanted to imitate him and went to Wang Chang's home in the south gate of Datong to find Gu Cai, who formally became his teacher. He had his eyebrows and face shaved, parted his hair into three sections, wore a specific hairstyle, and dressed as a woman. There, he learned women's crafts, designing and cutting patterns, sewing the tops of shoes, preparing meals, and other tasks, then thanked and returned home. Later, people from Beijia Mountain, Ren Mao and Zhang Hu, Zhang Dada from Gucheng County, Wang Daxi from Majian Village, and Ren Fang, Sun Cheng, and Sun Yuan from Wenshui County came to see Chong and learned the previous situation.

Chong told everyone: "Be cautious when entering and leaving each household. If anything happens, don't implicate me." They then dispersed and completed their tasks. In the third month of the third year of the Chenghua era, Chong had been away from home for ten years, with no other means of livelihood, and had been dedicated to plotting evil. He had traveled through Datong, Pingyang, Taiyuan, Zhending, Baoding, Shuntian, Shunde, Huan, Jinan, and Dongchang prefectures, as well as Shuozhou, Yongnian, and Dagou states, a total of 45 prefectures, states, and counties, and 78 villages, towns, and markets. Everywhere, he carefully inquired about well-bred, beautiful women, devising a plan to pretend to be a runaway beggar woman, first going to poor small households to work. After one or two days, he had them spread the word and introduce her as a seamstress, meeting at night to rest together, telling lies and pretending to play, coaxing them to agree, and secretly committing adultery. If there was someone who was upright and refused, he would wait until very late and use a trick, taking a chicken egg with him, removing the green, and using seven peaches (or other items). In one version of the text, "青" is written as "清" and "卒" is written as "辛."

In this way, for ten years, he committed adultery with 182 virtuous women, never once being discovered. On the 13th day of the 7th month in the 13th year of the Chenghua era, at the hour of the rooster, he arrived at the home of a scholar named Gao Xuan in the village of Nie in Jinzhou, Zhen Ding Prefecture, claiming to be the concubine of Zhang Lin from Zhao Prefecture, having fled from her husband's beatings and seeking shelter. The person remained in the southern room for lodging. Until the first watch of the night, Gao Xuan's son-in-law Zhao Wenju secretly entered the room to commit adultery. The person pushed and beat him, and was grabbed by Zhao Wenju and thrown down onto the kang (heating platform) and held down. Zhao Wenju felt for breasts but found none, and felt for testicles and found them, and the person was captured and sent to Jinzhou. The interrogation confirmed the previous statements were true.

Referring to the criminal's intent being extraordinary, similar to the ten grave offenses, and not covered by the law, except for listing the names of the criminal and the women he had intercourse with, and arresting them firmly and taking them to the judicial authorities for questioning, we request that the judicial authorities be instructed to sentence the criminal to a severe punishment. A memorial was presented, and in accordance with the imperial edict: "The Censorate has reviewed and reported." As the emperor has decreed. In accordance with the decree. We have examined the case of Sang Chong, who deserves death and more. As he confessed, Ren Mao and others were all skilled in the occult arts, engaging in promiscuous sexual acts. We intend to sentence Sang Chong to death. And we instruct the circuit censors in various regions to apprehend Ren Mao and others and bring them to the capital, punishing them uniformly, to warn future generations. As for the women involved, they were all bewitched by Sang Chong, and their promiscuity was not of their own volition, and involves many people, so they should also be exempted from further investigation. On the 20th day of the 11th month of the 13th year of the Chenghua era, the Grand Secretary and Left Vice-President of the Censorate, Wang, and others presented a memorial. On the 22nd, they presented it at the Hall of Supreme Harmony. In accordance with the imperial edict: "Correct. This person's crime is truly vile, harmful to public morals, and should be subjected to the death penalty by slicing, no need for further reporting. Ren Mao and others, seven in number, must be apprehended with urgency, and brought to the capital." As the emperor has decreed. [Taken from an old copy of a public document found in a friend's home.] ● The Tenth Scroll of the Geng Si Compilation Omens of Ascension** **Sincerity, the Marquis of** **The Day for Raising the Beam

**Yi Xiang Xing** Zhang Chao** **Wang Guan** **Dou Shen Jiu Zai** **Zhang Shi Zi Ru Ming** **Yang Shaoqing** **Zhang Mengjie** **Yan Shangshu** **Mu Yao** **Tang ■〈Wang Si〉** **Zhang Dudian** **Pi Nue Jing** **Bi Long** **Jiu Wei Gui** **Poyang Shui Shen** **Zong San She Ren** **Ma Shaoshi** **Zhang Yushi Shen Zheng Ji **Chapter: The Omen of Ascension** In the seventeenth year of the Hongzhi era, a commoner in Suzhou's Zhu Zhu Lane (also known as Drilling Turtle Lane) died of illness. Upon arriving in the underworld, he met the Lord of Hell. The records were inspected, and it was said, "Your lifespan has not yet ended." He was then allowed to return. The palaces, utensils, and attire in the underworld were all like those in the human world, but it was strange that the officials and servants were all dressed in mourning attire. He privately asked about this, and they replied, "The emperor in the mortal world has passed away, so we are in mourning." The commoner returned to life and privately told his relatives about the experience. In the following May, the emperor abdicated. According to the *Xuan Guai Lu*, during the late Yuanhe period, the Inspector of Gao'an, Xin Gongping, encountered officials of the underworld welcoming the emperor. He accompanied them into the main hall, where he saw the emperor ascend into a carriage, escorted by soldiers and horses. Several months later, there were tears of mourning. Today, what this commoner saw also occurred after half a year, and the events were roughly the same.

Chengyi, the Marquis of Loyalty** The meritorious service of Chengyi, the Marquis of Loyalty, follows in the footsteps of Wen Cheng, yet his achievements are rarely recorded. As for the source of his learning and the teachers he followed, no one can speak of them. [Some say he studied under Huang Chuyang of Jiujiang; further investigation is needed.] My fellow townsman, Gu Pian, who knew Qingtian County, traveled with Old Master Liu and is a descendant of Chengyi. He understands the family learning. He told me: When Chengyi had not yet achieved fame, he knew that Qingtian Mountain had supernatural phenomena. He would hold a book in his hand, sit facing the mountain, and never take his eyes off it. After a year, suddenly two doors opened on the cliff. He quickly threw down his book and rushed inside, hearing someone warn him, "This place is poisonous and evil, do not enter." But he paid no heed and pushed forward with great effort. Inside, the sunlight was bright, and there was a small stone room. On the wall, he saw seven large characters: "This stone was broken by Liu Ji." He was delighted and pulled a large stone to push it, breaking it with a single touch. He found a stone box containing four ancient military treatises, which he carried out. As soon as he turned his foot, the wall closed again as it was. Upon returning, he recited the texts and became very familiar with them, but still did not fully understand their essence. Thus, he frequently visited deep mountains and grand temples to seek out extraordinary individuals.

After a long time, he entered a mountain temple and saw an old Taoist sitting by a table reading a book. Recognizing him as a hermit, he bowed and asked for instruction, but the Taoist ignored him. The official pleaded earnestly, and the Taoist handed him the book he was reading and said, "If you can recite this in ten days, then you may proceed; if not, leave." The book was two inches thick, and the official memorized half of it in one night. The Taoist exclaimed, "You are a genius!" Thus, the official obtained the Taoist's teachings. Later, he assisted Emperor Gao. Once, he mentioned the Taoist to the emperor. The emperor ordered him to be summoned by imperial decree to the capital. The Taoist was nearly eighty years old but appeared very youthful. The emperor ordered him to choose with Cheng Yi and Zhang Tianguan the site for the imperial palace. Initially, they did not communicate with each other, but later they all submitted plans, and the measurements were identical. The emperor wanted to keep him, but it was not possible, so he was released to return to the mountain, and his final fate is unknown. The Taoist also said that when the official was gravely ill, he told his son, "Our family's title of nobility will be cut off, but after five generations, we should obtain a military position. From this point, it can be passed down." When the official's grandson succeeded to the title, it was eventually abolished. In the early years of the Hongzhi era, the emperor ordered that the descendants of the official be appointed as the commander of Chuzhou, exactly five generations later. At that time, the legitimate grandson was imprisoned for a crime, and the officials removed his shackles and dressed him in official robes, which people considered a rare opportunity. [Gu Pin told this story to his son Yuan.]

The Auspicious Day and Time for Raising the Main Beam Once, the Venerable Master passed through Wu Gate and, in the middle of the night, heard the sounds of "Ya He" (a traditional chant used during the beam-raising ceremony). He asked those around him, and they replied, "A household is raising the main beam." He then inquired about the family's wealth and the size of their house, and they answered, "It is a poor family with just a few rooms." The Master sighed and said, "The skill of selecting the day and time for such an event is truly refined." He added, "Alas, it will not last long." When those around him asked why, the Master explained, "On this day and at this time, raising the main beam is the most auspicious, and the household will prosper greatly. However, this prosperity will only occur if the household is large and wealthy. If a poor family suddenly becomes wealthy, they will likely rebuild the house. Once the good fortune leaves, the decline is inevitable." Indeed, the household's financial situation gradually improved over time, and within a few years, they accumulated a million units of currency. They then demolished and expanded the house. Soon after, they returned to their previous state of poverty.

plaintext A Poem of the Righteous Elephant In the era of Hongwu, Lin Ziyu, the Minister of Rites, once composed this poem: There was an elephant, an elephant, who came to the imperial capital, hesitating by the great river. Lured across, it was presented to the Son of Heaven, but it did not kneel and bow like the other elephants. The elephant handler urged it to kneel, but it angrily struck him with its trunk. When offered wine, it refused to drink. When fed, it refused to eat. For ten days, it stood firm, enduring hunger and thirst, shaking its head, shedding tears, and expressing anger and sorrow. The emperor commanded it to be killed, but the officials were helpless and dared not slaughter it. The guards summoned brave warriors, who donned armor and wielded long spears. The battle with the elephant lasted a long time without success, but the soldiers eventually triumphed, and the elephant fell. Recalling the emperor's frequent tours, the elephant had always been part of the ceremonial procession, adorned with coral and pearls, dressed in beautiful brocade and red felt. The imperial seal was carried in a purple clay box, and the grand music followed the emperor. The seal returned to the desert, and the dragon returned to the Lake of the Dead. Thus, the old elephant's heart, coming from the south, vowed to die and become a skeleton. Alas, you who feed on the emperor's bounty, are useless despite your seven-foot-tall bodies. Once high-ranking officials in white jade halls, now reduced to lowly status. Alas, you who feed on the emperor's bounty, are no better than the simple food given to animals. How pure the elephant, how corrupt you. The emperor governs the world with ease and authority, allowing the elephant to die while preserving its noble character. Alas, such an elephant was never seen before! ```

**Zhang Cao Wei Xin** Zhang Cao Wei Xin, a scholar from Suzhou, died of a cold illness on the twenty-eighth day of the twelfth month in the year of Wu Yin, at the age of forty-two. His daughter, grieving her father's death, cried out in anguish and collapsed, only to revive after a long time. She reported seeing her father dressed in a red robe and holding a yellow canopy, followed by two others in green robes and canopies, all riding sedan chairs, with many attendants accompanying them as they proceeded with shouts and commands. The daughter, seeing her father in the sedan chair, called out to ask where he was going. He replied, "I am now the governor of Quzhou, and I am rushing to take office on New Year's Day." The daughter, with tears in her eyes, asked, "Father, now that you are in office, mother and I are left alone and without support. Why didn't you take her along with you?" Zhang Cao also wept, saying, "Not yet. Your mother is destined to live to seventy-five, and at that time, I will come to take her myself. I am there, but I lack attendants. The woman from the Wang family who runs the sock shop next door is kind and wise, and I wish to marry her." He then pointed to two men and said, "These two are joint governors or deputy magistrates. One is from Kunshan, surnamed Zhang, and the other is from Taicang, surnamed Wang. They are all scholars. We were selected together and became colleagues, and now we are all taking office." After saying this, he sped away. At that time, the Wang family girl was also very ill, and not long after, she died. One of my friends, who was close to Zhang Cao, went to offer condolences and heard the daughter speak thus.

Wang Guan, courtesy name Yizhi, was originally from Sichuan but had his household registered with the Jinyiwei and resided in the capital. He passed the imperial examination in the Dingwei year of the Chenghua era and served as the magistrate of □□ County. After a year in office, he was known for his integrity and ability. One day, he suddenly told his wife Xu that, "I will become the City God of this place and will soon have to part from you." His wife was startled and asked, "Are you insane?" Wang replied, "No, last night I dreamed that the Emperor sent a messenger with a command. I excused myself because of my many family responsibilities and unfinished career, but it was not allowed, so I must go. The deadline is tomorrow night." He then called his son Yongnian and instructed him, "Be good to your mother and strive to be a virtuous person." By the next night, after several hours had passed, he dressed in formal attire, ascended the hall, summoned the officials to beat the drum, and gathered the officials.

The official reported, "It is late at night and not the proper time." But Guan did not listen. After the drumming was finished, all the officials gathered. Guan calmly said, "I am fortunate to work with all of you. Today, I have been appointed as the City God by the emperor, and I will no longer be able to interact with you. I deeply appreciate your kindness and dare to entrust my wife and children to you. The small salary is enough for preparation, but please provide some assistance so that I may return to my hometown." The officials were just startled. Guan stood up and bowed twice, saying, "I am not crazy now; I will leave immediately." After saying this, he soon claimed to have a headache and then closed his eyes and passed away. By morning, his appearance was still lifelike. The officials protected his wife and children and returned them to the capital. [The physician Chen Xien, Guan's sister's son, recounted this story.] **The Worship of the Fighting God to Save from Disaster** The Qian family in Xipi Village, Changshu County, was a large clan. In the third year of the Zhengde era (1506), several branches of the family were burned down in succession, and the fire lasted for three nights before it was extinguished. Among the ruins, a small pavilion with three rooms stood alone, known as the "Small Fourth Branch." A widow and her daughter-in-law lived together in the pavilion. When the fire was at its fiercest, smoke and flames surrounded them, and the two women were in a desperate situation with no means to escape. They had always worshipped the Fighting God, so they knelt down and prayed for help. Soon, they saw seven figures in red clothes standing under the eaves, raising their sleeves to disperse the fire. The seven figures suddenly disappeared. The widow and her daughter-in-law stood up and bowed, only to find that there was not a single roof beam left on all sides. [Pang Jin recounted this story.]

The Son of the Zhang Family Enters the Underworld Grand Censor Zhang Ximing Xizai, a native of Yunnan, had a youngest brother who was twelve or thirteen years old and died of an illness, but his body did not turn cold, and the family did not dare to bury him; three days later, he opened his eyes and came back to life. The mother asked him why this had happened, and he replied: "While I was sick, I felt dazed and unaware of myself. I only felt that two officials held me tightly as I walked in the main thoroughfare, where there were people, houses, markets, and shops, just like in the world of the living. I arrived at a government office, whose appearance was very lowly, and my father and uncle stood together at the door. They saw me and called out, asking why I had come. I replied: 'I was just now at home when two officials led me here.' My father and uncle were both happy and sad, and they asked about the safety of the family and their livelihood in great detail. I answered them one by one. My father said: 'Do not worry, son. Your life is not yet finished. When you get to the previous office, you will be able to be released back.' He also gave a warning, saying: 'The people ahead will give you soup or water, but do not eat it. If you eat it, you will not be able to return.' I respectfully agreed. The official led me toward one office, but the head of the office had not come out, and the heads and faces of the officials and soldiers in the courtyard were all strange and frightening."

I saw a book titled "Register of Death Notes" on the table. I lifted and looked at it, and the first line said: "On a certain day, the scholar Zhou died at noon, and the official Zhu died at戌 (戌 is a time unit in ancient Chinese, roughly equivalent to 7-9 PM)." I wanted to see the next entry, but two officials saw me and shouted, "How could a child see this?" They covered the book with their hands. Later, the master came out, called and asked for names, checked the register and finished looking. He said, "No, the surnames are the same but the names are different; the pursuit was mistaken." He ordered the officials to send me back. I returned to the original place, where the two fathers were still there. They happily told me, "From here, you can quickly reach home." So I followed the path and returned home, not realizing I was alive. My mother did not fully believe what I said and sent someone to secretly investigate the two people I mentioned. On that day, the scholar Zhou returned from school in the morning and caught a cold upon reaching the gate. He died at noon. Zhu was fine in the evening, but in the evening, a palace guard passed by his place. Zhu was overseeing the laborers, and because the number of people was insufficient, he was beaten to death. The timing was exact. Only then did I realize the strangeness of it. At that time, Xi Zai was serving abroad. My mother sent a letter instructing him to buy 500 pieces of dark silk to make monastic robes for my son's repentance. Xi Zai went to Wujiang and entrusted a famous physician to buy them, and told him everything in detail. [This story is told by Gu.]

Yang Shaohua of Changxing Yang Fu, from Changxing, was a young man with exceptional talent. When he was a student, he went to meet the provincial examiner outside the pass, but before the examiner arrived, he waited in the land temple. In jest, he took a divination tool and wished, saying, "When the examiner arrives, may I receive a favorable sign." He threw the divination tool, and it fell onto the beam, making it impossible to retrieve. He then wrote on the back of the deity's image with lime, saying, "Send me to the Wu Ling post station." That night, the deity appeared in a dream to the provincial examiner, saying, "The officials under your jurisdiction branded my back and made me a post station worker. Please speak up and release me." The next day, the examiner questioned the students, and Yang Fu felt ashamed and apologized, ordering him to wash it off. That night, the deity appeared again in a dream to thank him, and asked, "This young man, why do you fear him so much?" The deity replied, "This person will one day become a Deputy Minister of Justice." Yang Fu later passed the imperial examination and gradually rose to become a high-ranking official in the Yushi Tai (Supervisory Court). He served for a short time and then requested retirement to return to his hometown. Before reaching home, he first bid farewell to his relatives and friends, saying, "I will die on a certain day." On that day, he sat upright and died. Throughout his life, Yang Fu was always upright and principled, and he was highly respected by his hometown.

**Zhang Mengjie** Zhang Mengjie, a high-ranking official from Huzhou, served as the Governor-General in Yunnan. Once, he went on an inspection tour to the border regions and stayed overnight in a military camp. After midnight, he took a lamp with him and went for a walk. Suddenly, he noticed a beautiful woman standing beside him. Zhang was startled and quickly handed the lamp to her. The woman did not hesitate and took the lamp, standing by his side. After a while, he gestured for her to go ahead, and she complied. He then entered the tent, intending to read a book to distract himself, but found no books. By chance, he came across the Great Ming Code and began to read it, so intently that he did not even blink until dawn. The woman could not seduce him, so she threw the lamp away and disappeared, leaving him still unsure of what kind of spirit she was.

严尚书** 严震直，字子敏，是湖之骥村人，深得高皇朝的宠信，官至户部尚书。后来奉命出使安南，途中不幸去世，归葬于城外。某日，有一艘船经过他的墓旁，遇到一位老者登船，说：“我要去骥村。”船到严氏宅前，老者对船夫说：“我进去，让家人给你钱。”于是登岸，一脚踩入水中，弄湿了靴子。进入宅中后，许久不见出来，船夫便敲门询问：“刚才有一位老者登船，进门后现在在哪里？”家人惊讶地说：“没有啊。”船夫看到地上有脚印，循着脚印，发现老者进入了家庙，看到严公的雕像，发现一只靴子果然湿了，这才明白老者是严公的神魂归来。【以上三事由湖州的柳如嵩讲述。】 **木妖** 我居住的临顿里有一位百姓金氏，家中有一张旧的杨木肉杌，已有七八年的历史。己卯四月，这张肉杌突然长出了十多根枝条，青色，粗得超过食物。又听说桃花坞徐铁匠家的木杌也长出了枝条，与金氏家的情况相似，可能是木妖作祟。

Tang Wengsi, a clerk from Wuxian County, was from the town of Jiangwan in Jiading. At eighteen years old, he had been studying official duties. Once, after escorting a guest into the city, he returned feeling very tired and leaned against a table to fall asleep. Suddenly, as if in a dream, he saw two figures in black clothing leading a horse and said, "An official from Kunshan invites you to drink." Tang Wengsi immediately mounted the horse and galloped out through the north gate of Jiading, entering the south gate of Kunshan, winding along a ruined city wall. Suddenly, two more people appeared, holding a document and shouting, "We are sent by the Mountain God to pursue those who have missed their deadlines. You may no longer ride a horse." They seized Tang Wengsi to the ground, pulled out a rope from their sleeves, tied it around his neck, and led him to the gate of the Mountain God's temple to report. Inside, they called out to summon him, and he knelt in the courtyard. The deity was dressed in a yellow robe, adorned with golden flowers, and was surrounded by many attendants. The deity told Tang Wengsi, "I know you have talent as a clerk and have specially summoned you to manage the official documents of the four halls and eight pavilions." Pointing to sixteen bamboo containers on the steps, the deity said, "These are all the documents." The Mountain God is a local deity of Kunshan, and Tang Wengsi had always known about this. He thought to himself that once he accepted this position, he would never be able to return to life.

He refused with great effort, saying, "I have never been familiar with administrative matters, nor do I know a single character. Only Your Majesty, please have mercy and forgive me." Despite repeated urging, he still refused. The deity became angry and scolded the attendants to inflict punishment, using all five types of punishments to the utmost, causing unbearable pain. Yet, ■〈王巳〉 remained steadfast in his refusal. The deity could do nothing about it and then ordered the executioner to take him and place him under the eaves. The deity wrote a separate document and had someone take it to summon a certain person from the county to come. After about half a day, the summoned person arrived. The deity repeated the previous words to him, and this person gladly accepted the order. The deity was pleased and immediately changed his headdress and clothing, leading him with sixteen chests and withdrawing into the office. The deity called the two people forward to hand over ■〈王巳〉 to the land temple, ordering them to convey him to the Eastern Peak for revival. Upon arriving at the temple, the god of the land, wearing a headdress and ornaments, came out to receive the document and sent two of his subordinates to escort him to the temple of the Eastern Peak. Having arrived, he stopped outside the gate, entered to deliver the document but did not emerge. ■〈王巳〉 looked inside and saw the emperor of the Eastern Peak, wearing a headdress and a brown robe, sitting at the table to handle affairs. Those standing in attendance were all wearing purple robes and gauze hats, numbering in the hundreds, and there were another several hundred people bustling in the courtyard. ■〈王巳〉 thought to himself, "My feet are still sore, and I fear that entering will not withstand the trampling of many people." While hesitating, suddenly someone came out from inside the door and called, "Young master, why are you here?" Looking at him, it was an old servant of the Tang family.

■〈王巳〉 recounted the previous events, and the servant said, "The young man will come back to life, and I will now send him back." ■〈王巳〉 reported his foot pain, and the servant said, "I will find a boat to ferry you." The servant helped ■〈王巳〉 to a boat below the shore, an unusual vessel with no bow or stern, only a hull. They helped him onto it, and he curled up his feet and lay down. The servant stood on the boat, not seeing him row, but the boat moved on its own. From the temple to home was about forty miles, but they arrived in a moment. They helped him back to the shore, and looking back, they lost sight of the servant and the boat. Entering the house, he was knocked over by the door threshold, and he awoke with a start. Feeling the walls, he realized he was in a coffin. He kicked the board with his feet, and the family, startled, quickly lifted the coffin lid. ■〈王巳〉 was already alive, having been dead for forty-seven days. They wanted to help him out, but he was too exhausted to stand. They broke the coffin after a board, and finally, he was freed. They poured ginger juice into him, and his breath was just barely connected. His eyes were dim, and his hands and feet were all injured, showing no signs of life. They planned to bring in a doctor, when suddenly a Daoist priest passed by the door asking for alms. Hearing this, the priest said to his father, "I can treat him, but I must first apply heat to his chest acupoint."

"If one knows pain, then one can live," the Taoist said. The father, overjoyed, brought the child in and the Taoist performed moxibustion on him. As the fire was just lit, the child immediately cried out in pain. The Taoist said, "He's alive." However, the child still could not speak. The Taoist took out a purple pill from his bag, shaped like ink, and instructed them to grind it into powder and mix half of it with rice water to administer it. The other half was to be saved for consumption at dusk. When the medicine was finished, the child would be able to speak and walk. The family followed the Taoist's instructions and prepared everything as directed. The Taoist did not eat, only consuming a few pieces of fruit and three cups of wine. As dusk approached, he announced that he was leaving. Soon after he departed, the family discovered that the hidden medicine had disappeared. They blamed each other, believing that there was no hope for the child. The next morning, the Taoist returned, and the family told him what had happened. He laughed and said, "It's not worth worrying about." He then produced another half pill, which was exactly the one that had been lost. The family, in their ignorance, did not find this strange, and they gave him ten taels of gold. The Taoist laughed and said, "I am a hermit, what use do I have for gold?" He then gave them twenty bolts of cloth, saying, "Keep it for now, I will come back for it tomorrow." He left, and never returned. ■〈王巳〉 was saved from death, and later detailedly described the events in the afterlife.

○ Zhang the Grand Censor Grand Censor Zhang Gong [Tai], a native of Sucheng, was extremely ugly in his youth. He once fell gravely ill and dreamed that his father was arrested for a crime and was sentenced to death. In the dream, he petitioned the officials, offering himself in place of his father. The officials agreed, and he was shackled and led to the marketplace to face execution. The sword swung, and his head fell off instantly. His soul then entered the underworld, where he met the Judge of Hell. The Judge said, "This man is innocent and should be reborn. He shall be born as a boy in a commoner's family in Shandong." Officials were sent to escort him, with a large rooster leading the way. He followed and eventually arrived at the family's home, where the woman was just giving birth. He was reborn into the world. After bathing, he was placed on a kang. Family members gathered around to look at him, and someone pointed at him and said, "What a good little boy." Soon, his body grew bigger. Another person said, "How could it be like this?" He suddenly woke up and lay in a deep sleep on the bed, remaining unconscious for a whole day. In the morning, when he woke up, his family members were all surprised and did not recognize him. He had a full face and a majestic appearance, greatly different from before. He told everyone about his dream, and they were even more amazed and frightened. After a while, they gradually noticed that his voice and behavior were not much different from before, and then believed him. Later, he passed the imperial examination to become a Jinshi and served in various positions up to his current rank. [As told by the scholar Gu Hui.]

#### Fever-Repelling Mirror The Chen family of San Du in Wuxian passed down an ancient mirror, measuring about eight or nine inches in diameter. Anyone suffering from malaria who holds and gazes into the mirror will see an object attached to their back. The object has a wild and unkempt appearance, with a dark and indistinct face. When the mirror is raised, the object seems to be startled and suddenly vanishes, and the illness is immediately cured. It is said that the malaria ghost fears seeing its own form and flees. The mirror is considered a treasure. During the Hongzhi period, when the brothers divided their inheritance, they split the mirror, each getting half. When they used the halves to cure malaria, they no longer saw the ghost. [Reported by the physician Zhou Weizhong.] #### Dragon on the Arm According to an old saying, there was a monk at the Jinshan Temple on the Yangtze River who was carefree and unrestrained. One day, while he was sleeping during the day, a fellow monk drew a dragon on his arm as a joke. The monk woke up and saw it. He said playfully, "I slept and a dragon appeared on my arm. Is this not a gift from heaven? I should brand it to make it unique." He then pricked the area with a needle and added ink. After several months, the ink color gradually turned purple. After another few months, the pattern slightly raised, about the height of a grain of millet. Every time there was a storm, the dragon seemed to wriggle as if moving, causing the arm to shake and feel uneasy. The monk was troubled by this. One day, while bathing in the river, the water parted for several zhang, and the arm flapped up and down as if it were not his own.

The traveler grew even more revered, often disappearing into the water where he could see turtles, alligators, fish, and crabs clearly before his eyes. One day, he thought to himself, "Mount Jin lies in the middle of the river, and beneath it should have a foundation; why not explore it?" He then dived down and reached the bottom of the river, where he saw the mountain's base was only as wide as several embraces, as if supported by a single pillar. He swung his arm and shook it, causing the mountain to shake violently and the buildings to move. The monk was frightened, thinking it was an earthquake, and burned incense to pray to the Three Jewels. After a meal's time, the situation settled. The traveler climbed the mountain, knowing the truth and secretly laughing at it. After ten days, he told his fellow monks the truth, explaining the divinity of the dragon's arm. The fellow monks were shocked and reported to the elder monk, who said, "This is a demon." The elder monk secretly went to Zhenjiang to report to the authorities, asking them to kill it. The officials dismissed it as a false accusation and did not handle it. The monk feared the traveler would be a burden on him, so he intoxicated and strangled him. After the traveler's death, the dragon suddenly disappeared, and there was no more magic.

In Hai Ning, a commoner named Wang Tu and his son went out for a walk when they encountered a fisherman carrying a large turtle with a diameter of over a foot. They bought the turtle and tied it to a pillar, intending to roast it. A merchant from Jiangxi, who lived nearby, saw the turtle and told the shopkeeper, asking to buy it for a thousand coins. The shopkeeper was surprised by the high price, and the merchant explained, "This is a nine-tailed turtle, a divine creature. I wish to buy it and release it. If you help me, you will receive half the merit; this is your share." They went together to verify the claim. The merchant stepped onto the turtle's back, and on either side of its tail, four smaller tails were revealed. He then gave the money to Wang Tu, but Wang Tu refused, so they roasted the turtle instead. Father and son ate it together. That night, a sudden flood came from the sea, rising three feet above the ground. All the beds and tables floated, and after more than ten hours, the water receded. By midday the next day, the shopkeeper was surprised that Wang Tu and his son had not risen, so he broke down the door to check on them. He only found their clothes on the bed, but father and son were nowhere to be seen. Some people said that because they had harmed the divine turtle, it was taken away by the water god and killed. [Chou Ning, a guest from Wu, personally witnessed the event.]

The Water Deity of Poyang Lake Qi Lan, from Yuyao, whose courtesy name was Wentai, passed the imperial examination in the second year of the Jingtai era and was appointed as an editor in the Hanlin Academy. After completing the mourning period for his parent and returning to the capital, he crossed the Qiantang River when a great storm arose. Hundreds of pairs of red silk lanterns illuminated the river water brightly, and nine men, wearing turbans, trousers, and boots, carrying swords, and riding white horses, flew across the water as if it were flat ground. The boatmen were greatly frightened, but Qi Gong said, "Do not be afraid, I know what is happening." He pushed open the window to look and saw that the nine men had all dismounted and were kneeling. He asked, "Are you not the nine brothers of General Sang Shi?" They replied, "Yes." He said, "Go, I understand now." They all dispersed. Qi Gong ordered the boatmen to turn back, saying, "If there is something, I will return." He then returned home. Upon arriving, he told his family, "On a certain day, I will pass away." On that day, he bathed, dressed formally, and sat down. The nine men led by armored soldiers came to greet him, walking and stepping on the roof tiles, which all broke, with their weapons and banners shining and crowded together.

In a short while, 【有顷 纪录汇编本作「有项」，据说库本改。】 after the public figure passed away, chariots and riders galloped about, as if there were someone guarding in front and behind, disappearing into the void. Later, the wife of Mr. Qi, a distinguished official from Qiongshan, entered the capital by boat, passing over Poyang Lake. At night, she dreamed of a noble person wearing red clothes who appeared and said, "I am Qi Lan, an old friend of Zhong Shen, and I am now a water deity. Yesterday, I received a heavenly command to protect hundreds of boats. Lady, be cautious and do not cross." Upon waking, the boatman was just about to untie the mooring and set off, but the lady quickly stopped him. In an instant, a strong wind arose, and all those on the boat drowned. The next day, the lady crossed the lake. Upon arriving in the capital, she informed Mr. Qi, who was deeply moved by her experience and sealed a memorial to honor him. A fellow villager named Xiang served Mr. Qi in crossing the river and personally witnessed him conversing with nine deities. 【又尝得见丘公祭文 纪录汇编本无「见」字，据说库本补。】

○ The Spirit of the Three Attendants The Three Attendants, known as the *Zong Cabled*, was a spirit that arose from a rope used in boats. During the First Emperor’s naval campaign, he defeated Chen Youliang at Poyang Lake, where tens of thousands perished. Upon his return, he cast the *Zong Cabled* into the lake, and the souls of the wronged attached themselves to it, and thus it became a source of supernatural mischief. Boatmen must offer sacrifices to it, or else they risk capsizing and drowning. ○ Ma Shaoshi Ma Shaoshi Wen Sheng of Junzhou was a jinshi (imperial examinee) from the Xinwei year of the Jingtaiguan era. By the end of the Hongzhi era, he had risen to the position of *Zongzai*, the highest official of the civil service. He served in office for over fifty years, and although he had reached old age, his energy did not wane. Later, he retired from office and died at home in the Renshen year of the Zhengde era. On that day, as the sun approached the *bu* hour, a man from the village who had business to attend to was returning from outside the city. On the road, he encountered Ma Shaoshi riding in a sedan chair, accompanied by many attendants. From within the chair, Ma Shaoshi raised his hands in greeting and asked where he was going. The man replied, "To the village." When the man returned to the official’s gate, he heard the sound of weeping and realized that Ma had already passed away. The moment they met was exactly the moment he had died. 【According to Mr. Han, the county school supervisor from the official’s hometown.】

○The Tale of Zhang the Magistrate's Divine Governance At first, I read the histories of the Former Han dynasty and others, encountering the stories of various upright officials. Some of these officials were so effective in their governance that they seemed to communicate with the divine, and their spiritual power could move heaven and earth. I never fail to sigh and admire deeply, thinking it was something not seen in later generations. But now, in Zhang Gong of Cixi, I see it again. His name is Bing, and his courtesy name is Zhongming. He is the son of Duyushi Kai. During the Chenghua period, he first served as the magistrate of Qianshan County through the imperial examination. Initially, there was a woodcutter in the county who loved to eat eels. After selling firewood, he would use half the money to buy eels, and he ordered his wife to cook and eat them. One day, after returning from the market, he ate them greedily when he was hungry. Shortly after, he died of abdominal pain. The neighbors suspected that his wife had poisoned him, and they arrested her and took her to the authorities. No other evidence was found, and the case could not be resolved, so she was imprisoned for over a year. When Zhang Gong first took office, he once climbed the hall during the day. Suddenly, he saw someone dressed in green robes and a black hat slowly entering from outside the door. Those around him did not notice at all. When this person came to the front of the desk, he bowed deeply, and Zhang Gong greeted him and invited him to sit. The visitor said, "Do not be afraid, I am not a human, but the local god of a certain village in this county. There is an unjust case that has not been cleared. I know you are wise and decisive, and you communicate with the divine. You will surely clear it up." Zhang Gong asked about the matter, and the god said, "The wife of a man named Jiǎ in my village is in prison for killing her husband, but he actually died from eating eels, not because of her." Zhang Gong wanted to verify this, so he placed the eels in a water jar. Any eel that raised its head above the water by two or three inches should be killed. If cooking and feeding them to other prisoners caused them to die, then the case would be cleared. After speaking, the god disappeared. Zhang Gong found this strange. The next day, he summoned all the fishermen in the county to catch eels, and they caught several hundred pounds.

As the god commanded, they tested it, and those who raised their heads were seven in total. They set a pot in the hall and summoned this woman to be cooked in front of everyone. They brought out a condemned prisoner in the courtyard and gave them food; as soon as they swallowed, they immediately claimed to have abdominal pain and soon fell to the ground and died. The magistrate said to the woman, "Your innocence is now clear." He then released her. Slowly investigating her hometown, they indeed found a shrine, and upon looking at the image, it was exactly what was seen. Also, A married her daughter to B; when they arrived at B's door and lifted the curtain to see, it was just an empty carriage. B accused A of deceiving him and sued in the county, and A also accused B of killing her daughter, leading to a prolonged and contentious dispute. The previous magistrate arrested the matchmaker and others and interrogated them; all said that the daughter indeed boarded the carriage, but they did not know how she was lost. The magistrate could not resolve the case.

When the official arrived, he happened to go out into the suburbs to inspect the land and equalize the tax. [At the beginning, when the First Emperor pacified Chen Shi, he passed through Yanshan and set the tax rate. There was an excess of tax that was added to several hundred acres of land, with some acres taxed at several shi. The official went to inspect this, and upon seeing it, he reported to the emperor to have the tax rates adjusted.] As he approached the county boundary, he saw a tree with a trunk several dozen chi in circumference, casting shade over more than twenty acres. The area beneath the tree was unsuitable for growing wheat or millet. The official wanted to cut it down to expand the farmland. All his attendants advised against it, saying that this tree was the abode of a deity. If the people lost their reverence, they would die of illness. The magistrate cannot treat it lightly. The official did not listen and sent a document to the neighboring county, requesting their assistance in cutting down the tree. The magistrate of the neighboring county feared disaster and refused to comply. The elders, officials, and soldiers again strongly advised against it, but the official's determination grew stronger. On the appointed day, he led dozens of men, dressed in military attire and accompanied by music, to the site. As they approached within a few hundred steps, three men dressed in formal attire appeared on the side of the road and bowed to greet him, saying, "We are the tree spirits, and we have resided here for many years. We humbly request your mercy and ask you to spare us." The official rebuked them, and they suddenly disappeared.

The official commanded the man to chop the tree, and as he did so, the tree bled as if it were wounded. The crowd was afraid and wanted to stop, but the official personally took up the axe and began chopping to set an example. It took three hundred chops before the tree finally fell. At the top of the tree was a large nest, and inside the nest were three women who fell to the ground, unconscious and nearly dead. The official ordered his attendants to support them and pour hot water over them. After a long time, they began to revive and asked how they had come to be there. The women explained, "Last year, we were blown here by a strong wind. We were in a high building, enjoying a feast with three young men. Everything we ate was delicious food. We often looked down at the city below, which was clearly visible, but there was no way to go down. The young men came and went, flying through the air, and we did not know we were living in a tree nest." The official thoroughly searched for their families and returned them. One of them was the girl that person A had lost, who said she was taken away by a demon while in a carriage. The lawsuit was thus resolved. The official used the wood to repair several public buildings, and the land under the shade of the tree became good farmland again.

Zhang Gong thus completely destroyed all unauthorized temples within his domain, leaving none behind. Only one temple in the rural areas remained, which the villagers secretly preserved. One day, as Zhang Gong passed through the area on business, he dreamed of the god earnestly saying, "Please forgive me, I was destroyed by Zhang Gong." The next day, he summoned the villagers and ordered them to destroy the temple. The god suddenly descended upon the common people in the neighboring town, saying, "I was destroyed by Zhang Gong, and now I have nowhere to stay. You are an upright person, and I dare not offend you. I wish to borrow a small piece of land to stay temporarily. When you leave, the temple can be restored. If you do not follow me, disaster will surely come within five days." The villagers initially did not believe him, but within three days, the man who had been summoned became anxious and distressed, vomiting. The god then descended, and the family prostrated themselves, and they built a temple for him. There was a Taoist priest skilled in the art of invisibility, who often seduced women. Zhang Gong captured him, severely whipped him, and he suffered no pain. Afterward, his form disappeared.

The official pretended to go out on some pretext, then rushed to the suspect's residence, bound them, and brought them back. He stamped a seal on their back, then beat them. The suspect screamed in agony until they died under the whip. A widow from the town had only one son, who was gathering firewood in the mountains when he was eaten by a tiger. A mischievous neighbor wanted to trap the official, so he wrote a petition on behalf of the widow and submitted it to the county office, claiming that he wanted to see the tiger punished. The official looked at the petition and laughed, saying, "Are these wicked people trying to trap me?" He agreed to meet with the widow in five days. He then fasted and wrote an offering to the city deity, saying, "You are the master of this town, yet you cannot prevent disasters and protect the people from harm, allowing the tiger to eat them. Today, I make a pact with you: within five days, I will drive the tiger to confess and take responsibility. Otherwise, I will demolish your temple and replace it with another." Five days later, before dawn, he dreamed that the deity told him, "The tiger is coming."

The official started up in alarm, wearing his bow and arrows, he ascended to the hall, ordered the door to be opened, and there entered two tigers that lay hidden in the courtyard, as if guarded by someone. The official sternly rebuked, saying: "My good citizens' children, and you have eaten them, by law, you should be executed. If you did not harm humans, retreat." One tiger rose, circled the other tiger once, lowered its tail and left, while the other remained motionless. The official was skilled at archery, drew his arrows, fired three times and hit the head each time, then ordered his subordinates to kill them with whips. He called the woman, and she returned with the tiger corpses. From then on, the story spread widely, and he was regarded as a divine person. After three years, for his excellent governance, he was promoted to the position of Inspector of the Censorate. Now retired and living at home, he closed his door and declined official business, never entered the government office, becoming a respected figure in the local area, gentlemen lamented that his position did not match his virtue. I heard from the people of his hometown and wrote this record, future accounts of upright officials may find it useful. Right here is the Record of the Divine Governance of Inspector Zhang, written by my disciple Yuan, recorded to conclude my compilation. Ten Volumes of the *Gengsi Collection* (Ming) by Lu Can; annotated by Tan Dihua and Chen Jiahe. Collection of Historical Notes from the Yuan and Ming Dynasties. Beijing: Zhonghua Book Company, 1987 [Republic of China Year 76], 1997 Hubei Second Edition.

**Appendix:** *Zuo Zhuan Annotations and Notes in Five Volumes* (Submitted by the Governor of Zhejiang) By Lu Can of the Ming dynasty, who was also known as Ziyu and hailed from Changzhou. He passed the imperial examinations in 1546 (Jiajing bingxu) and held the position of censor in the Ministry of Works. Later, he was exiled to a postal station for impeaching Zhang Cong and Gui E. He served as the magistrate of Yongxin County until his death. His biography is recorded in the *Ming Shi* (official history of the Ming dynasty). This compilation consists of five volumes: the first three volumes correct Du Yu's annotations, the second volume corrects Kong Yingda's exegesis, and the fifth volume corrects Lu Deming's phonetic interpretations of the *Zuo Zhuan*. It draws on the theories of various scholars and occasionally includes the author's own opinions, providing significant benefit to scholars of classical Chinese. Gu Yanwu, in his *Rizhilu*, wrote in the section on correcting the *Zuo Zhuan* annotations: "Any points already addressed by Mr. Shao, Mr. Fu, and Mr. Lu are not included here. 'Shao' refers to Shao Bao, 'Left Zhuan Zhong' to Fu Xun, and 'Lu' is Can himself. This shows that Yanwu highly values this work." Can also wrote *Chunqiu Zuo Zhuan Jue* in two volumes, arguing that the *Zuo Zhuan* was written by a Warring States period author, and that Liu Xin further tampered with it, resulting in a work that is often shallow and does not conform to the proper path, sometimes containing strange and peculiar statements that run after the fringe. Can attributed the *Zuo Zhuan* to the Warring States period, likely because Cheng Hao and Cheng Yi stated that "Lao" (腊) was a Qin ritual and "shuzhang" (庶长) was a Qin official, which he considered rigid and unyielding. He attributed the tampering to Liu Xin, likely because Lin Li argued that any mention of "junzi" (君子) in the *Zuo Zhuan* was a word of Liu Xin, with no evidence to support this claim. This led Can to indulge in lofty speculation, still following the speculative habits of Ming scholars, which is like adding a foot to a snake. Therefore, only this compilation is included, while *Chunqiu Zuo Zhuan Jue* is mentioned separately. *Chunqiu Hu Shizhuan Bianyi* in Two Volumes (Submitted by the Governor of Jiangsu)

Ming dynasty, by Lu Can, compiled. With a preface written by the author, he says that Hu's interpretation of the classics sometimes errs in being overly detailed and verbose, making the meaning of the sages more obscure. Therefore, he writes this to debate and clarify it. The main point is to believe in the text of the classics rather than in established rules. He says, "Not viewing the *Spring and Autumn Annals* with a straightforward and honest perspective but instead creating twisted meanings, where will it not lead?"
